identity.research_question: : Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in *Cancer Discovery* 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1
identity.project_summary: : Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in Cancer Discovery 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492). Integrates 30-antibody t-CycIF proteomics, Whole Slide Image (WSI) validations from the Oncosys-OVA (HRPritti) cohort, and GeoMx Spatial Transcriptomics. Custom preprocessing was developed using OpenCV denoising, Ilastik (DAPI+LaminB) pixel classification, CellProfiler segmentation, and Tribus cell-type calling. MultiNicheNetR ligand-receptor profiling was performed to map tumor-immune cell interactions (CD4+, CD8+, regulatory T cells, DCs vs tumor cells) in MHCII-expressing niches, validated via GeoMx. Includes immunopeptidomic profiling (with Cerullo's laboratory) of MHCII peptides from 12 HGSC study samples (Anastasia). Description: Analysis of a large cohort of 265 HGSC patients using 10 TMAs (four cores per tumor) to profile HLA-class II expression on cancer cells and its association with survival and clinical outcomes. This study was provisionally accepted/published in Cancer Discovery 2026 (doi:10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492). Integrates 30-antibody t-CycIF proteomics, Whole Slide Image (WSI) validations from the Oncosys-OVA (HRPritti) cohort, and GeoMx Spatial Transcriptomics. Custom preprocessing was developed using OpenCV denoising, Ilastik (DAPI+LaminB) pixel classification, CellProfiler segmentation, and Tribus cell-type calling. MultiNicheNetR ligand-receptor profiling was performed to map tumor-immune cell interactions (CD4+, CD8+, regulatory T cells, DCs vs tumor cells) in MHCII-expressing niches, validated via GeoMx. Includes immunopeptidomic profiling (with Cerullo's laboratory) of MHCII peptides from 12 HGSC study samples (Anastasia).
identity.responsible: Fernando Perez (Julia Casado, Anastasia Kachalova, Aleksandra Shabanova)

document_index.excerpt: Dear Dr. Michele Hartsough, We are pleased to submit our manuscript, titled “Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor ecosystems and clinical outcomes,” for consideration in Cancer Discovery. This work presents a clinically and molecularly resolved single-cell spatial atlas of 280 metastatic ovarian cancers, integrating spatial proteomics, genomics, bulk, single-cell, and spatial transcriptomics to map the tumor-immune microenvironment (TME) at unprecedented scale and resolution. As discussed in the email (25th June 2025), we have now included supporting functional data from our patient-derived organoid platform, and we would be grateful for your feedback on the manuscript. The authors present new data supporting notion that MHCII is expressed by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. document_index.name: Untitled presentation.pptx
document_index.title: Objectives of the study & their impact
document_index.excerpt: Objectives of the study & their impact The project aims to discover how the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and MHC dysregulation facilitate immune escape in ovarian cancer: Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and determine their clinical significance. Using t-CycIF, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples and characterized cellular communities and their interactions. Through machine-learning–guided feature extraction, we identified distinct MHC dysregulation patterns associated with specific HGSC molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes. This work demonstrates that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is a key determinant of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness. The findings, validated across four independent cohorts, were submitted to (se

# Logs
import logging
# Configure logging
logging.basicConfig(filename='exp.out', level=logging.DEBUG)
logger = logging.getLogger(__name__)

# Handling dataframe
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd

# Pre-process data
import scipy.stats as stats
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler, StandardScaler

# Preprocess features
from sklearn.preprocessing import LabelEncoder

# Load models
from sklearn.ensemble import RandomForestClassifier

# Model tuning 
from skopt.space import Integer
from skopt.utils import use_named_args
from sklearn.model_selection import RepeatedStratifiedKFold, cross_val_score
from skopt import gp_minimize
from time import time

# Model testing
from sklearn.metrics import f1_score, balanced_accuracy_score, auc

# Feature selection 
from sklearn.feature_selection import RFECV
from sklearn.inspection import permutation_importance

import os

# multiprocessing
import multiprocessing as mp
from loky import get_reusable_executor
from joblib import parallel_backend

class Model:
 def __init__(self, exp_name = None, channels = None, channels_to_transform = None, channels_to_outliers = None, channels_to_scale = None, cells = None, target = None, therapies = None, mol_profiles = None, scaler = None, df = None, random_seed = None):

 self.rng = random_seed

 # Variables for data pre-processing
 self.df = df

 # from experiments.csv:
 self.exp_name = exp_name # Experiment column
 
 # Set the output directory and filename for the figures
 self.output_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))

 # Collect required variables from csv file
 self.features = channels.split(",") # channels column
 self.types_of_cells = cells # types_of_cells column
 self.target = target # classes_types column

ut_dir = os.path.dirname(os.path.abspath(__file__))

 # Collect required variables from csv file
 self.features = channels.split(",") # channels column
 self.types_of_cells = cells # types_of_cells column
 self.target = target # classes_types column
 self.mol_profiles = mol_profiles.split(",") # mol_profiles column
 self.therapies = therapies # therapies column 

 self.features_to_transform = channels_to_transform.split(",")
 self.features_for_outliers = channels_to_outliers.split(",")
 self.features_to_scale = channels_to_scale.split(",")

 # Define transformation type. Available types: LOG, LOG2, BOXCOX
 self.transformation = 'LOG2' 
 
 # Define how to remove outliers. Available options: trim_by_slide & remove
 self.operation = 'remove'
 
 # Scale data
 self.scaler_dict = {'MinMax': MinMaxScaler(),'Standard': StandardScaler()}
 # MinMaxScaler scales the data to be within a specific range, usually between 0 and 1
 # StandardScaler scales the data to have a mean of zero and a standard deviation of one
 self.scaler_type = scaler # Scaling type
 self.scale_by = 'slide' # Define how to scale. Available options: whole, patient, slide

 # Variables for machine learning step
 self.categorical_variables = ['Molecular.profile2', 'therapy_sequence','figo_stage']
 self.best_params = None
 self.RF = RandomForestClassifier(random_state = self.rng, class_weight = 'balanced')

 # Define the evaluation procedure
 self.cv = RepeatedStratifiedKFold(n_splits=5, n_repeats=5, random_state=self.rng)
 
 # Save results 
 self.train_results = None
 self.train_time = None
 self.predictions_train = None
 
 self.test_results = {}
 self.predictions_test = None

 self.feature_selection_results = {}

 def choose_features(self):
 self.df = self.df.loc[:,self.features]

sform(self.df[variable].astype('str'))
 
 # Save the encoding results for the current variable in 'label_encoder_dict'
 # The dictionary will store the unique class labels and their corresponding encoded values
 self.label_encoder_dict[variable] = {
 'label_codes': label_encoder.classes_.tolist(), 
 'label_values': label_encoder.transform(label_encoder.classes_).tolist()
 }
 
 logger.info(f"\nPrepared successfully categorical inputs in {self.exp_name}.")

 def prep_train_test_data(self):
 
 # To receive same number of patients independent of runs
 np.random.seed(self.rng)
 
 # Group df by Molecular profile and therapy
 grouped = self.df.groupby(['Molecular.profile2', 'therapy_sequence'])

 # Assign 80% of patients from each unique group of Molecular profile and therapy to training and rest to test sets
 self.X_train_full = grouped.apply(lambda x: x.loc[x['patient'].isin(np.random.choice(x['patient'].unique(), size=int(0.8*len(x['patient'].unique())), replace=False))])
 self.X_test_full = grouped.apply(lambda x: x.loc[x['patient'].isin(np.setdiff1d(x['patient'].unique(), self.X_train_full['patient']))])
 
 # Assign target columns to y_train and y_test
 self.y_train=self.X_train_full[self.target]
 self.y_test=self.X_test_full[self.target]
 
 # Drop target columns
 self.X_train = self.X_train_full.drop(columns = [self.target, 'patient', 'cycif.slide','Molecular.profile2'])
 self.X_test = self.X_test_full.drop(columns = [self.target, 'patient', 'cycif.slide','Molecular.profile2'])
 
 logger.info(f"\nPrepared X_train of size {self.X_train.shape}, y_train of size {len(self.y_train)} and X_test of size {self.X_test.shape}, y_test of size {len(self.y_test)} in experiment {self.exp_name}.")

 def find_RF_by_Bayesian(self, X_train, y_train):
 '''
 inputs:

red X_train of size {self.X_train.shape}, y_train of size {len(self.y_train)} and X_test of size {self.X_test.shape}, y_test of size {len(self.y_test)} in experiment {self.exp_name}.")

 def find_RF_by_Bayesian(self, X_train, y_train):
 '''
 inputs:
 - X_train: features training set
 - y_train: classes' labels of training set
 '''
 try:
 # Define hyperparameters search space 
 space = [Integer(200, 900, name='min_samples_split'),
 Integer(200, 900, name='min_samples_leaf'),
 Integer(50, 200, name='n_estimators')]
 
 # Store standard deviations of cross-validated scores
 std_devs = []
 
 # Define evaluation for each call within hyperparameters' space
 @use_named_args(space)
 def evaluate_model(**params):
 with parallel_backend('threading', n_jobs=-1):
 scores = cross_val_score(self.RF, X_train, y_train, scoring="f1_weighted", cv=self.cv, n_jobs=-1)
 std_devs.append(np.std(scores))
 return -np.mean(scores)
 
 # Perform Bayesian search on defined hyperparameters search space 
 start = time() # Get start time
 self.train_results = gp_minimize(evaluate_model, space, random_state=self.rng, n_random_starts=5, n_calls = 30)
 end = time() # Get end time
 
 # Calculate the training time
 self.train_time = end - start

 logger.info(f"\nAverage standard deviation of cross-validated scores in {self.exp_name}: {np.mean(std_devs)}")
 logger.info(f"\nTraining_time in {self.exp_name}: {self.train_time}")
 
 # Store the best hyperparameters
 self.best_params = {'min_samples_split': self.train_results.x[0],
 'min_samples_leaf': self.train_results.x[1],
 'n_estimators': self.train_results.x[2]}
 
 # Create RF model with stored best hyperparameters
 self.RF = RandomForestClassifier(**self.best_params, random_state = self.rng, class_weight = 'balanced')

'min_samples_leaf': self.train_results.x[1],
 'n_estimators': self.train_results.x[2]}
 
 # Create RF model with stored best hyperparameters
 self.RF = RandomForestClassifier(**self.best_params, random_state = self.rng, class_weight = 'balanced') 

 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully created RF model in {self.exp_name} with hyperparameters: {self.train_results.x} ( score - {-self.train_results.fun:.5f})")
 
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred in find_RF_by_Bayesian during {self.exp_name}: {e}", exc_info=True)
 
 def test(self, X_train, y_train, X_test_full, X_test, y_test):
 '''
 inputs:
 - X_train: features training set
 - y_train: classes' labels of training set
 - X_test_full: features testing set with saved patient and slide IDs
 - X_test: features testing set
 - y_test: classes' labels of testing set
 '''
 
 # Training step: Fit the learner to the training data
 self.RF.fit(X_train, y_train)
 
 # Get the predictions on the training set(X_train),
 # then get predictions on the test samples(X_test)
 self.predictions_train = self.RF.predict(X_train)
 # Test step:
 self.predictions_test = self.RF.predict(X_test)
 
 # Save the predictions of X_test as df:
 # 1. Convert predictions_test to a pandas series
 predictions_series = pd.Series(self.predictions_test, name='predicted_label')
 # 2. Convert MultiIndex to a flat index in X_test
 X_test_flat = X_test_full.reset_index(drop=True)
 # 3. Concatenate X_test_flat and predictions_series along axis=1 to receive df of test set predictions 
 self.predictions_df = pd.concat([X_test_flat, predictions_series], axis=1)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on training samples
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_train = balanced_accuracy_score(y_train, self.predictions_train)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on the test set

f = pd.concat([X_test_flat, predictions_series], axis=1)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on training samples
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_train = balanced_accuracy_score(y_train, self.predictions_train)
 
 # Compute balanced accuracy on the test set
 self.balanced_accuracy_score_test = balanced_accuracy_score(y_test, self.predictions_test)
 
 # Compute weighted F1-score on training samples
 self.f1_score_train = f1_score(y_train, self.predictions_train, average='weighted')
 
 # Compute weighted F1-score on the test set
 self.f1_score_test = f1_score(y_test, self.predictions_test, average='weighted')
 
 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully tested RF model in {self.exp_name}")
 
 def calculate_mean_per_patient(self):
 name = self.exp_name + '_patients_predictions.csv'
 patients_predictions = self.predictions_df.groupby('patient')['predicted_label'].mean().reset_index()
 patients_predictions.to_csv(name, index=False)
 
 logger.info(f"\nSuccessfully calculated patients' predictions of RF model in {self.exp_name}")

 '''

 try:
 # Define cross-validated recursive feature elimination 
 # RFECV automatically selects the best number of features based on the provided scoring function of accuracy
 rfecv = RFECV(
 estimator=self.RF,
 min_features_to_select=5,
 step=5,
 n_jobs=-1,
 scoring="accuracy",
 cv=self.cv,
 )

 # Run cross-validated recursive feature elimination to select the most predictive features
 rfecv.fit(X_train.values, y_train.values)
 
 # Get the support mask indicating which features were selected by RFECV, where True values in the mask correspond to important features.

cursive feature elimination to select the most predictive features

 # Get the support mask indicating which features were selected by RFECV, where True values in the mask correspond to important features.
 support = rfecv.support_

 # Get the list of most predictive and eliminated features
 features = X_train.columns
 self.eliminated_features = list(features[~support])
 self.important_features = features[support]

 logger.info(f"\nImportant features in {self.exp_name}: {self.important_features}")
 logger.info(f"\nEliminated features in {self.exp_name}: {self.eliminated_features}")

 # Calculate feature importances using mean decrease in impurity (Gini impurity)
 importances = rfecv.estimator_.feature_importances_

 self.X_train_transformed = X_train[self.important_features]

 # Calculate permutation importances
 permutation_scores = permutation_importance(rfecv.estimator_, self.X_train_transformed.values, y_train.values, n_repeats=10, random_state=self.rng, n_jobs=-1)

 feature_permutations = dict(zip(self.important_features, permutation_scores.importances_mean))
 self.permutation_scores_dict = dict(sorted(feature_permutations.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))

 # For gini impurity:
 # Create dictionary with important feature names as keys and importances as values
 feature_importances = dict(zip(self.important_features, importances))
 self.importances = dict(sorted(feature_importances.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True))
 
 except Exception as e:
 logger.error(f"\nAn error occurred during {self.exp_name} in eliminate_features: {e}", exc_info=True)

def perform_ml_run(exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, cells, target, classes, therapies, scaler, best_params, acc_train, acc_test, f_train, f_test, pred_features, eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df):

 model = Model(exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, cells, target, classes, therapies, scaler, df)

 model.choose_features()
 model.transform_df()
 model.remove_outliers()
 model.scaling()
 model.choose_types_of_cells()
 model.choose_mol_profiles()
 model.choose_therapy_sequences()

 model.prepare_categorical_inputs()
 model.prep_train_test_data()
 model.find_RF_by_Bayesian(model.X_train.values, model.y_train.values)
 model.test(model.X_train.values, model.y_train.values, model.X_test_full, model.X_test.values, model.y_test.values)
 model.calculate_mean_per_patient()
 model.eliminate_features(model.X_train, model.y_train)
 model.save_values()

 # Collect the results in a dictionary
 results = {

 exp_name, channels, channels_to_transform, channels_to_outliers, channels_to_scale, types_of_cells, classes_column, \
 therapies, mol_profiles, scaling_type, best_parameters, balanced_acc_train, balanced_acc_test, f1_train, f1_test, \
 most_predictive_features, eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df = data

 balanced_acc_train, balanced_acc_test, f1_train, f1_test, most_predictive_features,
 eliminated_features, permutation_scores, random_seed, name, exps, df)

08/06/2020
Questions resolved with Julia:
- Images are too big and we don't have enough disk space. Should we ignore some channels for the pixel probmap?
Yes
- CD15 (channel 30) is a bit messy. What should we do in that case?
 Remove it if it’s something in all cores. Take note if it’s only in one.
- How to define background? Check all channels if there is no signal?
Use background channels. BG488, BG555, BG647.
- Sometimes the nuclei move, should we use only one nuclei channel for nuclei pixel probmap?
Yes. But we should detect which one could be the best nuclei to use
- Nuclei pixel probability in another task?
Yes. We will create one Ilastik project for no-membrane and Membrane. And another for nuclei.

rentangles
 in OMERO to identify where artifacts are like bubbles, hairs and overexposure. Then we can download those

Ligandomics
To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we assessed the activity of MHCII signature epithelial/cancer cells by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1 in single cell RNA sequencing cohort (done by Matias). The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: 
HLA-DMA
, 
HLA-DMB
, 
HLA-DOA
, 
HLA-DOB
, 
HLA-DPA1
, 
HLA-DPB1
, 
HLA-DQA1
, 
HLA-DQA2
, 
HLA-DQB1
, 
HLA-DRA
, 
HLA-DRB1
, 
HLA-DRB5
, 
CD74.
 Based on the MHCII Ucell scores, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). MultiNicheNetR (
https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git
) analysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples (done by Aleksandra and Gayani). Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–

cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing. The corresponding scripts can be found in github: 
https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts/tree/main/10_LR_analysis
Immuno peptidomics
01/2025
 Cerrulo’s laboratory (Jacopo and Salvatore) performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study. The selected sample info is located on G-drive: 
https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1_Umqw5b8cDr_h-ZkfTCM0R400uD7lt2rU8uoS4yViMs/edit?gid=0#gid=0
. Anastasia prepared around 1g of material for each sample.
MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
(
Chiaro J, Antignani G, Feola S, Feodoroff M, Martins B, Cojoc H, et al. Development of Mesothelioma-Specific Oncolytic Immunotherapy Enabled by Immunopeptidomics of Murine and Human Mesothelioma Tumors. Nat Commun. Nature Publishing Group; 2023;14:7056
)
 with minor modifications.

rder: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient 
was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms.

n. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification.

o missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher (https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant (https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant
) with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. The final files of peptide presence and binders was shared in one drive:

For the analysis, peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CycIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575) (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms were recorded. These genes were also filtered based on their presence in the ligandome of healthy ovary tissues

Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data
11/2025 (by Ziqi) 
RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale factors, and cell-type–specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. 
11/2025 (by Aleksandra)
 After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and immune–stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample.

re computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.

iPDCs
We analyzed 10 samples from Inga-Maria et al, cancer cell paper (2024). The samples were cultivated (Patient derived cultivated cells - PDCs) by Maria Hincapie and analyzed previously by Maria Hincapie and Iga Niemic. The samples were treated with pembrolizumab and anti-TIGIT therapy and cultivated for three days. There are also control samples not treated on day 0 and day 3 of cultivation. The samples were then used for flow cytometry with the next panel: GrzB, Epcam, IFNg, CD45, DCM, CD8, CD11c, CD3, CD56, CD14
CD4, Ki67, HLA-Dr. 
Each flow data for each sample was gated by Maria to differentiate cancer cells, immune cells and myeloid cells.
The data was backed up in P-Drive: P:\h345\afarkkilab\Projects\NKI\iPDCs
Also it was backed up in Datacloud: 
https://datacloud.helsinki.fi/index.php/f/1023071
The script for the analysis of the data is also backed up in P-drive: 
P:\h345\afarkkilab\Projects\NKI\iPDCs\Flow_cytometry_v2.Rmd
iPDCs flow cytometry analysis & Statistical analyses for blocking experiment 
Frozen tumor-derived samples from patients with high MHCII expression were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort. The tumor tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to cryopreservation. For iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as droplets in an omentum-derived extracellular matrix. Cultures were treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions.

tion treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam) and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in FlowJo v10.10.0, applying a consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and subsequently analyzed in R.
Statistical analyses were performed in RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418) using R (version 4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFNg, GrzB) along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition. Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons between conditions. Results are presented as a heatmap showing mean log2 fold change of marker expression across pooled patients, with statistically significant differences indicated (
p
 < 0.05).
Protocols and other relevant documents, raw data, R scripts to generate the plots, and generated plots can be found on the P Drive: P:\h345\afarkkilab\Projects\NKI\iPDCs\aHLA_iPDC

Legendplex cytokine profiling assay: 
Conditioned media from the HLA-Dr blocking experiment was collected by Kursat and frozen at -80 deg until the time of the assay. 
On the assay day (20.11.2025), María thawed the samples on ice and performed the assay following the manufacturer’s recommended protocol, assisted by Sarah.
Protocol can be found here:
https://www.biolegend.com/Files/Images/media_assets/pro_detail/datasheets/Hu-CD8-Panel-V02_FK.pdf
Samples were run in duplicates. 
Data was generated using the Fortessa cytometer in the Flow core, on the same day that the assay was performed. 
Data was analyzed using the LegendPlex proprietary software and final plots were generated using R scripts and averaged data among technical replicates, and then among patients. During the data analysis, Anniina informed María that the pembro and untreated conditions were mistakenly switched during the blocking experiment, so the labels for these two conditions were switched in the final plots as per her instructions. 
Raw data, generated plots, and scripts to generate the plots can be found in P Drive: P:\h345\afarkkilab\Projects\NKI\iPDCs\MHCII_blockade_LEGENDplex_assay

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 
22 
Lead contact.
Abstract
The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 
1
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5
and significant tumor heterogeneity 
6
. This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 
7
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with
BRCA1/2
mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte i
nfiltration
8,9
. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 
10
and unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies 
11,12
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
13–15
,
resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
16
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (
clinicomolecular
) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas
 capturing
 15.1 million single
cells from
 280 
advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multi
omic
 molecular profiling 
l
everaging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling
. 
Using 
a tailored
 image analysis 
approach

vector_chunks.text:
illion single
cells from
 280 
advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multi
omic
 molecular profiling 
l
everaging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling
. 
Using 
a tailored
 image analysis 
approach
, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA 
(Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest)
, to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover 
clinically relevant
 features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (
MHCII
) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune
infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.
RESULTS
Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping
 of 
HGSC tumors
To 
uncover
 the relationship between the 
TME ecosystem
 topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied 
multiomic
 profiling including 
targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling 
combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 
2
65 advanced HGSCs
(
Fig. 1a
)
. The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=
100
) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=
158
).

(

)
. The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=
100
) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=
158
). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven patients. Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, 
with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months)
(
S.
 Table 1
). 
M
ost

chemotherapy
, and the median progression-free survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). 
We conducted a hierarchical mul
tidimensional

Firstly,

 using targeted

BRCA1

Tumors without 
BRCA
loss (
BRCA1/2

CCNE1
-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and 
dedicated

We categorized 
174

S.

into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (
BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification
) 
17
. The rest (n=91) of the tumors were left “unassigned” as they
 could not be reliably categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods). 
To
precisely map the

(
t-CycIF
)
 targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing 
tissue microarrays (
TMAs) (

b,

To

(

)

b,

I
mage 
processing
 and quality filtering
 produced
9
29 
single-cell maps of HGSC from
 253 patients
(
Fig. 1a, 
S.
 Fig. 1
b
)
. In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485
high-qualit
y single cells, with an 
average
 of 20,000 cells for each tumor. The cell phenotyping was 
based on the multidimensional protein expression using two sequential approaches: 1) Per-tumor detection of 
cancer
 cells through unsupervised clustering based on epithelial marker expression and, 2) Annotation of s
tromal and immune populations using 
TRIBUS
18
 - a tool 
that performs highly granular unsupervised clustering, followed by automated cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme 
(
S.
 Fig. 1
c
). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping 
(Fig. 1
c,d
)
 by visual assessment of the cell annotation masks overlayed with the highly multiplexed images via customized Napari plugin 
19
(
Fig. 1e,f
). 
The annotated cell phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles corresponding to their respective cell lineages
 (
S.
 Fig. 1
d
). 
The deep cell phenotypes 
annotation
 comprised two non-immune stromal populations 
(annotated stromal)
constituting 40% of the cells
 (CD31+ endothelial cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15 immune populations corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages
 such as macrophages, neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells (
Fig. 1g,h
). The per-sample abundance of CD8+ T
and CD20+ B cells 
aligned

vector_chunks.text:
ns corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages
 such as macrophages, neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells (
Fig. 1g,h
). The per-sample abundance of CD8+ T
and CD20+ B cells 
aligned 
with the expert pathologists’ annotations based on 
immunohistochemistry (IHC) 
(
S.
 Fig. 1
e
,
f
). 
When visualized based on their marker expression using uniform manifold approximation and projection (UMAP), the cells formed distinct visual clusters that aligned with our cell type annotations (
Fig. 1d,h
). Following cell phenotyping, we quantified functional cell states, including Ki67 (
Fig. 1i, S. Fig. 1g
) and PDL1 expression (
S. Fig. 1g
). This analysis enabled us to explore lineage-specific phenotypes, such as the higher expression of the proliferative marker Ki67 in immune cell populations compared to stromal cells, and increased expression of the immune checkpoint inhibitor PDL1 in neutrophils and CD4+ T cells relative to stromal cells. Notably, some of these phenotypic patterns have been associated with long-term survival in HGSC 
20
. Additionally, we quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods (
Fig. 1j
).
To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer 
cells 
21
, we clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations 
(
Fig. 1k to m
).
These populations exhibited distinct molecular and morphological characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high expression of immun
e regulatory
 markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1);

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0008
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
phenotypic populations 
(
Fig. 1k to m
).
These populations exhibited distinct molecular and morphological characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high expression of immun
e regulatory
 markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle cluster displayed 
overall lower
 expression of the functional markers
 (
Fig. 1k, l; 
Ext. material - Fig
.
 1, 2
). 
Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles 
We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the defined 
clinicomolecular
 groups. 
Statistical testing across all 
molecular groups
 did not reveal statistically significant differences after multiple hypothesis testing
 (
S.
 Fig. 2a, 
S. Table 3
). 
However
, 
consistent with 
previous reports
8,22
, we observed that 
BRCA
loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (
Fig. 2a
). 
Notably, a higher abundance of 
general 
myeloid cells 
was observed exclusively in 
BRCA1
loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples (
Fig. 2a
). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were enriched in IDS samples 
(
S. Fig. 2b
).
Interestingly, all lymphocyte
 populations
 and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant co-infiltration within the TME 
across 
the 
tumors
 (
Fig. 2b, 
S. Table 4, 5
). In contrast, other
myeloid cells, 
such as
 macrophages, did not 
exhibit 
this co-infiltration (
Fig. 2b
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0009
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ulations
 and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant co-infiltration within the TME 
across 
the 
tumors
 (
Fig. 2b, 
S. Table 4, 5
). In contrast, other
myeloid cells, 
such as
 macrophages, did not 
exhibit 
this co-infiltration (
Fig. 2b
). These results imply that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated process potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms. 
We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the abundance of lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival (OS) (
Fig. 2c
, 
S.
 Fig. 2
c
) and progression-free survival (PFS) (
S.
 Fig. 2
d
). The abundance of CD11c.MY and 
general
m
yeloid cells also showed association with longer PFS (
S.
 Fig. 2
d
). Notably, in IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic significance, suggesting that tumor-intrinsic factors
 related to chemotherapy exposure 
beyond immune cell abundance 
are relevant for
 prognosis, as previously suggested 
23,24
. To note, the highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the interpretability of individual and 
multivariable
 hazard ratios.
For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant differences across the molecular profiles (
S. Fig. 2e
). However, the EMT phenotype was enriched in BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS samples (
S. Fig. 2e
). 
The relative abundance of these cancer cell phenotypes was associated with OS (
Fig. 2d
) and survival PFS (
S.
 Fig. 2
f
), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS.

) and survival PFS (
S.
 Fig. 2
f
), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For example, a higher abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was associated with shorter OS in IDS tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS but not in IDS (
Fig. 2d
). We next performed multivariable Cox 
models using
stricter
 inclusion criteria 
for patients in the analyses 
(
minimum of two tumor cores and more than 5% of cancer cells) 
(
S.
 Fig. 1
b
) further confir
ming
differential
 associations with clinical outcomes (
S.
 Fig. 2
g
). 
Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship between immune cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor's exposure to chemotherapy.
Spatial TME 
domains
 associate with distinct molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure 
To map the spatial TME domains, 
we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods).
We uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN) (
Fig. 2e to g
). Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01 to 
RCN
03), cancer (RCN04 to 
RCN
08),
 immune and stromal cells adjacent to cancer cells (annotated 
tumor-stroma
 interface, TSI; 
RCN09
 and RCN10)
 and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial 
domains
 included a 
large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial cell-rich domain (RCN03) (
Fig. 2e to g
). 
The cancer spatial domains included 
the

vector_chunks.text:
RCN09
 and RCN10)
 and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial 
domains
 included a 
large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial cell-rich domain (RCN03) (
Fig. 2e to g
). 
The cancer spatial domains included 
the
 phenotypically mo
st
heterogeneous
 neighbourhood (RCN04), and four neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell functional 
states
(
Fig. 2e to g
)
. 
The immune spatial 
domains
 included 
an immune
 B
cell 
neighborhood
 (RCN13), 
tentatively consisting of tertiary lymphoid structures (
S. Fig. 2h
), and neighborhood of myeloid cells (RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11) (
Fig. 2e to g
). These spatial domains were validated with visual inspection of the t-CycIF images (
Fig. 2f, g; S. Fig. 2h
).
Spatial composition analysis revealed that
 several of the
 domains
 were intercorrelated, suggesting spatially coordinated TME 
domains
 (
Fig. 2h, 
S.
 Table 
6, 7
). For example, two immune domains (RCN11 - T cells, RCN13 -B cells) showed a positive correlation with each other, as well as with the immune-infiltrated stroma (RCN02). In contrast, the immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation with these immune domains (
Fig. 2h
). 
The cancer cell domains also revealed significant correlations with other spatial domains. 
For instance, the neighborhood of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was positively correlated with the immune-cold tumor-stromal interface (TSI), whereas the neighborhood of cancer cells displaying immunogenic phenotypes was negatively correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01) (
Fig. 2h
). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME are spatially
 coordinated
 in HGSC.
We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the clinicomolecular profiles
.

vector_chunks.text:
ely correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01) (
Fig. 2h
). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME are spatially
 coordinated
 in HGSC.
We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the clinicomolecular profiles
. 
The overall comparisons did not reveal significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most heterogeneous RCNs,
 the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in 
BRCA
loss tumors (
Fig. 2i, j
).
Interestingly, 
comparison of the spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01 and RCN02), and immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (
Fig. 2k
). 
Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant in the IDS samples
 (
Fig. 
2k
) suggesting 
phenotypic convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our previous observation 
23
. 
These results 
highlight distinct
 spatial TME domain topographies associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure. 
T
umor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes
Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (
RCN
10) exhibited the highest div
ersity
(Shannon diversity index
: 2.3, 
Fig. 2e
), exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs.
Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of 
T cells,
myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cell
s suggesting the presence of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI 
(
Fig. 2l
)
.

omparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of 
T cells,
myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cell
s suggesting the presence of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI 
(
Fig. 2l
)
. Consistent with our previous observations 
8
, the 
BRCA
loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (
Fig. 2
j
)
. By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9)
, 
which was more abundant in the IDS samples compared to PDS (
Fig. 2
k
), was significantly enriched 
in fibroblast stromal cells 
(
Fig. 2l
)
.
 Additionally, the immune-cold TSI showed a higher prevalence of 'Idle'-type cancer cells lacking activity-linked phenotypic marker expressions, indicative of an 'immune-evasive' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of the immune system (
Fig. 2l
). 
Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched proliferative, immunogenic and EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive “immune escape” mechanisms at the TSI 
25
.
Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical outcomes. Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy sequence (PDS/IDS), we identified three RCNs (
RCN
03, RCN09, RCN10) significantly associated with both PFS and OS (
S.
 Fig. 2
i
, 
j
). Notably, the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular composition emerged as a key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across the entire patient cohort (
Fig. 2m
). Specifically, a high abundance of the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis, whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer outcomes (
Fig. 2m
). 
In summary, t
hese findings 
suggest a role 
for the

cally, a high abundance of the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis, whereas the abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer outcomes (
Fig. 2m
). 
In summary, t
hese findings 
suggest a role 
for the
 multicellular spatial domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI 
emerged as
 the most diverse spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial organization, and 
clinicomolecular
 characteristics suggests a coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.
CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features
To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
 a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) 
framework (
Fig. 3a
). The primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. 
CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by:

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Copy of Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
EFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. 
CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 
1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) 
to achieve a consensus estimation. Additionally
, our 
versatile
, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multi
dimensional
 data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles (
Fig. 3a
).
We aggregated
 the multidimensional
 spatial data for each of the
 4,644,485
 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (
Fig. 1e, i; 
S.
 Fig. 1g
), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (
Fig. 1j
), resulting in 43
 feature representations per cell (Materials and Methods). 
To uncover multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, 
we stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS
 (
S.
 Fig. 3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for 
either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1

ied the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS
 (
S.
 Fig. 3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for 
either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1
amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. As expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (
S.
 Fig. 3c,d
). 
The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both PDS (
Fig. 3b, S
. Table 8
) and IDS (
Fig. 3c
, S. Table 8
), 
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell spatial data. 
Cancer cell intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression is a key feature associated with of overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, 
CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic M
HC
II as one of the key

and IDS
 samples (
S.
 Fig. 3c,d
).
Notably, of the

cancer cell-
specific 
MHCII
expressio
n showed the highest fold-change enrichment
 in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(
Fig. 
3d,
 e
; 
S. Table 8
).
 By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (
Fig. 
3
d
). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring cells 
at
 t
he TSI
. Indeed, visual inspection of the 
t-CycIF
 images confirmed 
MHCII

at

he TSI
. Indeed, visual inspection of the

MHCII

Vimentin, CD3d, and PD-1 signals were primarily
signals originating from

 (

3

; S
. Fig. 3e
). In addition, 
MHCII was also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells 
in

Fig. 
3

.
 Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial proximity, are 
relevant 
for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.
N
ext, to determine if M
HC
II expression is 
specific
 to a molecular profile,
 we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of individual cells
. We observed that PDS samples demonstrated superior classification performance than IDS samples (
Fig. 
3
g
, 
S.

3
f
). In cancer cell models, MHCII was a key feature with higher expression in the HRD-like tumors (
Fig. 
3
h, 
S.

g
). The CEFIIRA model to classify 
BRCA
loss or HRP cells achieved the highest predictive performance (Median F1=0.77, 
Fig. 
3
g
), with 
MHCII

BRCA
loss cancer cells (
S.
 Fig. 
3
g, h
), also confirmed from the 
t-CycIF

Fig. 
3
i
). These results indicate that cancer
-
intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression is enriched in the HRD tumors.
We next aimed to characterize the heterogeneity of MHCII expression both across and within tumors.

lso confirmed from the 
t-CycIF
 images (
Fig. 
3
i
). These results indicate that cancer
-
intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression is enriched in the HRD tumors.
We next aimed to characterize the heterogeneity of MHCII expression both across and within tumors. We observed that 17% of cancer cells exhibited MHCII expression levels comparable to or exceeding those of CD11c+MY cells (
S. Fig. 3i
). Tumors showed variable proportions of MHCII+ cancer cells (
Fig. 3j
), with these proportions positively correlated with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; 
S. Fig. 3j
). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern, as confirmed through t-CycIF image analysis (
S. Fig. 3k, l
). This suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.
To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the multi
omics 
cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS (
Fig.
 3
k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. 
To further corroborate the prognostic 
role of 
MHCII
 expression by cancer cells
 in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
5
, by inferring the 
cancer-cell-specific
MHCII
 expression
 from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM 
26
.

rroborate the prognostic 
role of 
MHCII
 expression by cancer cells
 in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
5
, by inferring the 
cancer-cell-specific
MHCII
 expression
 from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM 
26
.
Notably, the observed MHCII expression was not associated with genomic alterations (somatic mutations, loss of heterozygosity, or promoter hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene (
S. Fig. 3l
). 
However, its expression was higher in 
BRCA
loss tumors compared to HRP and 
CCNE1
amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results (
S. Fig. 3m
).
Consistently using adjusted Cox models
, we observed that high 
MHCII
 expression by cancer cells 
predicted

Fig. 
3l
, 
m
). These findings confirm that 
MHCII
 expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of improved clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct tumor-immune interactions within the TME.
Cancer 
M
HCII
 expression 
associates with immune infiltration
To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression on overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis 
revealed a
 higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells,
 and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+
.MY
)
 (
Fig. 
4
a
). To corroborate these findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found 
that tumors with high 
MHCII
 expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T cells and M1-like macrophages (
S.
 Fig. 
4
a
). In contrast, tumors with 
high

vector_chunks.text:
cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found 
that tumors with high 
MHCII
 expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T cells and M1-like macrophages (
S.
 Fig. 
4
a
). In contrast, tumors with 
high 
MHCII
 expression
 had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-tumor responses (
S.
 Fig. 
4
a
). 
Overall, these results demonstrate that 
HGSC
 tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more
 immune infiltration in the TME.
To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and underlying t
ranscriptional programs
, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both from 
PDS (N=29)
, and 
IDS
 (n=22)
, 
from 29 patients using single-cell RNA sequencing
23
 (
scRNA-seq
) (
Fig. 
4
b
). The 
dataset 
comprised 136,452 cells, which included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and 97,409 immune cells, 
annotated
 using canonical phenotypic markers as described 
previously 
23
 (
see Methods
). Using gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a high 
MHCII
 expression level similar to that of canonical antigen-presenting cells (
S.
 Fig. 
4
b
).
 The proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity (
S. Fig. 4c,d
), prompting us to conduct sample-based downstream analyses. 
We observed that 
samples
 with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17 proinflammatory 
helper CD4+ T cells 
27
, central memory (CM) CD8+ T cells, 
related to activation after antigen exposure 
28,29
, and precursor exhausted (PEX) CD8+ T cells, which 
have traits of both exhaustion and memory 
30
(
Fig. 
4
c to e
).

proinflammatory 
helper CD4+ T cells 
27
, central memory (CM) CD8+ T cells, 
related to activation after antigen exposure 
28,29
, and precursor exhausted (PEX) CD8+ T cells, which 
have traits of both exhaustion and memory 
30
(
Fig. 
4
c to e
).
Similar results were observed at the patient level taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations (
CM
 CD8+ T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the smaller sample size 
(S. Fig. 4e
).
 Altogether, these findings suggest that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.
Cancer cell 
MHCII
 expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity
To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic 
MHCII

RCN
08). Our analysis revealed high levels of 
MHCII

S.
 Fig. 
4f
), abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers, including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (
Fig. 1
l
). 
This indicates that MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the cancer cell spatial compartments.
We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell 
MHCII
 expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of 
MHCII
 in cancer cells within the
 immune-infiltrated
 TSI (RCN10) as compared to the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (
S. Fig. 4f
). 
Consistently, analysis of
 the cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells resided more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to M
HC
II- cancer cells

). 
Consistently, analysis of

HC
II- cancer cells
across the diverse 
clinicomolecular
 profiles
 (
Fig. 
4f
, 
S.

4g
). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly 
neighboring stromal cells
. 
Visual inspection of the tumor tissues confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune cells, forming immune ‘hotspots’, while MHCII- cells were embedded in immune ‘cold’ tumor niches (
Fig. 
4g
).
To expand our analyses to 
whole tissue sections, we generated high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using 
t-CycIF

Fig. 
4h
) from an independent cohort in the Oncosys-OVA prospective trial (NCT06117384). We captured and phenotyped 10.5 million cells 
using the TRIBUS (

4i
,
S.
 Fig. 
4h
)
.
In line with previous findings, we observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression within and across the tumors 
(
S. Fig. 4
i,j
).
Consistently, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells associated with longer progression-free survival (PFS) (
Fig. 
4j
).
To note
,

S.
 Fig. 
4k
), in agreement with the observation in the mult
iomics

samples
. Importantly, we observed that the MHCII+ cancer cells 
(
S.
 Fig. 
4i
)
 had s
ignificantly m
ore neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+ antigen presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells, generating spatial immune hotspots (

4k
).

ignificantly m
ore neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+ antigen presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells, generating spatial immune hotspots (

). 
This additional single-cell spatial analysis further highlights that MHCII+ cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains, enriched in T-cells and antigen presenting cells.
To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the 
matched tumor and adjacent stromal regions 
TSI in 11 HGSC-IDS tumors (
Fig. 4l
). Using deconvolution, we observed that tumor MHCII expression was associated with a significantly higher inferred proportion of particularly dendritic cells and T cells in the adjacent stroma of the TSI (
Fig. 4m,n
). 
Altogether, these findings demonstrate that MHCII+ cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains and associate with spatial immune infiltration and 
antigen presentation

TSI

Cancer-intrinsic MHCII associates with

spatial immune activation

with the

II+ tumors.

(

(

Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort. For this, we utilized 
expression data of 335 genes profiled 
with the Nanostring technology 
36
.
Consistently, a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (
S.
 Fig. 
5d
, 
S.
 Table 
10
). 
Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) activation 
37–39
, were among the top overexpressed genes 
as in TCGA
, suggesting that IFN activation plays a key role in 
MHCII-associated
 immune activation, as previously suggested also in other cancers 
40–42
.
To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously available spatial transcriptomic dataset 
43
incorporating expert pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses 
(
Fig. 
5
b
, 
S.
 Fig. 
5e
).

I expression and IFN-γ signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously available spatial transcriptomic dataset 
43
incorporating expert pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses 
(
Fig. 
5
b
, 
S.
 Fig. 
5e
). The annotated TME areas exhibited distinct transcriptomic profiles 
visualized in
 differential clusters in a UMAP projection (
S.
 Fig. 
5f
). Using MHCII and IFN-γ signatures 
44
 (see Methods), w
e found that the 
MHCII
 expression was significantly correlated with IFN-γ signature expression across distinct tumor areas, including cancer and TSI regions (
Fig. 
5
c
). Moreover, a higher 
MHCII
 signature and increased IFN-γ in both cancer and 
TSI 
spatial regions predicted improved clinical responses to chemotherapy 
at 
spot level
(
S.
 Fig. 
5g
,
h
),
and similar trends were observed at the patient level, although statistical significance was not reached due to the smaller sample size (
S. Fig. 5i
,
j
)
.
 Interestingly, in our 
t-CycIF
 spatial data (multi
omics 
cohort), statistical analysis revealed significantly higher expression of phospho-STAT1 (pSTAT1), indicative of IFN activation in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and antigen-presenting (CD11c+) myeloid cells neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells at the TSI (
Fig. 
5
d
), confirmed via visual inspection (
Fig. 
5
e
, 
S.
 Fig. 
5k
). These findings suggest that cancer cell 
MHCII
 associates with spatially coordinated activation of antigen presentation and T cells via IFN signaling at the TSI. Further, 
using
 the 
scRNA-seq
 dataset, we observed that the 
MHCII
 expression in cancer cells correlated with 
IFNG
gene expression specifically in 
CD4+ memory helper and CD8+
 central memory (CM) T cells
, which are related to activation after antigen exposure
(
Fig. 
5
f
).

dataset, we observed that the 
MHCII
 expression in cancer cells correlated with 
IFNG
gene expression specifically in 
CD4+ memory helper and CD8+
 central memory (CM) T cells
, which are related to activation after antigen exposure
(
Fig. 
5
f
).
 Overall, these findings reinforce the link between cancer
-
intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of cancer cell 
MHCII
 in spatial immune activation. 
To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict ligand–receptor pairs (
Fig. 5g, Ext. material - Fig.4
). We identified the top seven ligand–receptor interactions across CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (
Fig. 5h
). Interestingly, IFN signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed 
TGF-β1 interactions across the cell populations
, which may indicate the induction of immune tolerance 
45
. Similarly, in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (
Fig. 5i
), we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL, IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (
Fig. 5
j
). In addition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig. 5j
).

ition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig. 5j
). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.
Cancer
MHCII
 is associated with distinct antigen presentation
, increased TCR diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response 
in vitro
Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins 
46
. 
To 
explore 
whether cancer-specific 
MHCII
 expression is linked with
 specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously 
identified
 antigens 
detected via
HLA ligandome analysis
 in HGSC 
47
(
Fig. 
6a
). We found that the deconvoluted expression 
of 
MHCII
 in cancer 
cells is indeed
associated 
with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in the TCGA dataset (
Fig. 
6b
). However, in our 
scRNA-seq
 dataset, only four specific antigen genes (
UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN
) were expressed in the cancer cells, and 
associated 
with tumor 
MHCII
 expression (
Fig. 
6c
). 
In our GeoM
x
 spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also obser
ved positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (
Fig. 6d
).
To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC,

vector_chunks.text:
r
ved positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (
Fig. 6d
).
To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC, 
we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq (
Fig. 6e
). We identified the presence of previously reported genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets that showed significant correlations and were consistently observed across the patients (
Fig. 6f
). 
Moreover, the genes enriched in samples
 with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells were significantly enriched in cellular components related to junctions, adhesion, the extracellular matrix, and cell–cell contact zones, as well as in biological processes involved in protein localization and regulation (
Fig. 6g,h
). To explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We observed a significantly increased number of T cell receptor clones—specifically TRAD and TRB —in samples with a high MHCII cancer proportion (
Fig. 6i
).
Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next investigated whether tumor MHCII status influences CD8+
T-cell responses to immunotherapy using our previously established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) 
16
. For this, 
we analysed 
flow cytometry data from 
drug perturbation assays
 of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five chemo-treated tumor samples (
Fig. 6j
). The iPDCs 
were stratified into MHCII-high
and MHCII-low
