To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the 
multiomics
 cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS (
Fig. 3k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
5

S. Fig. 3l
). However, its expression was higher in 
BRCA

Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration 
To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (
Fig. 4a
). To corroborate these findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T cells and M1-like macrophages (
S. Fig. 4a
). In contrast, tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-tumor responses (
S. Fig. 4a
).

S. Fig. 4a
). In contrast, tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-tumor responses (
S. Fig. 4a
). Overall, these results demonstrate that HGSC tumors with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more immune infiltration in the TME.

similar to

(S. Fig. 4e

Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity
To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (
S. Fig. 4f
), abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers, including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (
Fig. 1l
). This indicates that MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the cancer cell spatial compartments.
We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (
S. Fig. 4f
). Consistently, 
analysis of the cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells resided more often

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.
DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-
omic
 spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying phenotypic compositions. These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic profiles and 
clinicomolecular
 groups, underscoring the role of molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 
Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC.

tcomes, while the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 
Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI 
neighbourhoods
 generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance 
48,49
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T 
cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 
50
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 
51
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated, and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of 
neighbouring
 CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig. 7

d 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated, and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of 
neighbouring
 CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig. 7
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
52
. In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 
53
. Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer 
41,50,54,55
. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest benefits 
56
. In contrast, our 
in vitro 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in 
iPDCs
 suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our study revealed that other immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC 
16
.

ckpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC 
16
. Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active 
antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients with tumors lacking MHCII expression. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 
clinicomolecular
 subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting some of the statistical comparisons between patients. Our protein marker panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance.

e accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient cohorts.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA) 
57
.
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV
, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites).

riginated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV
, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as previously described 
57
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-
CycIF
. 
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.

Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (
S. Table 2
) and scanned with a 
RareCyte
CyteFinder
 scanner following the t-
CycIF
 protocol 
61
. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images’ illumination was corrected using the 
BaSiC
 tool (RRID:SCR_016371) 
62

Cell phenotyping
For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package 
mclust
 (RRID:SCR_027153).

either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package 
mclust
 (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.
01th

Machine learning supervised classification
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); (2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis.

oportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the 
PyCaret
 library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: 
BRCAloss
, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on 
PyCaret
 runs, we selected Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance (weighted-
averaged
 AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (
S. Table 14
). In addition, we utilized the 
PyCaret
 RF model to determine the number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct models for two classes (
S. Table 15
).
Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into

groups (

we opted to further construct models for two classes (

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
groups (
https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA
). The models' training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 
30 data
 partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
S. Fig. 3n
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores.

on accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

We retrieved 
GeoMx

Launonen

https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672

terest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. (RRID:SCR_015687) 
31
, followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied 
BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets 
78
. This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw 
scRNA

GeoMx
 counts. To prepare the 
scRNA
-seq reference, we 
downsampled

Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells).

final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard

We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (
ssGSEA
) scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated 
ssGSEA
 scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from 
BayesPrism
) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.

ysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),

Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described

 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 
μm

Thermo

Igepal
 (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an 
AminoLink
 Plus Immunoaffinity column (
Thermo
 Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-
DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, 
RayBiotech
, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid.

MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. ms.
 Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and 
resequenced
 until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K
0  =
  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K
0  =
  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 
10 ng
 injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (
ThermoFischer
 Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the 
FragPipe
 computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. 
MSFragger
81
 was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher 
82

, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher 
82
, applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using 
IonQuant
83
, enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) 
84
 with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using 
ArcasHLA
 (RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant
) 
85
 with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis, peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, 
scRNA
-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-
CyCIF
 imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups.

ts 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using 
Enrichr
 (RRID:SCR_001575) 
86
 (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms were recorded.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in 
immunopeptidomics
 and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the 
iCAN
 study. Tumor samples underwent RNA sequencing with Illumina 
NovaSeq
 6000 using the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at >100 million read pairs at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using 
MiXCR
 (RRID:SCR_018725) 
87
, following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘
mixcr
 analyze 
rnaseq
’, followed by ‘
mixcr
exportQc
 align’ and ‘
mixcr
exportQc
chainUsage
’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset.

ected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the 
immunarch
 R package (RRID:SCR_023089) 
88
. We employed the ‘
repExplore
’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in 
Immunopeptidomics
): (1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.
iPDCs
 flow cytometry analysis
The dataset used for this analysis was generated in 
Launonen
 et al., 2024 
16
. Briefly, tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was dissociated, and 
iPDCs
 were established by embedding the cells in an 
omentum
-derived matrix 
89
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor 
flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at 
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
.
In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying 
EpCAM
⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S. Fig. 6
).

stratifying 
EpCAM
⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S. Fig. 6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed 
using 
FlowJo
 v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages 
survminer
 (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
S. Fig. 1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups.

g immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-
Wallis
 test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
A
 schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss

BRCA
loss

pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss
 and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features 
are identified.

Abstract
The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 
1
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5

to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5
 and significant tumor heterogeneity 
6
. This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 
7
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 
BRCA1/2
 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher 
lymphocyte infiltration 
8,9
. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 
10
 and unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies 
11,12
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
13–15
, resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
16
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (
clinicomolecular
) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 
multiomic
 molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling.

solution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 
multiomic
 molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by 
RAndom
 forest
), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.
RESULTS
Multiomic
 profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors
To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied 
multiomic
 profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (
Fig. 1a
). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking

ofiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (
Fig. 1a
). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking 
surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=158). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven patients. Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months) (
S. Table 1
). Most patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and the median progression-free survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). 
We conducted a hierarchical multidimensional analysis to assign the tumors’ molecular profiles. Firstly, 
BRCA1/2

BRCA1

BRCA
loss

BRCA1/2

BRCA1

CCNE1
-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and dedicated algorithms (see Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (
S. Fig. 1a) 
into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (
BRCAloss
, HRD, Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification
) 
17
. The rest (n=91) of the tumors were left “unassigned” as they could not be reliably categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods).

To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used 
hyperplexed

CycIF
) targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing tissue microarrays (TMAs) (
Fig. 1b, S. Table 2

produced 929

Fig. 1a, S. Fig. 1b
). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485 high-quality single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each tumor. The cell phenotyping was based on the multidimensional protein expression using two sequential approaches: 1) Per-tumor detection of cancer cells through unsupervised clustering based on epithelial marker expression and, 2) Annotation of stromal and immune populations using TRIBUS 
18
 - a tool that performs highly granular unsupervised clustering, followed by automated cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping scheme (
S. Fig. 1c
). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping 
(Fig. 1c,d)

multiplexed images via customized Napari plugin 
19

Fig. 1e,f

S. Fig. 1d
). The deep cell phenotypes annotation comprised two non-immune stromal populations (annotated stromal) constituting 40% of the cells (CD31+ endothelial cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA
+ fibroblasts

+ fibroblasts
) and 15 immune populations corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and regulatory T cells, and Myeloid lineages such as macrophages, neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells (

g,h

e,f

Fig. 1
d,h

Fig. 1i, S. Fig. 1g
) and PDL1 expression (
S. Fig. 1g
). This analysis enabled us to explore lineage-specific phenotypes, such as the higher expression of the proliferative marker Ki67 in immune cell populations compared to stromal cells, and increased expression of the immune checkpoint inhibitor PDL1 in neutrophils and CD4+ T cells relative to stromal cells. Notably, some of these phenotypic patterns have been associated with long-term survival in HGSC 
20
. Additionally, we quantified the cell-to-cell spatial 
neighbourhoods
 (
Fig. 1j
).
To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells 
21
, we clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features.

To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells 
21
, we clustered the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers, mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations (
Fig. 1k to m
). These populations exhibited distinct molecular and morphological characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers (
Fig. 1k, l; Ext. material - Fig. 1, 2
). 
Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to 
clinicomolecular
 profiles 
We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the defined 
clinicomolecular
 groups. Statistical testing across all molecular groups did not reveal statistically significant differences after multiple hypothesis testing (
S. Fig. 2a, S. Table 3
). However, consistent with previous reports 
8,22

Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes
Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, 
Fig. 2e
), exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI (

). 
In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial organization, and 
clinicomolecular
 characteristics suggests a coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.
CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features
To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by 
RAndom
 forest
), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (
Fig. 3a
). The primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation

imensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation 
in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles (
Fig. 3a
).

Cancer MHCII expression associates with immune infiltration 
To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on overall immune infiltration, we compared the abundance of immune populations according to the fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (
Fig. 4a
). To corroborate these findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found that tumors with high MHCII expression in cancer cells were significantly enriched with T cells and M1-like macrophages (
S. Fig. 4a
). In contrast, tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-tumor responses (
S. Fig. 4a
).

S. Fig. 4a

Fig. 6d
).

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.
DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-
omic
 spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying phenotypic compositions. These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic profiles and 
clinicomolecular
 groups, underscoring the role of molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 
Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC.

tcomes, while the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 
Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI 
neighbourhoods
 generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance 
48,49
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T 
cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 
50
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 
51
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated, and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of 
neighbouring
 CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig. 7

d 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated, and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of 
neighbouring
 CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig. 7
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
52
. In HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 
53
. Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer 
41,50,54,55
. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest benefits 
56
. In contrast, our 
in vitro 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in 
iPDCs
 suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our study revealed that other immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC 
16
.

ckpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC 
16
. Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active 
antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients with tumors lacking MHCII expression. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 
clinicomolecular
 subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting some of the statistical comparisons between patients. Our protein marker panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance.

e accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient cohorts.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA) 
57
.
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV
, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites).

riginated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV
, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as previously described 
57
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-
CycIF
. 
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.

Machine learning supervised classification
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); (2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis.

oportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the 
PyCaret
 library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: 
BRCAloss
, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on 
PyCaret
 runs, we selected Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance (weighted-
averaged
 AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (
S. Table 14
). In addition, we utilized the 
PyCaret
 RF model to determine the number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct models for two classes (
S. Table 15
).
Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
groups (

we opted to further construct models for two classes (
S. Table 15
).
Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
groups (
https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA
). The models' training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 
30 data
 partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
S. Fig. 3n
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores.

on accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

GeoMx
 spatial transcriptomics data analysis 
We retrieved 
GeoMx
 spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy. Data generation is described in 
Launonen
 et al., 2024 
16
, and is publicly available at
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
. The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages.

terest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package 
(RRID:SCR_015687) 
31
, followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied 
BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets 
78
. This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw 
scRNA
-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw 
GeoMx
 counts. To prepare the 
scRNA
-seq reference, we 
downsampled
 the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells).

final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard 
BayesPrism
 workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell 
states’
. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (
ssGSEA
) scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated 
ssGSEA
 scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from 
BayesPrism
) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.

ysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),

Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (
logFC
) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the 
GeoMx

ssGSEA

Immunopeptidomics
We performed 
immunopeptidomic
 profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from 
iCAN
 study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
80
 with minor modifications.

through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
80
 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 
μm
 cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (
Thermo
 Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% 
Igepal
 (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an 
AminoLink
 Plus Immunoaffinity column (
Thermo
 Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-
DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, 
RayBiotech
, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid.

MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300

Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 
μm

). Chromatographic separation (

was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a

Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages 
survminer
 (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
S. Fig. 1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups.

g immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-
Wallis
 test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
A
 schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss

pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss
 and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features 
are identified.

b, 
and in panel 
c 
for IDS samples.

 Dot plots displaying the log2 fold change in scaled marker intensity between cells from short- and long-survival patients with HRP tumors. The markers displayed correspond to the top five most important features for each model (excluding clinical variables).
