43 Abstract

44 The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions,

f Obstetrics and Gynecology, Department of Oncology Clinical Trials Unit, Helsinki University
41 Hospital, Helsinki, Finland.
42 22
 Lead contact.


43 Abstract

44 The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions,
45 yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly
46 understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC
47 from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling.
48 Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically
49 heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the
50 tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we
51 identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival.
52 Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic
53 datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune
54 activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived
55 immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with
56 increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers
57 novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification
58 in HGSC.

59 INTRODUCTION

60 The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells,
61 immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1. The
62 continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
63 fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and
64 therapeutic resistance.

, and non-cellular components and their interactions 1. The
62 continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is
63 fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and
64 therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most
65 prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 2,3, characterized by high genomic
66 instability 4,5 and significant tumor heterogeneity 6. This heterogeneity is evident in
67 various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME
68 cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 7. The genetic
69 diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with
70 a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with
71 BRCA1/2 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher

 2
 72 lymphocyte infiltration 8,9. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD)
 73 tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 10 and unique responses to
 74 combinations with immunotherapies 11,12. Moreover, we and others have shown that
 75 exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by
 76 enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 13–15, resulting in
 77 spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 16. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex
 78 relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor
 79 ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment
 80 strategies for patients with HGSC.

 81 We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single

inicomolecular) features and the tumor
 79 ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment
 80 strategies for patients with HGSC.

 81 We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single
 82 cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and
 83 multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and
 84 mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial
 85 TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that
 86 associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool,
 87 CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate
 88 single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features.
 89 We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression
 90 specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of
 91 immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune
 92 hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of
 93 clinicomolecular profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune
 94 cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for
 95 targeted interventions in HGSC.

 96 RESULTS

 97 Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors

 98 To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical
 99 prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including
100 targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA

C tumors

 98 To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical
 99 prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including
100 targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA
101 methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265
102 advanced HGSCs (Fig. 1a). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking


 3
103 surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS
104 n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking
105 surgery, IDS n=158). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven
106 patients. Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected,
107 with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months) (S. Table 1). Most
108 patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and paclitaxel as adjuvant chemotherapy, and
109 the median progression-free survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months).

110 We conducted a hierarchical multidimensional analysis to assign the tumors’
111 molecular profiles. Firstly, BRCA1/2 germline or somatic mutation status was
112 assessed using targeted high-throughput sequencing, and BRCA1 promoter
113 hypermethylation was evaluated using a targeted DNA methylation assay. Tumors
114 without BRCAloss (BRCA1/2 somatic or germline mutation, or BRCA1 promoter
115 hypermethylation) were tested for Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and
116 CCNE1-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and dedicated
117 algorithms (see Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (S. Fig. 1a) into four clinically
118 and molecularly relevant groups (BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous Recombination
119 Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification) 17.

117 algorithms (see Methods). We categorized 174 tumors (S. Fig. 1a) into four clinically
118 and molecularly relevant groups (BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous Recombination
119 Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification) 17. The rest (n=91) of the tumors were left
120 “unassigned” as they could not be reliably categorized into the predefined molecular
121 profiles (see Methods).

122 To precisely map the spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used hyperplexed
123 imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence (t-CycIF) targeting 30 different proteins
124 and cell nuclei employing tissue microarrays (TMAs) (Fig. 1b, S. Table 2). Image
125 processing and quality filtering produced 929 single-cell maps of HGSC from 253
126 patients (Fig. 1a, S. Fig. 1b). In total, the dataset consists of 4,644,485 high-quality
127 single cells, with an average of 20,000 cells for each tumor. The cell phenotyping was
128 based on the multidimensional protein expression using two sequential approaches:
129 1) Per-tumor detection of cancer cells through unsupervised clustering based on
130 epithelial marker expression and, 2) Annotation of stromal and immune populations
131 using TRIBUS 18 - a tool that performs highly granular unsupervised clustering,
132 followed by automated cluster annotation based on a prior-knowledge cell phenotyping
133 scheme (S. Fig. 1c). We comprehensively validated the cell phenotyping (Fig. 1c,d)
134 by visual assessment of the cell annotation masks overlayed with the highly


 4
135 multiplexed images via customized Napari plugin 19 (Fig. 1e,f). The annotated cell
136 phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles corresponding to their respective cell
137 lineages (S. Fig. 1d).

 4
135 multiplexed images via customized Napari plugin 19 (Fig. 1e,f). The annotated cell
136 phenotypes displayed marker expression profiles corresponding to their respective cell
137 lineages (S. Fig. 1d). The deep cell phenotypes annotation comprised two non-
138 immune stromal populations (annotated stromal) constituting 40% of the cells (CD31+
139 endothelial cells and Vimentin+, α-SMA+ fibroblasts) and 15 immune populations
140 corresponding to 17% of the cells, including B cells, memory and regulatory T cells,
141 and Myeloid lineages such as macrophages, neutrophils and antigen-presenting cells
142 (Fig. 1g,h). The per-sample abundance of CD8+ T and CD20+ B cells aligned with
143 the expert pathologists’ annotations based on immunohistochemistry (IHC) (S. Fig.
144 1e,f). When visualized based on their marker expression using uniform manifold
145 approximation and projection (UMAP), the cells formed distinct visual clusters that
146 aligned with our cell type annotations (Fig. 1d,h). Following cell phenotyping, we
147 quantified functional cell states, including Ki67 (Fig. 1i, S. Fig. 1g) and PDL1
148 expression (S. Fig. 1g). This analysis enabled us to explore lineage-specific
149 phenotypes, such as the higher expression of the proliferative marker Ki67 in immune
150 cell populations compared to stromal cells, and increased expression of the immune
151 checkpoint inhibitor PDL1 in neutrophils and CD4+ T cells relative to stromal cells.
152 Notably, some of these phenotypic patterns have been associated with long-term
153 survival in HGSC 20. Additionally, we quantified the cell-to-cell spatial neighbourhoods
154 (Fig. 1j).

155 To decode the phenotypic and spatial heterogeneity of cancer cells 21, we clustered
156 the cancer cells based on the expression of cancer-cell phenotypic markers,
157 mesenchymal and functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using
158 unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer
159 cell phenotypic populations (Fig. 1k to m). These populations exhibited distinct
160 molecular and morphological characteristics. The immunogenic cluster corresponded
161 to cells with high expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1,
162 pTBK1); the Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell area;
163 the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle cluster displayed
164 overall lower expression of the functional markers (Fig. 1k, l; Ext. material - Fig. 1,
165 2).

166 Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to
167 clinicomolecular profiles

168 We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the defined
169 clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all molecular groups did not reveal
170 statistically significant differences after multiple hypothesis testing (S. Fig. 2a, S.
171 Table 3). However, consistent with previous reports 8,22, we observed that BRCAloss
172 tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY, specifically after
173 chemotherapy exposure (Fig. 2a). Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid
174 cells was observed exclusively in BRCA1loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples
175 (Fig. 2a).

173 chemotherapy exposure (Fig. 2a). Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid
174 cells was observed exclusively in BRCA1loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples
175 (Fig. 2a). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+
176 T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were enriched
177 in IDS samples (S. Fig. 2b). Interestingly, all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY
178 populations demonstrated significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors
179 (Fig. 2b, S. Table 4, 5). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages, did not
180 exhibit this co-infiltration (Fig. 2b). These results imply that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY
181 infiltrations represent a coordinated process potentially orchestrated through shared
182 biological mechanisms.

183 We next tested if the immune infiltration patterns were associated with clinical
184 outcomes. Using adjusted Cox models, we observed that the abundance of
185 lymphocytes was associated with longer overall survival (OS) (Fig. 2c, S. Fig. 2c) and
186 progression-free survival (PFS) (S. Fig. 2d). The abundance of CD11c.MY and
187 general myeloid cells also showed association with longer PFS (S. Fig. 2d). Notably,
188 in IDS tumors, all immune cell infiltrations lacked prognostic significance, suggesting
189 that tumor-intrinsic factors related to chemotherapy exposure beyond immune cell
190 abundance are relevant for prognosis, as previously suggested 23,24. To note, the
191 highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the interpretability of individual
192 and multivariable hazard ratios.

191 highly intercorrelated immune infiltration patterns limit the interpretability of individual

193 For most cancer cell populations, we did not observe significant differences across the
194 molecular profiles (S. Fig. 2e). However, the EMT phenotype was enriched in
195 BRCAloss tumors, particularly in IDS samples (S. Fig. 2e). The relative abundance of
196 these cancer cell phenotypes was associated with OS (Fig. 2d) and survival PFS (S.
197 Fig. 2f), with distinct patterns observed between PDS and IDS. For example, a higher

 6
198 abundance of the proliferative cancer cluster was associated with shorter OS in IDS
199 tumors but not in PDS, whereas the EMT cluster was linked to improved OS in PDS
200 but not in IDS (Fig. 2d). We next performed multivariable Cox models using stricter
201 inclusion criteria for patients in the analyses (minimum of two tumor cores and more
202 than 5% of cancer cells) (S. Fig. 1b) further confirming differential associations with
203 clinical outcomes (S. Fig. 2g). Altogether, these findings suggest that the relationship
204 between immune cell abundances and cancer cell phenotypes is linked to the
205 clinicomolecular profiles of tumors and influenced by the tumor's exposure to
206 chemotherapy.

207 Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and
208 chemotherapy exposure

209 To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells based on
210 their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We uncovered 14
211 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter referred to as recurrent cellular
212 neighborhoods (RCN) (Fig. 2e to g).

210 their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We uncovered 14
211 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter referred to as recurrent cellular
212 neighborhoods (RCN) (Fig. 2e to g). Four types of spatial domains were identified
213 containing stromal (RCN01 to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and
214 stromal cells adjacent to cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09
215 and RCN10) and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial
216 domains included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial
217 cell-rich domain (RCN03) (Fig. 2e to g). The cancer spatial domains included the
218 phenotypically most heterogeneous neighbourhood (RCN04), and four
219 neighbourhoods were predominated by distinct tumor cell functional states (Fig. 2e to
220 g). The immune spatial domains included an immune B cell neighborhood (RCN13),
221 tentatively consisting of tertiary lymphoid structures (S. Fig. 2h), and neighborhood of
222 myeloid cells (RCN12) and T-cells (RCN11) (Fig. 2e to g). These spatial domains
223 were validated with visual inspection of the t-CycIF images (Fig. 2f, g; S. Fig. 2h).

229 (RCN02). In contrast, the immune-cold stroma displayed a negative correlation with

251 Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes

252 Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the highest
253 diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, Fig. 2e), exhibiting the most heterogeneous
254 cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical comparison of the two TSI types,
255 revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T
256 cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated
257 immune infiltration at the TSI (Fig. 2l). Consistent with our previous observations 8,
258 the BRCAloss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (Fig.
259 2j). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was more abundant in the IDS
260 samples compared to PDS (Fig. 2k), was significantly enriched in fibroblast stromal
261 cells (Fig. 2l). Additionally, the immune-cold TSI showed a higher prevalence of 'Idle'-

 8
262 type cancer cells lacking activity-linked phenotypic marker expressions, indicative of
263 an 'immune-evasive' TSI where cancer cells could evade the recognition of the
264 immune system (Fig. 2l). Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) had
265 significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched proliferative, immunogenic
266 and EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive “immune escape” mechanisms at the TSI
 25
267 .

268 Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical outcomes.

268 Next, we assessed which RCNs were most strongly associated with clinical outcomes.
269 Using adjusted Cox regression models stratified by therapy sequence (PDS/IDS), we
270 identified three RCNs (RCN03, RCN09, RCN10) significantly associated with both
271 PFS and OS (S. Fig. 2i, j). Notably, the TSI spatial domain and its multicellular
272 composition emerged as a key determinant of OS in multivariable Cox models across
273 the entire patient cohort (Fig. 2m). Specifically, a high abundance of the immune-
274 infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was associated with improved prognosis, whereas the
275 abundance of immune-cold TSI (RCN09) correlated with poorer outcomes (Fig. 2m).

276 In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial domains and
277 immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes within the HGSC TME.
278 Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse spatial domain, exhibiting
279 divergent phenotypic compositions. The intricate interplay between immune co-
280 infiltration, spatial organization, and clinicomolecular characteristics suggests a
281 coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of
282 integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed
283 spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.

284 CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor
285 ecosystem features

286 To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and
287 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom

of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor
285 ecosystem features

286 To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and
287 prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom
288 forest), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (Fig. 3a). The
289 primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-
290 dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA
291 robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical
292 features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation


 9
293 in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent
294 overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and
295 balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a
296 prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a
297 sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical
298 features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the
299 previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus
300 estimation. Additionally, our versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate
301 multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient
302 and molecular profiles (Fig. 3a).

303 We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single
304 cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig.
305 1g), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (Fig.

(Fig. 3a).

303 We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single
304 cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (Fig. 1e, i; S. Fig.
305 1g), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (Fig. 1j), resulting in 43 feature
306 representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features
307 that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into three equally
308 sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (S. Fig. 3a,b). We generated CEFIIRA
309 models for either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-
310 like (BRCAloss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, CCNE1amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors,
311 respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. As
312 expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-
313 OS for most of the models (S. Fig. 3c,d). The models trained on HRP-like tumors
314 exhibited better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in
315 both PDS (Fig. 3b, S. Table 8) and IDS (Fig. 3c, S. Table 8), indicating that CEFIIRA
316 can separate the patients with differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using
317 multimodal single-cell spatial data.


318 Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with of overall
319 survival

320 Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the
321 key features for long or short OS prediction in both PDS and IDS samples (S. Fig.
322 3c,d). Notably, of the key features, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed the
323 highest fold-change enrichment in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to
324 short-OS patients (Fig. 3d, e; S. Table 8).

PDS and IDS samples (S. Fig.
322 3c,d). Notably, of the key features, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed the
323 highest fold-change enrichment in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to
324 short-OS patients (Fig. 3d, e; S. Table 8). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-

 10
325 SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were
326 enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive
327 marker of long-OS in PDS samples (Fig. 3d). The prevalence of stromal and immune
328 markers in cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring
329 cells at the TSI. Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed MHCII
330 expression by cancer cells, and that the Vimentin, CD3d, and PD-1 signals were
331 primarily signals originating from distinct neighboring cells adjacent to the cancer cells
332 (Fig. 3f; S. Fig. 3e). In addition, MHCII was also the top predictive marker in the
333 immune-stromal cells in IDS samples (Fig. 3e). Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed
334 that MHCII and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial
335 proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.

336 Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular profile, we trained
337 CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of individual cells. We observed that
338 PDS samples demonstrated superior classification performance than IDS samples

377 fraction of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of
378 MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells,
379 and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+.MY) (Fig. 4a). To corroborate these findings, we

of MHCII+ cancer cells per tumor. This analysis revealed a higher fraction of
378 MHCII+ tumor cells associated with enhanced infiltration of CD4+ and CD8+ T cells,

380 tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated
381 proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see
382 Methods). We found that tumors with high MHCII expression in cancer cells were

384 tumors with high MHCII expression had the lowest signal of immunosuppressive M2-
385 like macrophages, suggesting a more favorable immune microenvironment for anti-

387 with a high abundance of MHCII+ cancer cells have more immune infiltration in the
388 TME.

414 markers, including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (Fig. 1l). This indicates that MHCII+
415 cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the cancer cell spatial

453 Next, we set out to uncover transcriptional programs that underpin the immune
454 infiltration associated with the MHCII+ cancer cells. Interestingly, tumors with a high
455 MHCII expression exhibited upregulated genes related to immune activation, including
456 chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, granzymes, and immune receptors,
457 compared to tumors with low MHCII expression in the TCGA dataset 31 (S. Fig. 5a, b,
458 S. Table 9). Notably, we observed a significantly augmented expression of the
459 immune checkpoint molecules TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+ tumors.
460 Gene overrepresentation analysis 32 revealed 14 enriched pathways related to
461 immune activation (Fig. 5a), including both PD-1 and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ
462 activation. No pathways were enriched within the under-expressed genes.
463 Consistently, in DEG analysis of cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (Fig. 4b), we
464 found that MHCII positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors
465 RARRES3 and CLU associated with immune infiltration 33–35 (S. Fig. 5c).

466 Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort. For this, we

471 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with interferon-gamma (IFN-γ)
472 activation 37–39, were among the top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting

470 receptors (S. Fig. 5d, S. Table 10). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11
471 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with interferon-gamma (IFN-γ)

545 Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next investigated whether
546 tumor MHCII status influences CD8+ T-cell responses to immunotherapy using our
547 previously established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional patient-
548 derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) 16. For this, we analysed flow cytometry


 17
549 data from drug perturbation assays of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five chemo-
550 treated tumor samples (Fig. 6j). The iPDCs were stratified into MHCII-high and MHCII-
551 low groups based on baseline HLA-DR expression in EpCAM+ cancer cells compared
552 to immune and myeloid populations (see Methods and S. Fig. 6a,b). Notably, MHCII-
553 high tumors exhibited significantly greater CD8+ T-cell cytotoxic responses to
554 pembrolizumab (Fig. 6k, S. Table 11). Sample-by-sample analysis revealed a
555 statistically significant cytotoxicity gain (p < 0.05) between baseline and
556 pembrolizumab-treated conditions in the MHCII-high group (Fig. 6l).
557 Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via
558 MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying
559 spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic
560 MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the
561 context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.

562 DISCUSSION

563 We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026
564 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical
565 samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying
566 phenotypic compositions.

atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026
564 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical
565 samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying
566 phenotypic compositions. These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor
567 cell phenotypic profiles and clinicomolecular groups, underscoring the role of
568 molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies. Notably, of the
569 two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer
570 cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI
571 displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes.

572 Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional
573 single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through
574 spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that
575 MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate
576 immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with
577 enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and
578 antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize
579 MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune
580 surveillance 48,49, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T

 18
581 cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 50. While IFN expression in
582 tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests
583 it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 51. Through a robust spatial

rted recently in triple-negative breast cancer 50. While IFN expression in
582 tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests
583 it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 51. Through a robust spatial
584 TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within
585 immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity.
586 Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced
587 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively
588 present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII
589 and IFN-γ expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic
590 MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and
591 antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in Fig. 7,
592 support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+
593 T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated
594 with favorable prognosis in HGSC.

595 MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary
596 pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 52. In
597 HGSC, Burdett et al recently reported lower MHCII expression in HGSCs undergoing
 53
598 whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis .
599 Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved
600 response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-
601 negative breast cancer 41,50,54,55. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-

reover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved
600 response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-
601 negative breast cancer 41,50,54,55. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-
602 L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated
603 only modest benefits 56. In contrast, our in vitro analysis of immunotherapy responses
604 in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might
605 enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of
606 CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. In addition, our study revealed that other
607 immune checkpoint inhibitors, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were overexpressed in
608 tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical
609 exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients
610 with HGSC 16. Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in
611 MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR
612 clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. While tumor-
613 intrinsic MHCII expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active


 19
614 antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients
615 with tumors lacking MHCII expression. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas
616 uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the
617 clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic
618 approaches and patient stratification.

619 Study limitations

620 We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for

across the
617 clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic
618 approaches and patient stratification.

619 Study limitations

620 We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for
621 future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival
622 tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity,
623 limiting some of the statistical comparisons between patients. Our protein marker
624 panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell
625 types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there
626 is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have
627 matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning
628 models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further
629 improve predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable
630 hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient
631 cohorts.

713 Cohort description

714 We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors
715 from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL),
716 Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical
717 Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were

ors
715 from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL),
716 Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical
717 Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were
718 treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy
719 followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-
720 based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or
721 PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the
722 Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed,
723 paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network
724 of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA) 57.
725 Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or
726 IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (KvdV,
727 HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or
728 adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with
729 one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and
730 two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample.
731 Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+,
732 CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as previously described 57 and used for
733 pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-CycIF.

734 Whole slide cohort description

735 We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical
736 trial (NCT06117384). The samples were obtained from patients during interval
737 debulking surgeries (IDS). The selection criteria for these samples included HR-
738 proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-
739 free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease
740 (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent
741 IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based
742 on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-
743 PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.


744 Molecular profile assignment

745 For the TMA cohort, we first identified BRCA1/2 mutation and BRCA1 promoter
746 methylation status of each sample as previously described 57. In brief, BRCA1/2
747 germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer
748 Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a BRCA1/2 germline mutation, both
749 BRCA1/2 somatic mutations and BRCA1 promoter hypermethylation were assessed
750 using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was
751 performed using BRCA MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA,
752 USA) and Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent probe
753 amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the
754 number of tumors with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations and BRCA1 promoter methylation
755 are reported in S. Table 1.

753 amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the
754 number of tumors with BRCA1 or BRCA2 mutations and BRCA1 promoter methylation
755 are reported in S. Table 1.
756
757 Next, tumors without a BRCA1/2 mutation or BRCA1 promoter hypermethylation
758 underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using Illumina HiSeq2500 to
759 determine a BRCA-like CNV profile 58 and CCNE1-amplification status as described
760 previously 57. In addition, using the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using
761 the software shallowHRD 59. Finally, we categorized the tumors as BRCAloss, HRD,
762 HRP, and CCNE1amp using a hierarchical approach described in S. Fig. 1a. Samples
763 that failed QC for BRCA1/2 targeted DNA-sequencing, BRCA1 promoter
764 hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as “Unassigned” (or Other).

765 In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the
766 BRCA-like CNV profile 58 were labeled as “Unassigned”.

768 For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar
769 (RRID:SCR_027138) 60. For all analyses, only tumors with a TP53 mutation,
770 histological grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were
771 included. For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically validated HRD
772 classification performed at the Helsinki University Hospital, Finland, which uses
773 ovaHRDscar, and the detection of somatic and germline mutations and gene deletions
774 in BRCA1/2 genes.

775 Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing

776 To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were
777 represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we
778 sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in
779 a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker
780 panel (S. Table 2) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-
781 CycIF protocol 61. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture
782 the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images’ illumination was
783 corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371) 62. The per-image tiles were
784 stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266,
785 https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar). Individual TMA’s cores were cropped using
786 the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc.,
787 Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik
788 (RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 63 and Cellprofiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 64 using 2-
789 pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological
790 features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness were
791 quantified using MATLAB. Quality filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA
792 channel intensity across cycles (https://github.com/yunguan-
793 wang/cycif_analysis_suite), image regions with artifacts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with
794 less than 1000 high-quality cells.

805 Cell phenotyping

806 For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer
807 on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled
808 signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture
809 model from the R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were
810 fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer
811 cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors
812 (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the
813 annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the
814 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each

859 Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis

860 For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we
861 computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function
862 tl.spatial_count of the package scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) 69. We clustered the cells

 27
863 based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods
864 (RCNs), by using k-means and a k=40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the
865 package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value
866 of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick 70
867 (Ext. material - Fig. 3). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods
868 of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a radius of
869 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach
870 (Ext. material - Fig. 3). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as

900 Machine learning supervised classification

901 The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following
902 pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled
903 by slide using standard scaler (z-score); (2) three morphological features (area,
904 eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the
905 proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type;
906 additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS
907 the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis.
908 For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01
909 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had

ded patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis.
908 For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01
909 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had
910 less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent
911 machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed,
912 ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets,
913 maintaining balance across classes.
914
915 To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for
916 classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the PyCaret library on the entire
917 dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret). We employed
918 different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and
919 CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected Random Forest (RF) as a suitable
920 algorithm based on its mean performance (weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-
921 fold cross-validations (S. Table 14). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to
922 determine the number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC
923 and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running independent
924 models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold cross-validations. Based on the
925 results, we opted to further construct models for two classes (S. Table 15).
926
927 Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn
928 library (RRID:SCR_002577) 74 for binary classification of cells into clinicomolecular

 29
929 groups (https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA). The models' training process employs

mparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn

929 groups (https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA). The models' training process employs
930 nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training
931 and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose
932 30 data partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model
933 robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results
934 when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive
935 (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (S. Fig. 3n). For each nested cross-validation, using the
936 training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the
937 model (min_samples_split, min_samples_leaf, n_estimators). The Bayesian
938 optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’
939 values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine
940 cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-
941 folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-
942 validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This
943 step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training
944 set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the
945 target variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we evaluate
946 the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop

further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the
945 target variable through the computation of permutation scores. Finally, we evaluate
946 the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop
947 (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median
948 performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are
949 reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells,
950 Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients
951 into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the
952 cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those
953 with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

1026 were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206).

1027 Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was
1028 performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied
1029 BayesPrism (https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism), a deconvolution tool
1030 developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets 78. This
1031 method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles
1032 for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq
1033 counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx
1034 counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a
1035 patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due
1036 to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were
1037 grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240
1038 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403
1039 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs
1040 and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.

364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403
1039 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs
1040 and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient.
1041 Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with low-complexity genes
1042 filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’.

1043 We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) scores for
1044 the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-
1045 DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5,
1046 CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via
1047 the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between
1048 MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the
1049 estimated cell-type fractions (from BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple
1050 linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y
1051 ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.

1053 To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data based on MHCII
1054 UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing them into high, medium, and low
 79
1055 groups (as described in the scRNA-seq analysis). MultiNicheNetR


 33
1056 (https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git) analysis was performed to assess
1057 ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T
1058 cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
1059 comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples.

ormed to assess
1057 ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T
1058 cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
1059 comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the
1060 analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per
1061 type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression
1062 thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression
1063 fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was
1064 used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds
1065 of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
1066 receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high
1067 MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using
1068 the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor
1069 were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor
1070 deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments,
1071 as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma.
1072 Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing.

1078 complex purification was performed as previously described with minor

1080 mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel,

1083 buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors
1084 (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The
1085 resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and
1086 the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris.
1087 The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo
1088 Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-

1089 DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s
1090 coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was
1091 passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially
1092 washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl,
1093 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-
1094 HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the

ed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl,

1094 HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the
1095 resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides,
1096 along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters,
1097 WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic
1098 acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL
1099 of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials
1100 (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf)
1101 at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled
1102 into a single sample.
1103 Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical
1104 column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18;
1105 IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow
1106 rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent
1107 B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min,
1108 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient
1109 was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min,
1110 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS
1111 acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to
1112 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0 = 1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF
1113 mode.

1115 at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000,

1118 to 20 eV at 1/K0 = 0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested

1120 was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard
1121 (ThermoFischer Scientific).

1154 TCR repertoire analysis

1155 We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples
1156 used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16)
1157 originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples underwent RNA sequencing with
1158 Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero
1159 plus), aiming at >100 million read pairs at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data
1160 has been processed with Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped
1161 to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were
1162 extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725) 87, following the RNA-seq workflow
1163 (https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze
1164 rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality
1165 control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for
1166 T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded

y ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality
1165 control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for
1166 T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded
1167 identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and
1168 TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low
1169 group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly
1170 represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD
1171 and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R package
1172 (RRID:SCR_023089) 88. We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume"
1173 method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer
1174 proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII
1175 group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6
1176 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (3) Medium (the rest). Differences in
1177 clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the
1178 Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the
1179 Holm method.

 38
1215 Statistical analyses

1216 We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The difference in abundance
1217 between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test,
1218 unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different
1219 groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between
1220 variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted
1221 P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values
1222 (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

1462 a Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics tumor profiling.
1463 Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: BRCAloss,
1464 homologous recombination deficient (HRD-BRCAwt), homologous recombination
1465 proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification (CCNE1amp), using methylation assay,
1466 targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor
1467 microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026


 48
1468 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and
1469 extract single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline
1470 (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming
1471 to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles. Our results were
1472 validated using statistical methods, followed by further biological analysis for
1473 interpretation, both incorporating external cohorts. b Antibody panel employed for t-
1474 CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. c A bar plot displaying the composition of cancer

1479 composition of immune cells per tumor. The bar plot is sorted according to the CD11c+
1480 myeloid cell proportion. h UMAP projection of immune cells coloured by their
1481 annotation as in panel f. i t-CycIF image displaying the functional marker Ki67; this
1482 image corresponds to the one in panel e.

sorted according to the CD11c+

1554 Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a
1555 key prognostic feature in HGSC

1556 a A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined
1557 targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either
1558 BRCAloss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to
1559 the target (for example, BRCAloss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation
1560 iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding
1561 cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to
1562 the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the
1563 training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold
1564 cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target
1565 classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either BRCAloss or HRP tumors)
1566 and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of
1567 nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features

 51
1568 are identified. b-c Heatmap displaying the median accuracy (F1 score) of CEFIIRA
1569 models in predicting whether cells belong to tumors from short- or long-survival
1570 patients, across the indicated molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations
1571 (columns). The 95% confidence intervals for the F1 scores are shown in parentheses.

 The Multinichenetr predicted ligand-receptor interactions distinguishing high and
 low MHCII tumor expression in scRNA-seq dataset.
 Circos plots display ligand–receptor pairs prioritized by Multinichenetr – both immune–
 tumor and tumor–tumor cell interactions. Immune cells (CD8+ T cells, CD4+ T cells,
 FOXP3+ regulatory T cells, and dendritic cells (DC)) are shown in orange, while tumor
 cells are shown in yellow. Each plot displays the presence of ligand-receptor interactions,
 indicating whether they are associated with high or low cancer MHCII expression in the
 samples.

### word/document.xml
Dear 
Dr.
Michele Hartsough,
We are pleased to submit our manuscript, titled 
“
Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor ecosystems and clinical outcomes
,”
 for consideration in 
Cancer Discovery
.
This work presents a clinically and m
o
lecularly resolved single-cell spatial atlas of 280 metastatic ovarian cancers, integrating spatial proteomics, genomics, bulk, single-cell, and spatial transcriptomics to map the tumor-immune microenvironment (TME) at unprecedented scale and resolution. 
 As discussed in the email (25th June 2025), we have now included supporting functional data from our patient-derived organoid platform, and we would be grateful for your feedback on the manuscript. 
High-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGSC), the most lethal gynecologic malignancy, has shown limited responses to immunotherapy. Prior work by our group and others has demonstrated that tumor-immune interactions drive responses to chemotherapy (
Launonen 2021 Nat Commun
; 
Launonen 2024 Cancer Cell
) and immunotherapy combinations (
Färkkilä 2019 Nat Commun
). However, improving therapeutic strategies and patient stratification requires a comprehensive understanding of how the tumor ecosystem is orchestrated in HGSC.
In this study, we present a multi-omics, spatially resolved analysis of the TME in metastatic HGSC with a total number of 15.1 
million cells across multiomic datasets
. Using 4.6 million single cells across 929 high-dimensional maps, we identify distinct spatial domains that correspond to clinicomolecular subtypes and are predictive of clinical outcomes.

otal number of 15.1 
million cells across multiomic datasets
. Using 4.6 million single cells across 929 high-dimensional maps, we identify distinct spatial domains that correspond to clinicomolecular subtypes and are predictive of clinical outcomes. To uncover key features of these tumor ecosystems, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by Random Forest)—a versatile, open-source machine-learning pipeline. This tool robustly identifies clinically relevant features, such as tumor-intrinsic MHC-II expression, which we found to be a critical driver of prolonged survival. Tumor-intrinsic MHC-II also mediates immune infiltration
, 
antigen presentation, and responses to 
immunotherapy
in vitro
, providing novel insights into the immune surveillance mechanisms across HGSC subtypes.
Key Novelties and Impact of Our Work:
Unprecedented Scale and Resolution of the HGSC Single-cell Spatial Atlas:
We present a uniquely large, clinically annotated, multi-omics dataset of 280 HGSC tumors, integrating genomics, transcriptomics, and spatial imaging with comprehensive clinical data.
Publicly available, clinically and molecularly annotated single-cell spatial dataset. We will make the annotated single-cell spatial data with molecular profiles, therapy sequences, and clinical survival data publicly available. This will be an extremely valuable resource to the research community for additional hypothesis generation and exploration.
Our manuscript provides an unparalleled resource and conceptual framework for understanding tumor-immune interactions in HGSC. We believe that our findings will significantly advance efforts in biomarker discovery, therapeutic development, and patient stratification, with broad implications for cancer research.

framework for understanding tumor-immune interactions in HGSC. We believe that our findings will significantly advance efforts in biomarker discovery, therapeutic development, and patient stratification, with broad implications for cancer research.
Development of the CEFIIRA Machine-Learning Pipeline:
CEFIIRA
 is a robust and generalizable machine-learning tool that identifies spatial and molecular features critical for tumor-immune interactions. It incorporates cross-validation, feature elimination, and feature permutation across iterative training and testing cycles to minimize bias and overfitting.
As open-source software, CEFIIRA enables the integration of multi-level datasets, creating a scalable tool for cancer research and other diseases.
Improved Understanding and Clinical Relevance of TME spatial structures in HGSC.
In addition to tumor compositions and immune cell infiltration, we present a systematic analysis of the tumor spatial domains in HGSC. 
We show that, particularly, the tumor-stroma interface composition is associated with distinct clinical outcomes. 
Our work also further highlights the impact of the tumor molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure on the spatial and functional TME. Thus, our systematic assessment 
of
 our multiomics dataset brings important considerations into future TME analyses in HGSC.
Discovery of Tumor-Intrinsic MHC-II as a Critical Biomarker:
We demonstrate that spatially heterogeneous, tumor-intrinsic MHC-II expression associates with clinical outcomes in HGSC, independently of the clinicomolecular subtypes.
Using our two distinct spatial transcriptomics datasets, we show that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is associated with local immune infiltration and activation.

iates with clinical outcomes in HGSC, independently of the clinicomolecular subtypes.
Using our two distinct spatial transcriptomics datasets, we show that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is associated with local immune infiltration and activation.
Using mass spectrometry immunopeptidomics, we show that MHCII binds to specific immunopeptides in clinical tumors. This, together with increased TCR clonality associated with tumor-intrinsic MHCII, highlights a role for the local tumor microenvironment niches
in the overall immunogenicity in HGSC.
Using a functional patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression associates with enhanced cytotoxicity of CD8+ T cells after immune checkpoint inhibition.
Altogether, our findings have significant clinical implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts overall tumor immunogenicity, and guide the development of alternative therapeutic approaches for MHC-II–negative HGSCs.
Thank you for considering our manuscript for publication. We look forward to the opportunity to contribute to 
Cancer Discovery
.
Yours sincerely,
Anniina Färkkilä

### word/document.xml
Title (limit of 165 characters including spaces) - 
Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor ecosystems and clinical outcomes
 (152 characters)
Running title (limit of 60 characters including spaces) 
MHCII predicts spatial immune activation in HGSC (62/60 characters)
MHCII predicts immune activation and clinical outcomes
Unstructured Abstract
The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. 
Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality.
 Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.

Statement of significance (a one- or two-sentence summary of the manuscript’s salient findings)
We present a multi-omics, spatially resolved atlas of the TME in metastatic HGSC, profiling 15.1 million cells to identify spatial domains linked to clinicomolecular subtypes and clinical outcomes. Using our pipeline CEFIIRA, we uncover key features such as tumor-intrinsic MHC-II expression, a driver of prolonged survival that mediates immune infiltration, antigen presentation, and immunotherapy responses in HGSC.

Abstract
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex network of interactions between malignant and host cells, yet its orchestration in advanced 
high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma
 (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 
280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imagin
g and molecular profiling.
 Using 1026
 single-cell maps, 
we identify distinct spatial domains associated with phenotypically heterogeneous cellular compositions, and demonstrate that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly influences clinical outcomes.
 To uncover the key features of the tumor ecosystem, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), which identified tumor cell-intrinsic MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival, independent of clinical covariates and molecular profiles. 
Through validation using external single-cell spatial data and two spatial transcriptomics datasets, we demonstrate that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is associated with local immune activation and antigen presentation. Moreover, the presence of MHCII-specific immunopeptides and increased TCR clonality further support the role of local tumor microenvironment niches in immunogenicity of HGSC.
 Finally, using a functional patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression associates with enhanced cytotoxicity of CD8+ T cells after immunotherapy.
In conclusion, 
o
ur atlas offers novel insights into immune 
activation,
 paving the way for improved patient stratification
 in HGSC
.
INTRODUCTION

INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 
1
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5
and significant tumor heterogeneity 
6
. This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 
7
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with
BRCA1/2
mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte i
nfiltration
8,9
. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 
10
and unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies 
11,12
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
13–15
,
resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
16
.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
reover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
13–15
,
resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
16
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (
clinicomolecular
) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas
 capturing
 15.1 million single
cells from
 265 
advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multi
omic
 molecular profiling 
l
everaging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling
. 
Using 
a tailored
 image analysis 
approach
, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA 
(Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest)
, to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover 
clinically relevant
 features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (
MHCII
) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune
infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping
 of 
HGSC tumors
To

RESULTS
Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping
 of 
HGSC tumors
To 
uncover
 the relationship between the 
TME ecosystem
 topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied 
multiomic
 profiling including 
targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling 
combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 
2
65 advanced HGSCs
(
Fig. 1a
)
. The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=
100
) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=
158
). The therapy sequence (PDS/IDS) was unknown for seven patients. Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, 
with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months)
(
S.
 Table 1
). 
M
ost
 patients (87.5%) received carboplatin and paclitaxel as adjuvant
chemotherapy
, and the median progression-free survival was 16 months (IQR: 12-27 months). 
We conducted a hierarchical mul
tidimensional
 analysis to assign the tumors’ molecular profiles.
Firstly, 
BRCA1/2
 germline or somatic mutation status was assessed
 using targeted 
high-throughput
 sequencing, and 
BRCA1
 promoter hypermethylation was evaluated using a targeted DNA methylation assay. 
Tumors without 
BRCA
loss (
BRCA1/2
 somatic or germline mutation, or 
BRCA1
 promoter hypermethylation) were tested for Homologous Recombination Deficiency (HRD) and 
CCNE1
-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and 
dedicated
 algorithms (see Methods).

-amplification using shallow whole genome sequencing and 
dedicated
 algorithms (see Methods). 
We categorized 
174
 tumors (
S.
 Fig. 1a) 
into four clinically and molecularly relevant groups (
BRCAloss, HRD, Homologous Recombination Proficient - HRP, and CCNE1 amplification
) 
17
. The rest (n=91) of the tumors were left “unassigned” as they
 could not be reliably categorized into the predefined molecular profiles (see Methods). 
To
precisely map the
 spatial TME at a single-cell resolution, we used hyperplexed imaging with tissue cyclic immunofluorescence 
(
t-CycIF
)
 targeting 30 different proteins and cell nuclei employing 
tissue microarrays (
TMAs) (
Fig. 1
b, 
S.
 Table 2
).
I
mage 
processing
 and quality filtering
