lated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing.
Immunopeptidomics
We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi).
 MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
81
with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx::chunk_0057
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter,
1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx::chunk_0058
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm
−2
 to 0.64 Vs cm
−2 
in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm
−2
 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm
−2
. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFischer Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed 
using the FragPipe
 computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864
, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger 
82
 was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx::chunk_0059
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher 
83
, applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant 
84
, enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides
’
 searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) 
85
 with default settings. 
The threshold
 for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the 
t
hreshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using ArcasHLA 
(RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant
) 
86
with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis, peptides were mapped to 
significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
 antigen genes from TCGA
, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset),
 and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12) and low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) groups.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset),
 and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12) and low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using 
Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)
87
 (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 
10 top-enriched
 terms were recorded
.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples 
underwent 
RNA sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at >100 million read pairs at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725) 
88
, following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx::chunk_0061
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/OLD VERSION Perez et al - Summer 2025_.docx
vector_chunks.text:
used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the 
immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089) 
89
. We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB). Differences in clone numbers between groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.
iPDCs flow cytometry analysis
The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al., 2024 
16
. 
Briefly, tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an omentum-derived matrix 
90
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
.

vector_chunks.text:
ved matrix 
90
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
.
In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S. Fig. 6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) 
(RRID:SCR_008520) 
and custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer and survival. For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing 
(n=233, 
S. Fig. 1b
) 
were selected for survival analysis when 
assessing
 the 
association
 of 
cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes
.

vector_chunks.text:
cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing 
(n=233, 
S. Fig. 1b
) 
were selected for survival analysis when 
assessing
 the 
association
 of 
cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes
. 
For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). 
Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The 
difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. 
The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test.
 The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. 
P-
values
 or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

FIGURE LEGENDS
Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.
a
Study schematics
. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using 
multi-omics
tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: 
BRCA
loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-
BRCA
wt), homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification 
(
CCNE1amp
)
, using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract 
single-cell 
spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation, both incorporating external cohorts. 
b 
Antibody panel employed for t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers. 
c
A
bar plot
displaying the composition of cancer cells, stromal cells, myeloid cells, lymphocytes, and unassigned cells per tumor. The
 bar plot
 is sorted by cancer cell abundance. 
d
UMAP

Figure 2. Coordinated immune infiltration, cancer-phenotype patterns and spatial TME domains associate with distinct clinicomolecular features 
a 
Box plots
 for the median immune infiltration per tumor (across the sampled cores) grouped by molecular profiles. Only tumors sampled with at least two cores are included. In the boxplots, black horizontal lines represent the median, the boxes extend from the first to the third quartile, and the whiskers indicate the values at 1.5 times the interquartile range. Outliers are shown as dots. The differences in proportion were calculated using a two-tailed Wilcoxon rank-sum test. The notation for unadjusted P-values is as follows: *P<=0.05, **P<=0.01. 
b 
Heatmap displaying the Pearson correlation of median immune cell infiltration across the tumors (inter-tumours). Analysis was done for tumors sampled with more than one core after QC. Median i
nfiltration per tumor was calculated across the sampled cores.
 Only pairwise significant (FDR<0.05) correlations are shown. Correlation values are reported in S. Table 4 and FDRs in S. Table 5. 
c 
Forest plot of the Cox regression of Overall Survival (OS) in relation to the median abundance of the indicated immune cells per tumor. The regressions were adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and molecular profile and were performed separately for PDS and IDS samples. Analysis done for tumors sampled with more than one core after QC and with associated clinical information 
(S. Fig. 1b).

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
A
 schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, 
predicting
 if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (
for
 example
, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, 
for 
each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes 
(
for
 example,
 whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.
 b-c
Heatmap

BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.

Abstract
The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression correlates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after immunotherapy. Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 
1
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0003

vector_chunks.text:
to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer 
2,
3
, characterized by high genomic instability 
4,5
and significant tumor heterogeneity 
6
. This heterogeneity is evident in various aspects, such as histopathological architecture, genetic alterations, and TME cellular composition, leading to varying responses to chemotherapy 
7
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with
BRCA1/2
mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte i
nfiltration
8,9
. Emerging evidence also suggests that HR-deficient (HRD) tumors possess distinct immune escape mechanisms 
10
and unique responses to combinations with immunotherapies 
11,12
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
13–15
,
resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
16
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (
clinicomolecular
) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas
 capturing
 15.1 million single
cells from
 280 
advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multi
omic
 molecular profiling 
l
everaging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling
. 
Using 
a tailored
 image analysis 
approach

Using 
a tailored

approach
, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA 
(Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest)
, to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover 
clinically relevant
 features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (
MHCII
) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune
infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, impacting prognosis independently of 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

Spatial TME 
domains

To map the spatial TME domains, 
we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods).
We uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN) (
Fig. 2e to g
). Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01 to 
RCN

RCN

 and RCN10)

domains

). 
The cancer spatial domains included

Spatial composition analysis revealed that

The cancer cell domains also revealed significant correlations with other spatial domains. 
For instance, the neighborhood of cancer idle phenotype (RCN01) was positively correlated with the immune-cold tumor-stromal interface (TSI), whereas the neighborhood of cancer cells displaying immunogenic phenotypes was negatively correlated with the immune-cold stroma (RCN01) (
Fig. 2h
). These findings suggest that the spatial domains of the TME are spatially

Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant in the IDS samples

These results

Conversely, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) had significantly more active cancer phenotypes, with enriched proliferative, immunogenic and EMT phenotypes suggestive of adaptive “immune escape” mechanisms at the TSI

hese findings 
suggest a role

In summary, t
hese findings 
suggest a role 
for the

emerged as
 the most diverse spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial organization, and 
clinicomolecular

CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features
To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest),
 a robust and generalized
machine learning (ML) 
framework (
Fig. 3a
). The primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. 
CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by:

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
EFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. 
CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 
1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) 
to achieve a consensus estimation. Additionally
, our 
versatile
, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multi
dimensional
 data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles (
Fig. 3a
).
We aggregated
 the multidimensional
 spatial data for each of the
 4,644,485
 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (
Fig. 1e, i; 
S.
 Fig. 1g
), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (
Fig. 1j
), resulting in 43
 feature representations per cell (Materials and Methods). 
To uncover multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, 
we stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS
 (
S.
 Fig. 3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for 
either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1

ied the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS
 (

 Fig. 3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for 
either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1
amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. As expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (
S.
 Fig. 3c,d
). 
The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both PDS (
Fig. 3b, S
. Table 8
) and IDS (
Fig. 3c
, S. Table 8
), 
indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell spatial data. 
Cancer cell intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression is a key feature associated with of overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, 
CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic M
HC
II as one of the key

and IDS

S.
 Fig. 3c,d
).
Notably, of the

cancer cell-
specific 
MHCII
expressio
n showed the highest fold-change enrichment
 in HRP tumors from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients
(
Fig. 
3d,

; 
S. Table 8

 By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (

We next aimed to characterize the heterogeneity of MHCII expression both across and within tumors. We observed that 17% of cancer cells exhibited MHCII expression levels comparable to or exceeding those of CD11c+MY cells (
S. Fig. 3i
). Tumors showed variable proportions of MHCII+ cancer cells (
Fig. 3j
), with these proportions positively correlated with the proportion of MHCI+ cancer cells (R = 0.45, p = 1.7e-13; 
S. Fig. 3j
). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern, as confirmed through t-CycIF image analysis (
S. Fig. 3k, l
). This suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.
To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the multi
omics 
cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS (
Fig.

k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. 
To further corroborate the prognostic 
role of 
MHCII
 expression by cancer cells

5
, by inferring the 
cancer-cell-specific
MHCII
 expression
 from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM 
26
.

To investigate the effect of cancer-intrinsic

revealed a

 and M1-like macrophages (CD11c+
.MY
)

Fig. 
4
a
). To corroborate these findings, we tested the association between MHCII expression in cancer cells and the estimated proportions of immune cells using deconvoluted data from TCGA (CIBERSORT, see Methods). We found 
that tumors with high 
MHCII

S.
 Fig. 
4
a
). In contrast, tumors with 
high

4

Overall, these results demonstrate that 
HGSC

To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and underlying t
ranscriptional programs

comprised 136,452 cells, which included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and 97,409 immune cells, 
annotated

see Methods

 The proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity (

We observed that

(

c to e

Similar results were observed at the patient level taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations (

 Altogether, these findings suggest that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.
Cancer cell 
MHCII

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic

This indicates that MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the cancer cell spatial compartments.
We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell 
MHCII

MHCII

Consistently, analysis of

II- cancer cells

To expand our analyses to

In line with previous findings, we observed heterogeneity of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression within and across the tumors

ore neighboring CD4+ T, CD8+ T, and CD11C+ antigen presenting Myeloid cells as compared to the MHCII- cells, generating spatial immune hotspots (

This additional single-cell spatial analysis further highlights that MHCII+ cancer cells colocalize in immunoreactive spatial domains, enriched in T-cells and antigen presenting cells.
To further validate the association between MHCII expression by cancer cells and enhanced immune proximity, we conducted spatial transcriptomics (GeoMx) analysis of the

Consistently, a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (

Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with interferon-gamma (IFN-γ) activation

To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously available spatial transcriptomic dataset

and similar trends were observed at the patient level, although statistical significance was not reached due to the smaller sample size (

,

.
 Interestingly, in our

omics

,

 Overall, these findings reinforce the link between cancer
-
intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of cancer cell 
MHCII
 in spatial immune activation. 
To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict ligand–receptor pairs (
Fig. 5g, Ext. material - Fig.4
). We identified the top seven ligand–receptor interactions across CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (
Fig. 5h
). Interestingly, IFN signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8⁺ and CD4⁺ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed 
TGF-β1 interactions across the cell populations
, which may indicate the induction of immune tolerance 
45
. Similarly, in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (
Fig. 5i
), we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL, IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (
Fig. 5
j
). In addition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig. 5j
).

ition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig. 5j
). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.
Cancer
MHCII
 is associated with distinct antigen presentation
, increased TCR diversity and enhanced immunotherapy response 
in vitro
Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins 
46
. 
To 
explore 
whether cancer-specific 
MHCII
 expression is linked with
 specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously 
identified
 antigens 
detected via
HLA ligandome analysis
 in HGSC 
47
(
Fig. 
6a
). We found that the deconvoluted expression 
of 
MHCII
 in cancer 
cells is indeed
associated 
with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in the TCGA dataset (
Fig. 
6b
). However, in our 
scRNA-seq
 dataset, only four specific antigen genes (
UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN
) were expressed in the cancer cells, and 
associated 
with tumor 
MHCII
 expression (
Fig. 
6c
). 
In our GeoM
x
 spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also obser
ved positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (
Fig. 6d
).
To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC,

r
ved positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (
Fig. 6d
).
To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC, 
we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples alongside TCR repertoire analysis based on bulk RNA-seq (
Fig. 6e
). We identified the presence of previously reported genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets that showed significant correlations and were consistently observed across the patients (
Fig. 6f
). 
Moreover, the genes enriched in samples
 with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells were significantly enriched in cellular components related to junctions, adhesion, the extracellular matrix, and cell–cell contact zones, as well as in biological processes involved in protein localization and regulation (
Fig. 6g,h
). To explore the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We observed a significantly increased number of T cell receptor clones—specifically TRAD and TRB —in samples with a high MHCII cancer proportion (
Fig. 6i
).
Given this association with enhanced T cell presence, we next investigated whether tumor MHCII status influences CD8+
T-cell responses to immunotherapy using our previously established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) 
16
. For this, 
we analysed 
flow cytometry data from 
drug perturbation assays
 of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five chemo-treated tumor samples (
Fig. 6j
). The iPDCs 
were stratified into MHCII-high
and MHCII-low

ne-competent cultures (iPDCs) 
16
. For this, 
we analysed 
flow cytometry data from 
drug perturbation assays
 of iPDCs from five chemo-naïve and five chemo-treated tumor samples (
Fig. 6j
). The iPDCs 
were stratified into MHCII-high
and MHCII-low
groups based on baseline HLA-DR expression in EpCAM+ cancer cells compared to immune and myeloid populations (see 
Methods
 and 
S. Fig. 6a,b
). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited significantly greater CD8+ T-cell cytotoxic responses to pembrolizumab
(
Fig. 6k, S. Table 11
). Sample-by-sample analysis revealed a statistically significant cytotoxicity gain (p < 0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated conditions in the MHCII-high group (
Fig. 6l
). 
Together, t
hese 
findings
 suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via 
MHCII
 is an active process with the presentation of 
distinct
 antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC
. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context of PD-1 immune checkpoint blockade.
DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-o
mic 
spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 
1026
 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 2
80
 real-world clinical samples. Our spatial analysis approach identified distinct spatial domains with varying phenotypic compositions. 
These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic profiles and 
clinicomolecular
 groups, underscoring the role of molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies
.

vector_chunks.text:
ains with varying phenotypic compositions. 
These intercorrelated domains were linked to specific tumor cell phenotypic profiles and 
clinicomolecular
 groups, underscoring the role of molecular features and chemotherapy in shaping TME topographies
. Notably, of the two distinct TSI phenotypes, the immune-infiltrated TSI was associated with cancer cell phenotypic heterogeneity and improved outcomes, while the immune-cold TSI displayed stromal predominance and was linked to poorer outcomes. 
Using
 our machine learning framework 
CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered 
MHCII
 as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. 
Through spatial profiling of
 ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting 
cells.
 Interestingly, 
CD4+ T cells
 have been found
 to
 recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance 
48,49
, and 
the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells
 was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 
50
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 
51
. 
Through a
 robust spatial TME 
analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell 
MHCII
 correlated with IFN produced by 
antigen-experienced

ysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell 
MHCII
 correlated with IFN produced by 
antigen-experienced
 CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
expression are 
spatially 
correlated, and demonstrated that cancer
-
intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized
 in 
Fig. 
7
, support the hypothesis that antigen 
presentation is linked to activated
 CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI 
associated
 with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
52
. In 
HGSC,
 B
ur
dett et al recently reported lower MHCII expres
sion in HGSCs undergoing whole-genome duplication, a characteristic associated with poor prognosis 
53
.
 Moreover, the presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in melanoma, lung cancer, and triple-negative breast cancer 
41,50,54,55
. 
However
, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest benefits 
56
. In contrast, o
ur 
in vitro 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs
 suggests that st
ratification of patients based on 
MHCII expression might 
enhance

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0032
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
elected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated only modest benefits 
56
. In contrast, o
ur 
in vitro 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs
 suggests that st
ratification of patients based on 
MHCII expression might 
enhance
 the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by priming cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples
.
 In addition
, o
ur study revealed that other immune checkpoint inhibitors, 
such as TIGIT
and CTLA4
, were overexpressed in tumors with high 
MHCII
 expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of immune checkpoint inhibitors beyond PD-1/PDL-1 in selected patients with HGSC 
16
. 
Moreover, we showed that distinct immunopeptides are expressed in MHCII-expressing tumors, and presented by MHCII, which associates with higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. 
While tumor-intrinsic 
MHCII
 expression indicates a functional tumor-immunity cycle via active antigen presentation, other therapeutic approaches should be developed for patients with tumors lacking 
MHCII
 expression. 
In conclusion, 
our single-cell spatial atlas 
uncovers potential
 mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 
clinicomolecular
 subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. The retrospective design and reliance on archival tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting 
some of the statistical comparisons between patients
. Our protein marker panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all 
potential

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
l tissues may introduce biases related to sample preservation and heterogeneity, limiting 
some of the statistical comparisons between patients
. Our protein marker panel, while comprehensive, has inherent limitations in representing all 
potential 
cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as 
to have
 matched single-cell 
proteomic
 and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. 
Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient cohorts.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and f
ormalin-fixed
, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA) 
57
.
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking 
surgeries 
(PDS or IDS). 
The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0038
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA) 
57
.
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking 
surgeries 
(PDS or IDS). 
The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV, HMH
). 
Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). 
FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores 
targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from
 stromal-rich areas, 
and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as previously described 
57
and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with 
t-CycIF
.
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). 
The samples were obtained from patients during interval debulking surgeries (IDS). 
The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R 
> 0cm
) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.
Molecular profile assignment
For the TMA cohort, we 
first 
identified 
BRCA1/2 
mutation and 
BRCA1
promoter 
methylation status of each sample as previously described 
57
. 
In brief, 
BRCA1/2

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0039
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.
Molecular profile assignment
For the TMA cohort, we 
first 
identified 
BRCA1/2 
mutation and 
BRCA1
promoter 
methylation status of each sample as previously described 
57
. 
In brief, 
BRCA1/2 
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a 
BRCA1/2
 germline mutation, both 
BRCA1/2
 somatic mutations and 
BRCA1
promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using 
BRCA MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and 
Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the number of tumors with 
BRCA1 
or 
BRCA2 
mutations and 
BRCA1
 promoter methylation are reported in 
S. Table 1
.
Next, tumors without a 
BRCA1/2
 mutation 
or 
BRCA1
promoter
 hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using Illumina HiSeq2500 to determine a 
BRCA
-like
CNV profile 
58
 and 
CCNE1
-amplification status as described previously 
57
. In addition, using the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software shallowHRD 
59
. Finally, we categorized the tumors as 
BRCAloss
, HRD, HRP
, and 
CCNE1
amp using a hierarchical approach described in 
S.
 Fig
.
 1a
.
Samples that failed QC for 
BRCA1/2
 targeted DNA-sequencing, 
BRCA1
 promoter hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as “Unassigned” (or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the 
BRCA-like
 CNV profile 
58
 were labeled as “Unassigned”.
For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0040
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
, were labeled as “Unassigned” (or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the 
BRCA-like
 CNV profile 
58
 were labeled as “Unassigned”.
For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar 
(RRID:SCR_027138) 
60
. For all analyses, only tumors with a 
TP53
 mutation, histological grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were included. 
For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically validated HRD classification performed at the Helsinki University Hospital, Finland, which uses ovaHRDscar, and the detection of somatic and germline mutations and gene deletions in 
BRCA1/2
 genes.
Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface,
 resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 
30-plex
marker panel 
(
S.
 Table 2
)
 and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the 
t-CycIF
 protocol 
61
. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The 
images’
 illumination was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371) 
62
. The per-image tiles were stitched and registered using 
ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266, 
https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar
). Individual TMA’s cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using 
Ilastik (RRID:SCR_015246)
 v1.3.2 
63
 and
Cellprofiler
 (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 
64

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0041
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ped using the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using 
Ilastik (RRID:SCR_015246)
 v1.3.2 
63
 and
Cellprofiler
 (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 
64
 using 2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness were quantified using 
MATLAB. Quality filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles (
https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite
), 
image regions with artifacts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells. 
For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex validated antibody panel (
S.
 Table 
12
)
 and 
scanned with a 
RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the 
t-CycIF
 protocol. The image tiles were stitched with ASHLAR.
 For WSI, 
we developed
a
 more scalable
 analysis pipeline (
https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing
). Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei segmentation in StarDist 
65
,
implementing the pre-trained model ‘
2D_versatile_flu
o''. Then, the mean intensity of each channel was quantified by our in-house script based on skimage package 
66
. Image quality control was performed by 
CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157)
67
.
Cell 
phenotyping
For the TMAs
’
 cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package 
mclust (RRID:SCR_027153)
.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package 
mclust (RRID:SCR_027153)
. Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. 
Subsequently
, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel's signal was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package 
FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899)
. For each SOM node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (
S.
 Fig. 1
c
) by using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune cells), and then 
to annotate
 immune subtypes (
S.
 Fig. 1
c
). All the cell 
phenotype
assignments
 were validated via visual inspection using 
Napari (RRID:SCR_022765)
. After annotation, we quantified cell population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion for each patient’s sample. 
For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using 
unsupervised clustering
 with the software 
TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367) 
18
. Outliers were removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity, normalized via a
rcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0043
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
type annotation was performed using 
unsupervised clustering
 with the software 
TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367) 
18
. Outliers were removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity, normalized via a
rcsin transformation with a cofactor of 5 
68
, and scaled by Z-score. 
TRIBUS
 was run with the following parameter settings of 
sigma=1
, 
learning_rate=1
, 
clustering_threshold=100
, 
undefined_threshold=0.0005
, 
other_threshold=0.4
. A total of 6 cell types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+ T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table (
S.
 Table 
13
)
 for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are images S100_iOme and S110_iOme, which were manually gated with 
scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) 
69
 due to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, showing agreement across samples (
S.
 Fig. 
4l
). The MHCII+ cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the annotations to the 
t-CycIF
 images.
Cancer cell phenotype clustering
Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method section. We perform dimensionality reduction
 o
n
 the
 cancer cells 
based on the expression levels of the markers
: "Ki67", "Vimentin", "pSTAT1", "MHC-I", "ECadherin", "yH2AX", "pTBK1", "cPARP1", "CK7", "PDL1_488", and morphological features: "Area", "Eccentricity", and "Roundness" into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package flowSOM 
(RRID:SCR_016899)
. The SOM-nodes were clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
", and morphological features: "Area", "Eccentricity", and "Roundness" into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package flowSOM 
(RRID:SCR_016899)
. The SOM-nodes were clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954). For this, the number of clusters (
k
) was assessed from 2 to 12. For each value of 
k
 assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm “partitioning around medoids” (pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, 
k
, by assessing the resultant consensus-clustering matrix for each value of 
k
 and its cumulative density distribution 
(
Ext. material - Fig. 3
)
. The cancer meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers in each one 
(
Ext. material - Fig. 
2
)
.
Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis
For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that pas
sed quality control
, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30
µm, using
 the function tl.spatial_count of the package 
s
cimap 
(RRID:SCR_024751)
69
.
 We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k
=
4
0 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package 
s
cimap. Every cell was 
assigned
to only one type of RCN. 
To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the 
Python package YellowBrick 
70
(
Ext. material - Fig. 3
)
. 
We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent 
neighborhoods of
 cells that are in physical contact.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0045
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
type of RCN. 
To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the 
Python package YellowBrick 
70
(
Ext. material - Fig. 3
)
. 
We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent 
neighborhoods of
 cells that are in physical contact. 
The replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (
Ext. material - Fig. 3
).
The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.
Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis
To perform the analysis of the 
MHCII
 signature
 activity in the proximity of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data, we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices in h5 format, using the 
Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341) 
71
. For the QC, we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat's SCTransform function. 
Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an expert pathologist as ‘Cancer’, ‘TSI’ (tumor-stroma interface with partial tumor and stromal morphology), ‘Immune-Stromal’ and ‘other’. Spots annotated as ‘other’ have been excluded from the analysis. Within ‘Immune-Stromal’ spots, we have distinguished an additional type - ‘TSI2’, defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of 
MHCII-signature

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ded from the analysis. Within ‘Immune-Stromal’ spots, we have distinguished an additional type - ‘TSI2’, defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of 
MHCII-signature
 across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) 
44
. The 
MHCII
 signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor response was adopted from Böhm
 et al, 20
15 
72
. An IFN-γ signature, obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene set collection (RRID:SCR_016863) 
73
, was also quantified per spot. Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided Wilcoxon Rank Sum test. 
Machine learning supervised classification
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: (1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); (2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; (3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type
; additionally when the
 target for prediction was clinical outcome
s like short- or long-OS the model included
 patients’ clinical information 
–
 FIGO stage and 
a
ge
 at diagnosis.

f neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type
; additionally when the
 target for prediction was clinical outcome
s like short- or long-OS the model included
 patients’ clinical information 
–
 FIGO stage and 
a
ge
 at diagnosis.
For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp. Based on PyCaret runs, we selected Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance (weighted-averaged AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (
S. Table 14
). In addition, we utilized the PyCaret RF model to determine the number of classes that lead to better prediction (weighted-average AUC and F1) using the same pre-processed single-cell dataset, running independent models for four, three and two classes using ten-fold cross-validations. Based on the results, we opted to further construct models for two classes (
S. Table 15
).
Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
 groups (

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx

e opted to further construct models for two classes (
S. Table 15
).
Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) 
74
 for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
 groups (
https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA
). The models' training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling
mentioned 
previously
. 
W
e chose 30 
data partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) 
HRDs
 tumor samples (
S. Fig. 3n
).
For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
).
The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluate
s the
 combinations of 
hyperparameters’ values
 to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores.

accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of permutation scores. 
Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models 
across
 30 nested cross-validations 
are
 reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 
66th
 percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 
33rd
 percentile), independently for those with 
HRPs and HRDs tumors
. 
Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile
For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from 
The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)
 (RRID:SCR_003193) 
5
. 
The deconvoluted cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with 
PRISM and adopted from the
previous work 
26
.
 The inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT were obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository 
75
.
RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment
For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read counts estimated with the software 
STAR (RRID:SCR_004463) 
76
 from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC)
. The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository 
75
. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of 
HLA_DPB1

(RRID:SCR_004463) 
76
 from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC)
. The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository 
75
. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of 
HLA_DPB1
 in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33) expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) 
31
for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB v19.0 
32
, 
we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485). 
For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=2
02),
w
e first stratified the tumors into three 
equal-sized
 groups according to the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the 
t-CycIF
 dataset. We assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest (below quantile 0.33, N=6
7
) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells.

nalysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells.
 Genes were considered with differential expression according to the 
following
 criteria: Log2-fold change >=1 or 
Log2-fold change <=1 and
 FDR<1^-3. Overexpr
essed genes are reported in

.
scRNA-seq analysis
We retrieved scRNA-seq gene count matrices from 51 HGSC samples, pre-processed and annotated 
23
, from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis, 
resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from 27 patients.
 We assessed the activity of MHCII signature across cell types by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1 
44
. The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: 
HLA-DMA
, 
HLA-DMB
, 
HLA-DOA
, 
HLA-DOB
, 
HLA-DPA1
, 
HLA-DPB1
, 
HLA-DQA1
, 
HLA-DQA2
, 
HLA-DQB1
, 
HLA-DRA
, 
HLA-DRB1
, 
HLA-DRB5
, 
CD74.
Based on the MHCII Ucell scores, the cells were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). 
For each 
sample 
and 
cell type
, pseudo-bulk 
gene expression profiles were generated using the 
function ‘
AggregateExpression’
 from the 
Seurat package (RRID:SCR_016341) v4.3.0 
77
. A
ccording to the 
pseudo-bulk
 MHCII Ucell signature in cancer cells, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low, according to tertile distribution (
S. Fig. 4c
).
