Seurat package (RRID:SCR_016341) v4.3.0 
77
. A
ccording to the 
pseudo-bulk
 MHCII Ucell signature in cancer cells, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low, according to tertile distribution (
S. Fig. 4c
).
 To assess the correlation between cancer-specific MHCII expression and IFN-γ in immune populations, pseudo-bulk expression of the 
IFNG
gene was computed for each cell type per sample. Only immune cells were included in the correlation analysis, as they exhibited higher 
IFNG
 expression compared to fibroblasts or epithelial/cancer cells (
S. Fig. 5l
). Pearson’s correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943). Patient information was added to the design matrix and regressed out from the DEG analysis.
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis 
We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy. Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 
16
, and is publicly available at
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
. 
The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence.
 The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0053
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ased on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence.
 The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) 
31
, followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the 
Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206)
. 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied 
BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
)
, a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets 
78
.
 This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard 
BayesPrism
 workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0054
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
g ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard 
BayesPrism
 workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) scores for the 
MHCII
 gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the 
GSVA
R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from 
BayesPrism
) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.
Ligand-receptor analysis
To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq analysis). MultiNicheNetR 
79
 (
https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git
) 
analysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells 
(all possible directions),
 comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0055
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
d tumor cells 
(all possible directions),
 comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. 
The resulting prioritized ligand–receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions
. These candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We correlated using 
Pearson correlation
 the previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing.
Immunopeptidomics
We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi).
 MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
80
with minor modifications.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0056
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi).
 MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
80
with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0057
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
: (1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; (3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and (4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter,
1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm
−2
 to 0.64 Vs cm
−2 
in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0058
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
S acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm
−2
 to 0.64 Vs cm
−2 
in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm
−2
 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm
−2
. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFischer Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed 
using the FragPipe
 computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864
, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger 
81
 was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher 
82

, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher 
82
, applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant 
83
, enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides
’
 searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) 
84
 with default settings. 
The threshold
 for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the 
t
hreshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using ArcasHLA 
(RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant
) 
85
with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis, peptides were mapped to 
significantly correlated with MHCII tumor
 antigen genes from TCGA
, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset),
 and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CyCIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12)
, 
low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) a
nd medium (the rest of patients) 
groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups.

, 8, 11, 12)
, 
low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) a
nd medium (the rest of patients) 
groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using 
Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575)
86
 (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 
10 top-enriched
 terms were recorded
.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study. Tumor samples 
underwent 
RNA sequencing with Illumina NovaSeq 6000 using the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (with Ribo-Zero plus), aiming at >100 million read pairs at 2x101 bp read length. RNA sequencing data has been processed with Illumina Dragen. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725) 
87
, following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0061
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the 
immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089) 
88
. We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): (1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); (3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.
iPDCs flow cytometry analysis
The dataset used for this analysis was generated in Launonen et al., 2024 
16
. 
Briefly, tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an omentum-derived matrix 
89
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at 
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
.
In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S. Fig. 6
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0062
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
y stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S. Fig. 6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) 
(RRID:SCR_008520) 
and custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing 
(n=233, 
S. Fig. 1b
) 
were selected for survival analysis when 
assessing
 the 
association
 of 
cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes
. 
For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). 
Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0063
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S. Fig. 1b
). 
Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3. The 
difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. 
The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test.
 The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. 
P-
values
 or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

, using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract 
single-cell 
spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation, both incorporating external cohorts.

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
A
 schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, 
predicting
 if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (
for
 example
, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx::chunk_0079
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Perez et al - Summer 2025.docx
vector_chunks.text:
for example, 
predicting
 if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (
for
 example
, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, 
for 
each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes 
(
for
 example,
 whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.

 displaying the median accuracy (F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95% confidence intervals for the F1 scores are shown in parentheses. The results corresponding 
to
 PDS samples are in panel 
b, 
and in panel 
c 
for IDS samples.

led 
marker intensity between cells from short- and long-survival patients with HRP tumors. The markers displayed correspond to the top five most important features for each model (excluding clinical variables). Only markers that were statistically different between short- or long-survival groups are shown (Wilcoxon rank sum test, unadjusted p < 0.05).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0002
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ki University Hospital, Helsinki, Finland. 
22 
Lead contact.
Abstract
The tumor microenvironment is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) remains poorly understood. We present a comprehensive single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Across 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with phenotypically heterogeneous cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface significantly impacts clinical outcomes. Using our CEFIIRA, we identify tumor cell MHCII expression as a critical predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets confirms that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression associates with local immune activation, antigen presentation, and increased TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we show that tumor MHCII expression 
associates 
with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade, 
while blocking MHCII abrogates this activation.
 Together, our atlas offers novel insights into immune activation, paving the way for improved patient stratification in HGSC.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions 
(1)
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer
,

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer
,
 characterized by high genomic instability 
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity 
(3)
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 
BRCA1/2
 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration 
(4)
 and better responses to combinations with immunotherapies 
(5,6)
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment 
(7–9)
, resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion 
(10)
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
everaging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, 
and tumor reactivity 
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles. 
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition ex-vivo.
 This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.
RESULTS
Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors
To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (
Fig.1a
). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=158).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0007
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
nd functional markers, and cell morphology features. Using unsupervised consensus clustering (see Methods), we uncovered four distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations, 
exhibiting distinct morphological and molecular characteristics
 (
Fig.1k-m
). The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers (
Fig.1k,l; Ext. material - Fig.1,2
). 
Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles 
We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all molecular groups did not reveal significant differences after multiple hypothesis correction (
S.Fig.2a, S.Table 3
). However, consistent with previous reports 
(4)
, we observed that 
BRCA
loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (
Fig.2a
). Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed exclusively in 
BRCA1
loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples (
Fig.2a
). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (
S.Fig.2b
). Interestingly, all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (
Fig.2b, S.Table4,5
). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages, did not exhibit this co-infiltration (
Fig.2b
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0011
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
omparisons did not reveal significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in 
BRCA
loss tumors (
Fig.2i,j
). Interestingly, comparison of the spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01 and RCN02), and immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (
Fig.2k
). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant in the IDS samples (
Fig.2k
) suggesting phenotypic convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our previous observation 
(7)
. These results highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure. 
Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes
Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, 
Fig.2e
), exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI (
Fig.2l
). Consistent with our previous observations 
(4)
, the 
BRCA
loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (
Fig.2j
). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was more abundant in the IDS samples compared to PDS (
Fig.2k
), was significantly enriched in fibroblast stromal cells (
Fig.2l
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0012
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0013
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0014
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ch of the 4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (
Fig.1e,i; S.Fig.1g
), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (
Fig.1j
), resulting in 43 feature representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (
S.Fig.3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1
amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. 
This strategy made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the cell-type–specific contributions. 
As expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (
S.Fig.3c,d
). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both PDS (
Fig.3b, S.Table8
) and IDS (
Fig.3c, S.Table8
), indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell spatial data. 
To account for anatomical site heterogeneity, we trained CEFIIRA models exclusively on primary tumor sites (adnex/ovary) and confirmed that the top predicted features remained consistent
(right panel of 
S.Fig.3o
).
Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples (
S.Fig.3c,d
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
.Fig.3o
).
Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples (
S.Fig.3c,d
). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients in HRP tumors (
Fig.3d,e; S.Table8
). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (
Fig.3d
). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring cells at the TSI. 
However, t
he MHCII levels in cancer cells did not correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is not due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells (
S.Fig.3j
). 
Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed MHCII expression by cancer cells (
Fig.3f; S.Fig.3e
).
 We also performed Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which confirmed the co-localization of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII complex, specifically on the tumor cells (
Fig.3f
). I
n addition, MHCII was also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS samples (
Fig.3e)
. Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.
Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of individual cells.

vector_chunks.text:
.7e-13; 
S.Fig.3k
). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern (
S.Fig.3k,l
). This suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.
To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS (
Fig.3k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) 
(2)
, by inferring the cancer-cell-specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using 
PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136)
. Notably, the observed MHCII expression was not associated with genomic alterations (somatic mutations, loss of heterozygosity, or promoter hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene (
S.Fig.3
m
). However, its expression was higher in 
BRCA
loss tumors compared to HRP and 
CCNE1
amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results (
S.Fig.3
n
). Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that high MHCII expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this independent cohort at the mRNA level (
Fig.3l,m
). These findings confirm that MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of improved clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct tumor-immune interactions within the TME.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0019
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
.
To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both from PDS (N=29), and IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA sequencing 
(10)
 (scRNA-seq) (
Fig.4b
). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic markers as described previously 
(10)
 (see Methods). Using gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a high MHCII expression level similar to that of canonical antigen-presenting cells (
S.Fig.4b
), 
while showing no expression of immune-related markers (
S.Fig.4c
), supporting the accuracy of our annotations
. The proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity (
S.Fig.4
d,e
), prompting us to conduct sample-based downstream analyses. We observed that samples with a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17 proinflammatory helper CD4+ T cells
,
 central memory (CM) CD8+ T cells, related to activation after antigen exposure 
(15)
, and precursor exhausted (PEX) CD8+ T cells, which have traits of both exhaustion and memory 
(16)
 (
Fig.4c-e
). Similar results were observed at the patient level taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations (CM CD8+ T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the smaller sample size 
(S.Fig.4
f
). Altogether, these findings suggest that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.
Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity

r, these findings suggest that MHCII+ cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.
Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity
To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (
S.Fig.4
g
), abundant in cancer cells co-expressing other immunogenic markers, including MHCI, pSTAT1, pTBK1, PDL1 (
Fig.1l
). This indicates that MHCII+ cancer cells are often spatially aggregated even within the cancer cell spatial compartments.
We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to the immune-cold TSI (RCN9) (
S.Fig.4
g
). Consistently, analysis of the cell-cell physical proximity revealed that MHCII+ cancer cells resided more often in close proximity to immune cells as compared to MHCII- cancer cells across the diverse clinicomolecular profiles (
Fig.4f, S.Fig.4
h
). In contrast, MHCII- cancer cells were predominantly neighboring stromal cells. Visual inspection of the tumor tissues confirmed that MHCII+ cells were surrounded by dense clusters of immune cells, forming immune ‘hotspots’, while MHCII- cells were embedded in immune ‘cold’ tumor niches (
Fig.4g
).
To expand our analyses to whole tissue sections, we generated high-dimensional single-cell spatial data using t-CycIF on 15 HRP-IDS samples (
Fig.4h

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0023
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
es TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+ tumors. Gene overrepresentation analysis with PANTHER (RRID:SCR_004869) revealed 14 enriched pathways related to immune activation (
Fig.5a
), including both PD-1 and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways were enriched within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG analysis of cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (
Fig.4b
), we found that MHCII positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors 
RARRES3
 and 
CLU
 associated with immune infiltration 
(17–19)
 (
S.Fig.5c
).
Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort. For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the Nanostring technology 
(20)
. Consistently, a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (
S.Fig.5d, S.Table10
). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with IFN-γ activation 
(21–23)
, were among the top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting that IFN activation plays a key role in MHCII-associated immune activation, as previously suggested also in other cancers 
(24–26)
. 
To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously available spatial transcriptomic dataset 
(27)
 incorporating expert pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses (
Fig.5b
, 
S.Fig.5e
). The annotated TME areas exhibited distinct transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in a UMAP projection (
S.Fig.5f
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0024
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
incorporating expert pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses (
Fig.5b
, 
S.Fig.5e
). The annotated TME areas exhibited distinct transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in a UMAP projection (
S.Fig.5f
). Using MHCII and IFN-γ signatures (see Methods), we found that the MHCII expression was significantly correlated with IFN-γ signature expression across distinct tumor areas, including cancer and TSI regions (
Fig.5c
). Moreover, a higher MHCII signature and increased IFN-γ in both cancer and TSI spatial regions predicted improved clinical responses to chemotherapy at spot level (
S.Fig.5g
,
h
), and similar trends were observed at the patient level, although statistical significance was not reached due to the smaller sample size (
S.Fig.5i
,
j
). Interestingly, in our t-CycIF spatial data (multiomics cohort), statistical analysis revealed significantly higher expression of phospho-STAT1 (pSTAT1), indicative of IFN activation in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells and antigen-presenting (CD11c+) myeloid cells neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells at the TSI (
Fig.5d
), confirmed via visual inspection (
Fig.5e, S. Fig.5k
). 
Moreover, CD8+ T-cells 
neighboring MHCII+ cancer cells
 display a higher expression of PD-1 in indicating increased tumor reactivity independent of MHCI (
Fig.5f
).
These findings suggest that cancer cell MHCII associates with spatially coordinated activation of antigen presentation and T cells via IFN signaling at the TSI. Further, using the scRNA-seq dataset, we observed that the MHCII expression in cancer cells correlated with 
IFNG 
gene expression specifically in CD4+ memory helper and CD8+ central memory (CM) T cells, which are related to activation after antigen exposure (
Fig.5
g
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0025
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
RNA-seq dataset, we observed that the MHCII expression in cancer cells correlated with 
IFNG 
gene expression specifically in CD4+ memory helper and CD8+ central memory (CM) T cells, which are related to activation after antigen exposure (
Fig.5
g
). Overall, these findings reinforce the link between cancer-intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of cancer cell MHCII in spatial immune activation, 
also independent of MHCI.
To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict ligand–receptor pairs (
Fig.5
h
, Ext. material - Fig.
5
). We identified the top seven ligand–receptor interactions across CD8+ T cells, CD4+ T cells, Tregs, and DCs (
Fig.5
i
). Interestingly, IFN signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8+ and CD4+ T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions across the cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune tolerance 
(28)
. Similarly, in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (
Fig.5
j
), we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL, IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (
Fig.5
k
). In addition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig.5
k
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0026
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig.5
k
). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.
Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity 
Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins 
(29)
. To explore whether cancer-specific MHCII expression is linked with specific antigen presentation, we focused on previously identified antigens detected via HLA ligandome analysis in HGSC 
(30)
 (
Fig.6a
). We found that the deconvoluted expression of MHCII in cancer cells is indeed associated with the expression of most antigen-encoding genes in the TCGA dataset (
Fig.6b
). However, in our scRNA-seq dataset, only four specific antigen genes (UBC, RARRES1, ADRG2, MSLN) were expressed in the cancer cells, and associated with tumor MHCII expression (
Fig.6c
). In our GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, we also observed positive associations of MHCII expression in the tumor segment with the expression of several antigen-encoding genes in the adjacent stroma at the TSI (
Fig.6d
).
To quantify the expression of these peptides on tumor MHCII molecules in HGSC, 
we performed immunopeptidomic profiling of the MHCII peptides alongside bulkRNAseq and additional deconvolution and TCR repertoire analysis (
Fig.6e
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0028
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
nterestingly, many of these peptides were linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion (
YAP, SNGC,
TNS1, PKP2
). 
Fina
lly, to investigate the immunological context of distinct MHCII antigen presentation, we assessed T cell clonal diversity in these samples. We observed a significant increase in the number of TCR clones – specifically TRAD and TRB – within samples showing a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells (
Fig.6j
).
Tumor MHCII associates with responses to anti-PD1 and is abrogated upon MHCII blockade
 ex vivo
We functionally investigated the role of tumor MHCII in modulating T-cell responses to immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) using our established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs) 
(32)
. Specifically, we analyzed the effects of PD-1 and TIGIT inhibition, as well as the impact of MHCII blockade on PD-1 inhibition-induced immune cell phenotypes and cytokine secretion across 13 iPDCs (
Fig.7a, S.Fig.7a,b
). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited significantly enhanced CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity in response to pembrolizumab (
Fig.7b, S.Table11)
. A sample-by-sample analysis revealed a statistically significant increase in cytotoxicity (p < 0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated conditions within the MHCII-high group 
(Fig.7c
), confirming that MHCII expression contributes to the response to PD-1 blockade. To further assess whether MHCII is essential for this response, we utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII. Notably, MHCII blockade abolished PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (
Fig.7d
) and Granzyme B (
Fig.7e
) expression in multiple T cell subsets.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0030
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
hin the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance 
(33,34)
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+ T cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer 
(35)
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms 
(36)
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+ T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig.7g
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC (
Fig.7h
). 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
(37)
.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0031
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
e activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC (
Fig.7h
). 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
(37)
. 
The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to ICBs in various tumor types 
(25,35,38,39)
. 
However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated disappointing results 
(40)
. We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides 
linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to post translational modifications 
(41)
with increased immunopeptide potential 
(33)
.
The proximity to MHCII tumor cells associated
 with CD8+T cell tumor reactivity, independent of MHCI, and higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC.
 Our 
ex vivo 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. 
I
mportantly, we show that MHCII positive tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally dependent on MHCII.
 This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in 
Fig 7h.
Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1 
(10)
.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
h.
Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1 
(10)
.
In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. 
The retrospective design and reliance on archival tissues on a TMA may introduce biases related to tissue and patient heterogeneity, limiting some of the statistical comparisons, and limiting conclusions on larger tissue structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to comorbidities or extent of disease 
(42)
. 
Our protein marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our analysis culminated in a testable hypothesis, which we encourage future studies to explore in larger prospective patient cohorts.

DATA AVAILABILITY
For the multiomics cohort, single-cell spatial t-CycIF data with group-level annotations including the molecular profiles, therapy sequences, and clinical survival data is deposited in 
synapse.org
: 
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn66511447
. Fluorescence microscopy images will be provided upon reasonable request to the corresponding author for non-commercial and academic purposes, and all requests will be promptly reviewed within 15 working days. Anonymous individual-level clinical data sharing from the Dutch Cancer Registry (NKR) will be considered for non-commercial, research, or statistical-based use on a case-by-case basis (to be requested and approved by the NKR; gegevensaanvraag@iknl.nl).

vector_chunks.text:
onymous individual-level clinical data sharing from the Dutch Cancer Registry (NKR) will be considered for non-commercial, research, or statistical-based use on a case-by-case basis (to be requested and approved by the NKR; gegevensaanvraag@iknl.nl). The human sequence data generated in this study are not publicly available due to patient privacy requirements, but can be made available upon reasonable request from the corresponding author.
CODE AVAILABILITY
The custom scripts used to generate the results are available at
https://github.com/farkkilab/devNKI-scripts.git
.
MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0038
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as described 
(43)
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-CycIF. 
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). 
All patients received neoadjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, followed by interval debulking surgeries (IDS), and none of them received PARP inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS
. The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to 
ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138) 
(44)
and absence of 
BRCA1
 or 
BRCA2
 germline or somatic mutation
. In addition,
 a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0039
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
tients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.
For genomic alteration and number of HRD scar calculations, DNA was extracted from FFPE blocks and profiled using the Illumina SNP-array GSAv3. Allelic imbalances were detected using GenomeStudio (RRID:SCR_010973) and 
ASCAT
v3 (RRID:SCR_016868). The fraction of the genome altered was defined as the number of base pairs in segments larger than 1 kb with both scaled major and minor copy numbers different from 1, divided by the total length of the human genome. 
The tumor mutational burden 
was adopted from the iMTB reports (see iMTB report subsection).
Molecular profile assignment
For the TMA cohort, we first identified 
BRCA1/2 
mutation and 
BRCA1
 promoter methylation status of each sample as previously described 
(43)
. In brief, 
BRCA1/2 
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a 
BRCA1/2
 germline mutation, both 
BRCA1/2
 somatic mutations and 
BRCA1 
promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the number of tumors with 
BRCA1 
or 
BRCA2 
mutations and 
BRCA1
 promoter methylation are reported in 
S. Table 1
.
Next, tumors without a 
BRCA1/2
 mutation or 
BRCA1
