vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the number of tumors with 
BRCA1 
or 
BRCA2 
mutations and 
BRCA1
 promoter methylation are reported in 
S. Table 1
.
Next, tumors without a 
BRCA1/2
 mutation or 
BRCA1 
promoter hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using Illumina HiSeq2500 
at 0.5X
 to determine a 
BRCA
-like
CNV profile 
(45)
 and 
CCNE1
-amplification status as described previously 
(43)
. In addition, using the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software shallowHRD 
(RRID:SCR_027714)
. Finally, we categorized the tumors as 
BRCAloss
, HRD, HRP, and 
CCNE1
amp using a hierarchical approach described in 
S. Fig. 1a
. Samples that failed QC for 
BRCA1/2
 targeted DNA-sequencing, 
BRCA1
 promoter hypermethylation, sWGS, or CNV analysis, were labeled as “Unassigned” (or Other). In addition, samples with contradictory HRD status between shallowHRD and the 
BRCA-like
 CNV profile 
(45)
 were labeled as “Unassigned”.
For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138) 
(44)
. For all analyses, only tumors with a 
TP53
 mutation, histological grades G3 and G4, and those at high stages (stage III and stage IV) were included. For the whole slide cohort, we adopted the clinically validated HRD classification performed at the Helsinki University Hospital, Finland, which uses 
ovaHRDscar
(44)
, and the detection of somatic and germline mutations and gene deletions in 
BRCA1/2
 genes.
Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0041
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
and germline mutations and gene deletions in 
BRCA1/2
 genes.
Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (
S.Table2
) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol 
(46)
. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images’ illumination was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371). The per-image tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266, 
https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar
). Individual TMA’s cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik (RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using 2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB (RRID:SCR_001622). Quality filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles (
https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite
), image regions with artifacts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells. 
For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex validated antibody panel (
S.Table12

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0042
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
acts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells. 
For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex validated antibody panel (
S.Table12
) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were stitched with ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266). For WSI, we developed a more scalable analysis pipeline (
https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing
). Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei segmentation in 
StarDist
 (RRID:SCR_027715), implementing the pre-trained model ‘
2D_versatile_flu
o''. Then, the mean intensity of each channel was quantified by our in-house script based on scikit-image package (RRID:SCR_021142). Image quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157).
Cell phenotyping
For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0043
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
g tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel's signal was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (
S. Fig. 1c
) by using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (
S. Fig. 1c
). All the cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion for each patient’s sample. 
For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367) 
(47)
. Outliers were removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity, normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of
5 
(48)
, and scaled by Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of 
sigma=1
, 
learning_rate=1
, 
clustering_threshold=100
, 
undefined_threshold=0.0005
, 
other_threshold=0.4
. A total of 6 cell types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+ T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table (
S.Table13
) for all images when running Tribus.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0044
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
_threshold=0.0005
, 
other_threshold=0.4
. A total of 6 cell types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+ T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table (
S.Table13
) for all images when running Tribus. 
The exceptions are two images, which were manually gated with scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) due to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells.
 We corroborated our annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, showing agreement across samples (
S.Fig.4
m
). The MHCII+ cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the annotations to the t-CycIF images.
Cancer cell phenotype clustering
Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based on the expression levels of the markers: "Ki67", "Vimentin", "pSTAT1", "MHC-I", "ECadherin", "yH2AX", "pTBK1", "cPARP1", "CK7", "PDL1_488", and morphological features: "Area", "Eccentricity", and "Roundness" into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954). For this, the number of clusters (
k
) was assessed from 2 to 12. For each value of 
k
 assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm “partitioning around medoids” (pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, 
k

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0045
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
es (markers and morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm “partitioning around medoids” (pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, 
k
, by assessing the resultant consensus-clustering matrix for each value of 
k
 and its cumulative density distribution (
Ext. material - Fig. 3
). The cancer meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers in each one (
Ext. material - Fig. 2
).
Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis
For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap (RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k
=
40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick
 (RRID:SCR_027716)
 (
Ext. material - Fig. 3
). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (
Ext. material - Fig. 3
). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.
Xenium 
The Xenium procedure was performed according to 10x standard protocol. Image processing and quality control were performed using the latest version of 
Xenium Ranger
 (v1.2).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0046
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ber of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.
Xenium 
The Xenium procedure was performed according to 10x standard protocol. Image processing and quality control were performed using the latest version of 
Xenium Ranger
 (v1.2). The tissue sections were then stained according to the tCycIF protocol 
(46)
with PanCK (AE1/AE3, ThermoFisher, Catalog No. 757900382).
Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis
To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data, we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC, we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat's SCTransform function. 
Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an expert pathologist as ‘Cancer’, ‘TSI’ (tumor-stroma interface with partial tumor and stromal morphology), ‘Immune-Stromal’ and ‘other’. Spots annotated as ‘other’ have been excluded from the analysis. Within ‘Immune-Stromal’ spots, we have distinguished an additional type - ‘TSI2’, defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0047
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
efined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109). The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor response was adopted from Böhm et al, 20
15 
(49)
. An IFN-γ signature, obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene set collection (RRID:SCR_01686
3)
, was also quantified per spot. Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided Wilcoxon Rank Sum test. 
CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification
 CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ll data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled
, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0049
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0050
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
S.Fig.3o
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of 
the mean decrease accuracy scores. 
By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the contribution of individual features to the model's accuracy.
 Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0051
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
val prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors. 
Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile
For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (RRID:SCR_003193) 
(2)
. The deconvoluted cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM 
(RRID:SCR_027136)
. The inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT (RRID:SCR_016955) were obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository.
RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment
For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_00446
3)
 from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas reposito
ry
. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1 in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33) expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0052
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
rom each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB v19.0 (RRID:SCR_004869), we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485). 
For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202), we first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest (below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold change >=1 or Log2-fold change <=1 and FDR<1^-3. Overexpressed genes are reported in 
S.Table10
.
scRNA-seq analysis
We retrieved scRNA-seq gene count matrices from 51 HGSC samples, pre-processed and annotated 
(7)
, from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis, resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from 27 patients.

vector_chunks.text:
ated 
(7)
, from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis, resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from 27 patients. We assessed the activity of MHCII signature across cell types by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1
.
 The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: 
HLA-DMA
, 
HLA-DMB
, 
HLA-DOA
, 
HLA-DOB
, 
HLA-DPA1
, 
HLA-DPB1
, 
HLA-DQA1
, 
HLA-DQA2
, 
HLA-DQB1
, 
HLA-DRA
, 
HLA-DRB1
, 
HLA-DRB5
, 
CD74.
 Based on the MHCII Ucell scores, the cells were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). For each sample and cell type, pseudo-bulk gene expression profiles were generated using the function ‘
AggregateExpression’
 from the Seurat package (RRID:SCR_016341) v4.3.0. According to the pseudo-bulk MHCII UCell signature in cancer cells, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low, according to tertile distribution (
S.Fig.4c
). To assess the correlation between cancer-specific MHCII expression and IFN-γ in immune populations, pseudo-bulk expression of the 
IFNG 
gene was computed for each cell type per sample. Only immune cells were included in the correlation analysis, as they exhibited higher 
IFNG
 expression compared to fibroblasts or epithelial/cancer cells (
S.Fig.5l
). Pearson’s correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
orrelation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943). Patient information was added to the design matrix and regressed out from the DEG analysis. 
In addition, we assessed the expression of immune markers across EOC
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis 
We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy. Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 
(10)
, and is publicly available at
(https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
). The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0055
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499). This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from BayesPrism) for each AOI.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0056
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
r-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.
Ligand-receptor analysis
To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq analysis). MultiNicheNetR
(
https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git
) analysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0057
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
en cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing.
Immunopeptidomics
We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
(50)
 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0058
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2;

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0059
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
es were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient 
was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0060
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
mance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher (https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher)
,
 applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant (https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_02543
4)
 with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0061
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
HCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_02543
4)
 with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quan
t
) with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis, peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CycIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_00157
5)
 (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms were recorded. These genes were also filtered based on their presence in the ligandome of healthy ovary tissues 
(31)
. Resulting 35 genes’ expression was studied in deconvoluted bulk RNAseq of the same 12 samples from iCAN study.
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0062
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
in deconvoluted bulk RNAseq of the same 12 samples from iCAN study.
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq. We especially focus on the somatic mutation module (PCGR, https://github.com/sigven/pcgr), which contains tumor purity and ploidy estimation, variant classification (somatic SNVs/InDels and copy number aberrations), and tumor mutational burden (TMB) estimation. Tumor purity was used to verify the result of PRISM deconvolution.
Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data
RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale factors, and cell-type–specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0063
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ons from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and immune–stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.
TCR repertoire analysis

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0064
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
raction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study 
(as described in previous section)
. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725), following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089)
.
 We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0065
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
tratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.
iPDCs flow cytometry analysis
To study how tumor MHCII affects the response to ICB, t
he dataset generated in Launonen et al., 2024 
(10)
 was used. Briefly, tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an omentum-derived matrix 
(32)
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at 
(10)
. 
In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S.Fig.6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0066
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and custom R scripts.
To reveal how the blockade of MHCII affects the response to ICB, frozen tumor-derived samples from patients with high MHCII expression were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort based on RNA expression in deconvoluted with PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) tumor proportion. The tumor tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to cryopreservation. For iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as droplets in an omentum-derived extracellular matrix 
(32)
. Cultures were treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) (RRID:AB_395939) or isotype control was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a consistent gating strategy across all samples.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0067
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
cquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and subsequently analyzed in R. Statistical analyses were performed in RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418, RRID:SCR_000432) using R (version 4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFNg, GrzB) along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition. Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons between conditions. 
Cytokine profiling via LEGENDplex
™
 assay
Supernatants from the cultures used to assess the effect of MHC-II blockade in the responses to pembrolizumab treatment were collected at the experimental endpoint (day 5), clarified by centrifugation (1500 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and stored at –80 °C until the time of assay. Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK Panel (13-plex) (BioLegend, catalog #741187, lot #B409342). The assay was carried out according to the manufacturer’s recommended protocol. Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa
TM
 cell analyzer (BD Biosciences), and the resulting data were processed using the LEGENDplex™ Data Analysis Software. Final plots were generated using custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
Analysis Software. Final plots were generated using custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
S.Fig.1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S.Fig.1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3 (RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations.

sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

FIGURE LEGENDS
Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.
a
 Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: 
BRCA
loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-
BRCA
wt), homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification (
CCNE1amp
), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles. Our results were validated using statistical methods, followed by further biological analysis for interpretation, both incorporating external cohorts.

Antibody panel employed for t-CycIF, targeting 30 different markers.

projection of the analyzed cells, the colors correspond to their main cell type group assignment.

.

Bar plot illustrating the composition of immune cells per tumor. The bar plot is sorted according to the CD11c+ myeloid cell proportion. 
h 
UMAP
projection of immune cells coloured by their annotation as in panel f.

t-CycIF image displaying the functional marker Ki67;

Figure 2. Coordinated immune infiltration, cancer-phenotype patterns and spatial TME domains associate with distinct clinicomolecular features 
a 
Box plots for the median immune infiltration per tumor (across the sampled cores) grouped by molecular profiles. Only tumors sampled with at least two cores are included. In the boxplots, black horizontal lines represent the median, the boxes extend from the first to the third quartile, and the whiskers indicate the values at 1.5 times the interquartile range. Outliers are shown as dots.

Outliers are shown as dots. The differences in proportion were calculated using a two-tailed Wilcoxon rank-sum test. The notation for unadjusted P-values is as follows: *P<=0.05, **P<=0.01. 
b 
Heatmap displaying the Pearson correlation of median immune cell infiltration across the tumors (inter-tumours). Analysis was done for tumors sampled with more than one core after QC. Median infiltration per tumor was calculated across the sampled cores. Only pairwise significant (FDR<0.05) correlations are shown. Correlation values are reported in S. Table 4 and FDRs in S. Table 5. 
c 
Forest plot of the Cox regression of Overall Survival (OS) in relation to the median abundance of the indicated immune cells per tumor. The regressions were adjusted by patient age, FIGO stage and molecular profile and were performed separately for PDS and IDS samples. Analysis done for tumors sampled with more than one core after QC and with associated clinical information (S. Fig. 1b). The small squares represent the mean hazard ratio (HR), and the whiskers show the 95% confidence intervals. Only cell populations from which was detected statistical significance (FDR < 0.05) are shown. Inferences for the full immune populations are shown in S. Fig. 2c. 
d
 Forest plot of the Cox regression OS in relation to the median abundance of the indicated cancer cell cluster per tumor. Analysis done for tumors sampled with more than one core after QC, with more than 5% of cancer cells per tumor and with associated clinical information (S. Fig. 1b).

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
 A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss and HRP).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx::chunk_0082
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For coauthors/Perez et al manuscript text.docx
vector_chunks.text:
‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.
 b-c
 Heatmap displaying the median accuracy (F1 score) of CEFIIRA models in predicting whether cells belong to tumors from short- or long-survival patients, across the indicated molecular profiles (rows) and cellular populations (columns). The 95% confidence intervals for the F1 scores are shown in parentheses. The results corresponding to PDS samples are in panel 
b, 
and in panel 
c 
for IDS samples. 
d-e
 Dot plots displaying the log2 fold change in scaled marker intensity between cells from short- and long-survival patients with HRP tumors. The markers displayed correspond to the top five most important features for each model (excluding clinical variables).

### word/document.xml
Dear 
Dr.
Michele Hartsough,
We are pleased to submit our revised manuscript, titled 
“
Single-cell spatial atlas of high-grade serous ovarian cancer uncovers MHC class II as a key predictor of spatial tumor ecosystems and clinical outcomes
”
. 
We sincerely thank the reviewers for their invaluable and positive comments. We have now comprehensively addressed all the comments and believe that the manuscript has been significantly strengthened. 
Importantly, we provide new experimental validations including 
Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics and 
functional ex vivo experiments confirming the mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation.
The key improvements in the manuscript include 
Improved resolution of tumor-specific expression of MHCII - with 
Xenium single-cell transcriptomics, additional representations of spatial proteomics, scRNAseq and imaging data analysis
 excluding the possibility of artefacts/spill over and demonstrating true MHCII expression in the tumor cells.
Additional compelling evidence supporting the role of tumor MHCII in HGSC taking into account 
tumor genomic features, anatomical site variation and associated immune cell signatures.
Additional validation on the effect of tumor MHCII independently of MHCI demonstrating MHCII specific effect on CD8+ T-cell tumor reactivity. 
New analysis demonstrating the 
technical robustness of CEFIIRA
, excluding the effect of overall feature variability explaining the model performance. 
Improved malignant-compartment specific immunopeptidomic analysis showing 
MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion
 providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor cells.
New functional ex vivo experiments confirming the mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation

eptidomic analysis showing 
MHCII antigens linked to cytoskeleton and adhesion
 providing the mechanistic background of MHCII expression on tumor cells.
New functional ex vivo experiments confirming the mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation
. Using our novel platform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we demonstrate that MHCII blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production. These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes in cancer treatment
The key findings of our manuscript are summarized below:
Direct spatial link between MHCII⁺ tumor cells and immune hotspots.
We demonstrate, for the first time, that intra-tumoral immune hotspots spatially coincide with 
MHCII expression on cancer cells
. Adjacent immune cells are 
more activated and tumor-reactive
, establishing MHCII as a driver of localized immune activation.
Immune activation
 is spatially confined.
The composition of the tumor–stroma boundary shapes immune activity 
independently of tumor molecular features
.These microanatomical structures influence local immune behavior in ways not detectable by bulk or non-spatial methods.
Mechanistic validation of MHCII as a predictive immunotherapy biomarker. 
Tumors with high MHCII expression show 
enhanced responsiveness to immune checkpoint blockade
. Blocking MHCII function eliminates this benefit, confirming a 
causal role
 in treatment response.
Altogether, our

predictive immunotherapy biomarker. 
Tumors with high MHCII expression show 
enhanced responsiveness to immune checkpoint blockade
. Blocking MHCII function eliminates this benefit, confirming a 
causal role
 in treatment response.
Altogether, our 
work establishes a mechanistic, spatially resolved link between tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression and localized immune activation, defines how microanatomical interfaces shape immune behavior, and demonstrates that MHCII function predicts response to immune checkpoint blockade. Our 
findings now have significant clinical implications, suggesting that MHC-II expression predicts overall tumor immunogenicity, potentially stratifying pati
ents who benefit from PD-1 inhibition,
 and guide the development of alternative therapeutic approaches for MHC-II–negative HGSCs.
Thank you for considering our manuscript for publication. We look forward to the opportunity to contribute to 
Cancer Discovery
.
Yours sincerely,
Anniina Färkkilä
MD PhD, Assistant Professor in Translational Gynecologic Oncology
Specialist in Obstetrics and Gynecology
Research Program in Systems Oncology &
Department of Oncology and Obstetrics and Gynecology
HiLIFE and FIMM University of Helsinki and Helsinki University Hospital
B327b 00014 University of Helsinki
www.farkkilab.org
anniina.farkkila@helsinki.fi

Number of words: 5811
Number of Figures: 7
Number of Supplementary Figures: 7
Number of Supplementary Tables: 16
Number of Extended materials: 5
Abstract
The tumor microenvironment in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration remains poorly understood. We present a single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Analyzing 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0003
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
entify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with immunopeptidomic analysis, confirms that MHCII expression correlates with immune activation, antigen presentation, and TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we demonstrate that tumor MHCII expression 
associates
 with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade, 
while blocking MHCII inhibits this activation
. Our atlas offers new insights into immune activation, potentially improving patient stratification in HGSC.
Statement of significance
This study delivers the first large-scale single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC, revealing how tumor–immune organization shapes outcomes. We identify tumor-intrinsic MHCII as a key driver of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness, providing a mechanistic biomarker that can immediately inform improved patient stratification and therapeutic decision-making in ovarian cancer.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions
(1)
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0004
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
anges, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 
BRCA1/2
 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
(4)
 and better responses to combinations with immunotherapies
(5,6)
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment
(7–9)
, resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
(10)
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0005
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
at associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, 
and tumor reactivity 
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles. 
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition ex-vivo.
 This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.
RESULTS
Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors
To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied multiomic profiling including targeted and shallow whole-genome sequencing, gene-expression and DNA methylation profiling combined with high-plex imaging at a cellular resolution on 265 advanced HGSCs (
Fig.1a
). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=158). Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months) (
S.Table1
).

vector_chunks.text:
distinct cancer cell phenotypic populations, 
exhibiting distinct morphological and molecular characteristics
 (
Fig.1k-m
). The immunogenic cluster corresponded to cells with high expression of immune regulatory markers (MHCI, PDL1, pSTAT1, pTBK1); the Proliferative cluster showed high Ki67 expression and a larger cell area; the EMT cluster had elevated Vimentin expression; and the Idle cluster displayed overall lower expression of the functional markers (
Fig.1k,l; Ext.material - Fig.1,2
). 
Coordinated immune infiltration and cancer-phenotype patterns are linked to clinicomolecular profiles 
We set out to evaluate patterns of immune infiltration across the defined clinicomolecular groups. Statistical testing across all molecular groups did not reveal significant differences after multiple hypothesis correction (
S.Fig.2a, S.Table3
). However, consistent with previous reports 
(4)
, we observed that 
BRCA
loss tumors had a higher infiltration of T.cells and CD11c.MY, specifically after chemotherapy exposure (
Fig.2a
). Notably, a higher abundance of general myeloid cells was observed exclusively in 
BRCA1
loss tumors in IDS, but not in PDS samples (
Fig.2a
). When comparing tumors based on therapy sequence, we found that CD8+ T cells, effector CD4+ T cells, B cells, dendritic cells, and macrophages were enriched in IDS samples (
S.Fig.2b
). Interestingly, all lymphocyte populations and CD11c.MY populations demonstrated significant co-infiltration within the TME across the tumors (
Fig.2b, S.Table4,5
). In contrast, other myeloid cells, such as macrophages, did not exhibit this co-infiltration (
Fig.2b
). These results imply that lymphocyte and CD11c.MY infiltrations represent a coordinated process potentially orchestrated through shared biological mechanisms.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0012
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
did not reveal significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in 
BRCA
loss tumors (
Fig.2i,j
). Interestingly, comparison of the spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of stromal-non endothelial neighbourhoods (RCN01, RCN02), and immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and proliferative tumor cell neighbourhood (RCN7) in the PDS samples (
Fig.2k
). Further, the cancer heterogenous neighbourhood (RCN4) was less abundant in the IDS samples (
Fig.2k
) suggesting phenotypic convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our previous observation
(7)
. These results highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure. 
Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes
Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3, 
Fig.2e
), exhibiting the most heterogeneous cell composition among all the RCNs. Statistical comparison of the two TSI types, revealed that immune-infiltrated TSI contained significantly higher proportions of T cells, myeloid cells, macrophages, and B cells suggesting the presence of coordinated immune infiltration at the TSI (
Fig.2l
). Consistent with our previous observations
(4)
, the 
BRCA
loss tumors contained the highest abundance of immune-infiltrated TSI (
Fig.2j
). By contrast, the immune-cold TSI (RCN9), which was more abundant in the IDS samples compared to PDS (
Fig.2k
), was significantly enriched in fibroblast stromal cells (
Fig.2l
).
