vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0015
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ch of the 4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (
Fig.1e,i; S.Fig.1g
), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (
Fig.1j
), resulting in 43 feature representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (
S.Fig.3a,b
). We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1
amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. 
This strategy made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the cell-type–specific contributions. 
As expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (
S.Fig.3c,d
,e
). The models trained on HRP-like tumors exhibited better classification performance than those trained on HRD-like tumors in both PDS (
Fig.3b, S.Table8
) and IDS (
Fig.3c,S.Table8
), indicating that CEFIIRA can separate the patients with differential prognosis within the HRP-like tumors using multimodal single-cell spatial data. 
To account for anatomical site heterogeneity, we trained CEFIIRA models exclusively on primary tumor sites (adnex/ovary) and confirmed that the top predicted features remained consistent (
S.Fig.3f
).
Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples (
S.Fig.3c,d
,e
).

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
g.3f
).
Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples (
S.Fig.3c,d
,e
). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients in HRP tumors (
Fig.3d,e; S.Table8
). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (
Fig.3d
). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring cells at the TSI. 
However, the MHCII levels in cancer cells did not correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is not due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells (
S.Fig.3g
). 
Indeed, visual inspection of the t-CycIF images confirmed MHCII expression by cancer cells (
Fig.3f
).
 We also performed Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which confirmed the co-localization of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII complex, specifically on the tumor cells (
Fig.3f
). I
n addition, MHCII was also the top predictive marker in the immune-stromal cells in IDS samples (
Fig.3e)
. Altogether, CEFIIRA models revealed that MHCII and immune-stromal markers, indicative of cancer-stromal spatial proximity, are relevant for OS prediction regardless of therapy sequence.
Next, to determine if MHCII expression is specific to a molecular profile, we trained CEFIIRA models to predict the molecular profile of individual cells.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0018

vector_chunks.text:
e-13; 
S.Fig.3
l
). However, several tumors displayed high MHCII expression coupled with low MHCI expression in a non-concordant pattern (
S.Fig.3
l,m
). This suggests that MHCII expression in cancer cells can occur independently of MHCI expression.
To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS (
Fig.3k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) 
(2)
, by inferring the cancer-cell-specific MHCII expression from bulk RNA-seq using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136). Notably, the observed MHCII expression was not associated with genomic alterations (somatic mutations, loss of heterozygosity, or promoter hypermethylation) in the MHCII gene (
S.Fig.3
n
). However, its expression was higher in 
BRCA
loss tumors compared to HRP and 
CCNE1
amp tumors, confirming CEFIIRA results (
S.Fig.3
o
). Consistently using adjusted Cox models, we observed that high MHCII expression by cancer cells predicted better OS in this independent cohort at the mRNA level (
Fig.3l,m
). These findings confirm that MHCII expression in cancer cells is a predictor marker of improved clinical outcomes in HGSC, suggesting a link to distinct tumor-immune interactions within the TME.

vector_chunks.text:
TME.
To further investigate the distinct immune cell proportions and underlying transcriptional programs, we analyzed 51 HGSC samples, both from PDS (N=29), and IDS (n=22), from 29 patients using single-cell RNA sequencing
(10)
 (scRNA-seq;
Fig.4b
). The dataset comprised 136,452 cells, which included 25,091 epithelial/cancer cells, 13,952 non-immune stromal cells, and 97,409 immune cells, annotated using canonical phenotypic markers as described previously
(10)
 (see Methods). Using gene signature analysis, 12% of cancer cells had a high MHCII expression level similar to that of canonical antigen-presenting cells (
S.Fig.4b
), 
while showing no expression of immune-related markers (
S.Fig.4c
), supporting the accuracy of our annotations
. The proportion of MHCII positivity in cancer cells exhibited notable intra-patient heterogeneity (
S.Fig.4
d,e
), prompting us to conduct sample-based downstream analyses. We observed that samples with a high proportion of MHCII+cancer cells had increased proportions of type 17 proinflammatory helper CD4+T cells, central memory (CM) CD8+T cells, related to activation after antigen exposure
(15)
, and precursor exhausted (PEX) CD8+T cells, which have traits of both exhaustion and memory 
(16)
 (
Fig.4c-e
). Similar results were observed at the patient level taking only PDS samples; however, for one of these immune populations (CM CD8+T cells), statistical significance was not reached, due to the smaller sample size 
(S.Fig.4
f
). Altogether, these findings suggest that MHCII+cancer cells influence immune infiltration by promoting antigen presentation and driving proinflammatory responses.
Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity

vector_chunks.text:
checkpoint molecules TIGIT, CTLA4 and TMIGD2 in the MHCII+ tumors. Gene overrepresentation analysis with PANTHER revealed 14 enriched pathways related to immune activation (
Fig.5a
), including both PD-1 and chemokine signaling, and IFN-γ activation. No pathways were enriched within the under-expressed genes. Consistently, in DEG analysis of cancer cells of the scRNA-seq dataset (
Fig.4b
), we found that MHCII positivity was associated with upregulation of the tumor-suppressors 
RARRES3
 and 
CLU 
(
S.Fig.5c
)associated with immune infiltration
(17–19)
.
Next, we aimed to validate these findings using the multiomics cohort. For this, we utilized expression data of 335 genes profiled with the Nanostring technology 
(20)
. Consistently, a high proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was associated with overexpression of chemokines, cytokines, immune checkpoints, and immune receptors (
S.Fig.5d; S.Table10
). Interestingly, CXCL9, CXCL10, and CXCL11 chemokines, and their receptor CXCR3, associated with IFN-γ activation 
(21–23)
, were among the top overexpressed genes as in TCGA, suggesting that IFN activation plays a key role in MHCII-associated immune activation, as previously suggested
(24–26)
. 
To explore the spatial correlations between MHCII expression and IFN-γ signaling, we conducted an in-depth re-analysis of a previously available spatial transcriptomic dataset
(27)
 incorporating expert pathologist annotations and spatially resolved signature analyses (
Fig.5b
; 
S.Fig.5e
). The annotated TME areas exhibited distinct transcriptomic profiles visualized in differential clusters in a UMAP projection (
S.Fig.5f
).

vector_chunks.text:
M) T cells, which are related to activation after antigen exposure (
Fig.5
g
). Overall, these findings reinforce the link between cancer-intrinsic MHCII and IFN-γ activation and highlight the role of cancer cell MHCII in spatial immune activation, 
also independent of MHCI.
To investigate distinct interactions between immune and cancer cells in MHCII-high tumor samples, we utilized scRNA-seq data to predict ligand–receptor pairs (
Fig.5
h
, Ext.material Fig.
5
). We identified the top seven ligand–receptor interactions across CD8+T cells, CD4+T cells, Tregs, and DCs (
Fig.5
i
). Interestingly, IFN signaling and MPZL-mediated cell adhesion were increased specifically in CD8+ and CD4+T cells. DCs showed activation of EGFR signaling and APOE-mediated lipoprotein pathways. We also observed TGF-β1 interactions across the cell populations, which may indicate the induction of immune tolerance
(28)
. Similarly, in the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset from the TSI (
Fig.5
j
), we observed a significant correlation between tumor-specific MHCII ssGSEA scores and adjacent stromal segment expression of TGF-β1, MPZL, IFN, APOE, and ALOX5AP ligands (
Fig.5
k
). In addition, VEGFA, one of the predicted top ligands in dendritic cells (DCs), showed a negative correlation, suggesting that the VEGFA-NRP1 mediated DC-cancer cell interactions may occur at spatially distinct locations, rather than at the TSI (
Fig.5
k
). In conclusion, MHCII expressing cancer cells elicit distinct transcriptional programs and engage immune cells in the surrounding stroma through ligand-receptor interactions associated with activation, adhesion, and secretory pathways.
Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
vivo
We functionally investigated the role of tumor MHCII in modulating T-cell responses to immune checkpoint blockade (ICB) using our established immuno-oncology platform of three-dimensional immune-competent patient-derived cultures (iPDCs)
(32)
. Specifically, we analyzed the effects of PD-1 and TIGIT inhibition, as well as the impact of MHCII blockade on PD-1 inhibition-induced immune cell phenotypes and cytokine secretion across 13 iPDCs (
Fig.7a; S.Fig.7a,b
). Notably, MHCII-high tumors exhibited significantly enhanced CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity in response to pembrolizumab (
Fig.7b; S.Table12)
. A sample-by-sample analysis revealed a statistically significant increase in cytotoxicity (p < 0.05) between baseline and pembrolizumab-treated conditions within the MHCII-high group 
(Fig.7c
), confirming that MHCII expression contributes to the response to PD-1 blockade. To further assess whether MHCII is essential for this response, we utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII. Notably, MHCII blockade abolished PD-1-induced intracellular IFN-γ (
Fig.7d; S.Fig.7c
) and Granzyme B (
Fig.7e; S.Fig.7d
) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally, multiplexed cytokine profiling of conditioned media from the iPDCs confirmed the loss of extracellular cytokine production to the supernatant after MHCII blockade (
Fig.7f
). These functional assays demonstrate the critical role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and local immune responses, supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII.
Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0030

vector_chunks.text:
tion on MHCII.
Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context of PD-1 ICB.
DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical samples. Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
(33,34)
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+T cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer
(35)
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
(36)
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0031
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ay also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
(36)
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. 
These findings, summarized in 
Fig.7g
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
(37)
. The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to ICBs in various tumor types
(25,35,38,39)
. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated disappointing results
(40)
. We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides 
linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to post translational modifications
(31)
 with increased immunopeptide potential
(41)
.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
results
(40)
. We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides 
linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to post translational modifications
(31)
 with increased immunopeptide potential
(41)
.
 The proximity to MHCII tumor cells associated with CD8+T cell tumor reactivity, independent of MHCI, and higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. Our 
ex vivo 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. 
Importantly, we show that MHCII positive tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally dependent on MHCII. This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in 
Fig 7h.
Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1
(10)
.
In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. 
Regardless of the validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the multiomics cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0033
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ortunities for future refinement and exploration. 
Regardless of the validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the multiomics cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to comorbidities or extent of disease 
(42)
. 
Our protein marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our conclusions encourage future studies in prospective patient cohorts.

G.S.S., M.A.vd.A., K.K.Vd.V, and R.FPM.K contributed to reviewing, editing and gathering of resources. A.Vi. performed pathologist annotations. V.C., A.Vä., and P.K.S. provided resources and guidance. H.M.H. provided materials, performed study conceptualization, and drafting of the manuscript. A.F. performed study conceptualization and design, interpretation of results, writing of the manuscript and supervision.

synapse.org
: 
https://doi.org/10.7303/syn66511447
. Fluorescence microscopy images will be provided upon reasonable request to the corresponding author for non-commercial and academic purposes, and all requests will be promptly reviewed within 15 working days.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS).

stopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as described 
(43)
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-CycIF. 
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). 
All patients received neoadjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, followed by interval debulking surgeries (IDS), and none of them received PARP inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS
. The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to 
ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138) 
(44)
and absence of 
BRCA1
 or 
BRCA2
 germline or somatic mutation
. In addition, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial;

ssion-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.
For genomic alteration and number of HRD scar calculations, DNA was extracted from FFPE blocks and profiled using the Illumina SNP-array GSAv3. Allelic imbalances were detected using GenomeStudio (RRID:SCR_010973) and ASCATv3 (RRID:SCR_016868). The fraction of the genome altered was defined as the number of base pairs in segments larger than 1 kb with both scaled major and minor copy numbers different from 1, divided by the total length of the human genome. The tumor mutational burden was adopted from the iMTB reports (see iMTB report subsection).
Molecular profile assignment
For the TMA cohort, we first identified 
BRCA1/2 
mutation and 
BRCA1

(43)
. In brief, 
BRCA1/2 
germline mutation status for all patients was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR) database. For patients without a 
BRCA1/2
 germline mutation, both 
BRCA1/2

BRCA1 
promoter hypermethylation were assessed using DNA extracted from FFPE tumor samples. Targeted DNA sequencing was performed using BRCA MASTR Plus Dx kit (Agilent technologies, Santa Clara, CA, USA) and Illumina MiSeq, and methylation-specific multiplex ligation-dependent probe amplification (MLPA) was used to assess promoter hypermethylation. Details of the number of tumors with 
BRCA1 
or 
BRCA2 
mutations and 
BRCA1
 promoter methylation are reported in

.
Next, tumors without a

promoter hypermethylation underwent shallow whole genome sequencing (sWGS) using Illumina HiSeq2500 
at 0.5X

. In addition, using the sWGS, we performed HRD status estimation using the software shallowHRD (RRID:SCR_027714). Finally, we categorized the tumors as

For TCGA, we annotated the tumors as HRD and HRP based on ovaHRDscar (RRID:SCR_027138) 
(44)

Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA).

Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (
S.Table2
) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol 
(46)
. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images’ illumination was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371). The per-image tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266, 
https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar
). Individual TMA’s cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik (RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using 2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB (RRID:SCR_001622). Quality filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles (
https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite
), image regions with artifacts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells. 
For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex validated antibody panel (
S.Table13

acts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells. 
For the whole slide image (WSI) validation dataset, FFPE tissue sections were collected from 15 tumors. The slides were stained with a 14-plex validated antibody panel (
S.Table13
) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol. The image tiles were stitched with ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266). For WSI, we developed a more scalable analysis pipeline (
https://github.com/farkkilab/image_processing
). Firstly, the single-cell objects were segmented using nuclei segmentation in StarDist (RRID:SCR_027715), implementing the pre-trained model ‘
2D_versatile_flu
o''. Then, the mean intensity of each channel was quantified by our in-house script based on scikit-image package (RRID:SCR_021142). Image quality control was performed by CyLinter (RRID:SCR_021157).
Cell phenotyping
For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0043

vector_chunks.text:
g tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel's signal was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (
S.Fig.1c
) by using the flowSOM function QueryStarPlot. This annotation was performed firstly to identify global cell types (non-immune stromal and immune cells), and then to annotate immune subtypes (
S.Fig.1c
). All the cell phenotype assignments were validated via visual inspection using Napari (RRID:SCR_022765). After annotation, we quantified cell population proportions per TMA core and estimated the median proportion for each patient’s sample. 
For whole slide images, the cell type annotation was performed using unsupervised clustering with the software TRIBUS (RRID:SCR_027367) 
(47)
. Outliers were removed using the 99.9th percentile for input marker intensity, normalized via arcsin transformation with a cofactor of
5 
(48)
, and scaled by Z-score. TRIBUS was run with the following parameter settings of 
sigma=1
, 
learning_rate=1
, 
clustering_threshold=100
, 
undefined_threshold=0.0005
, 
other_threshold=0.4
. A total of 6 cell types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+ T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table (
S.Table13
) for all images when running Tribus.

, 
other_threshold=0.4
. A total of 6 cell types were annotated, including tumor, stromal, CD4+ T-cell, CD8+ T-cell, CD11c+ myeloid and IBA1+myeloid. We used a constant logic table (
S.Table13
) for all images when running Tribus. The exceptions are two images, which were manually gated with scimap (RRID:SCR_024751) due to nonspecific staining of CD8a in cancer cells. We corroborated our annotations by comparing them to cancer cell proportions determined by expert pathologist annotations using hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining, showing agreement across samples (
S.Fig.4
m
). The MHCII+ cancer cells were identified using manual thresholds and comparing the annotations to the t-CycIF images.
Cancer cell phenotype clustering
Firstly, we scaled the marker signals by the TMA slide and performed outlier-cell signal truncation, as mentioned in the previous method section. We perform dimensionality reduction on the cancer cells based on the expression levels of the markers: "Ki67", "Vimentin", "pSTAT1", "MHC-I", "ECadherin", "yH2AX", "pTBK1", "cPARP1", "CK7", "PDL1_488", and morphological features: "Area", "Eccentricity", and "Roundness" into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package flowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). The SOM-nodes were clustered using the R-package ConsensusClusterPlus (RRID:SCR_016954). For this, the number of clusters (
k
) was assessed from 2 to 12. For each value of 
k
 assessed, 500 iterations were performed, in each of the 80% of the SOM-nodes and 90% of the features (markers and morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm “partitioning around medoids” (pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, 
k

vector_chunks.text:
es (markers and morphological features) were randomly selected. The algorithm “partitioning around medoids” (pam) was used to cluster the selected dataset per iteration. After the iterations, we determined the best number of cancer meta-clusters, 
k
, by assessing the resultant consensus-clustering matrix for each value of 
k
 and its cumulative density distribution (
Ext.material Fig. 3
). The cancer meta-clusters were named according to the expression of relevant markers in each one (
Ext.material Fig. 2
).
Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis
For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap (RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k
=
40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick (RRID:SCR_027716) (
Ext.material Fig. 3
). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (
Ext.material Fig. 3
). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.
Xenium
spatial transcriptomics
The Xenium procedure was performed according to 10x standard protocol.

The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.
Xenium
spatial transcriptomics
The Xenium procedure was performed according to 10x standard protocol. Image processing and quality control were performed using the latest version of Xenium Ranger (v1.2). The tissue sections were then stained according to the tCycIF protocol 
(46)
 with PanCK (AE1/AE3, ThermoFisher, Catalog No. 757900382).
Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis
To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data, we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC, we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat's SCTransform function. 
Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an expert pathologist as ‘Cancer’, ‘TSI’ (tumor-stroma interface with partial tumor and stromal morphology), ‘Immune-Stromal’ and ‘other’. Spots annotated as ‘other’ have been excluded from the analysis. Within ‘Immune-Stromal’ spots, we have distinguished an additional type - ‘TSI2’, defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0047

vector_chunks.text:
efined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109). The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. We compared the distribution of the computed MHCII-signature UCell scores across different spot types, and by the median expression across spots in the samples regarding the response to chemotherapy. The categorization of tumors as good, partial or poor response was adopted from Böhm et al, 2015 
(49)
. An IFN-γ signature, obtained from The Molecular Signatures Database (MSigDB) hallmark gene set collection (RRID:SCR_016863), was also quantified per spot. Statistical significance has been computed using a two-sided Wilcoxon Rank Sum test. 
CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification
 CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0048
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ll data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled
, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0050

vector_chunks.text:
ells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
S.Fig.3
e
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of 
the mean decrease accuracy scores. By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the contribution of individual features to the model's accuracy.
 Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

val prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors. 
Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile
For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (RRID:SCR_003193) 
(2)
. The deconvoluted cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM 
(RRID:SCR_027136)
. The inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT (RRID:SCR_016955) were obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository.
RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment
For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463) from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1 in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33) expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0052
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
rom each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal PantherDB v19.0 (RRID:SCR_004869), we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the Reactome database (RRID:SCR_003485). 
For the analysis of RNA expression from Nanostring data (N=202), we first stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups according to the proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells using the t-CycIF dataset. We assessed the differential expression between the tumors with the lowest (below quantile 0.33, N=67) and highest (above quantile 0.66, N=66) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold change >=1 or Log2-fold change <=1 and FDR<1^-3. Overexpressed genes are reported in 
S.Table10
.
scRNA-seq analysis
We retrieved scRNA-seq gene count matrices from 51 HGSC samples, pre-processed and annotated 
(7)
, from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis, resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from 27 patients.

ated 
(7)
, from the Gene Expression Omnibus (GEO) database (accession code: GSE266577). Samples containing fewer than 40 cancer cells were ignored from the analysis, resulting in 39 samples selected for analysis (26 PDS and 13 IDS) from 27 patients. We assessed the activity of MHCII signature across cell types by computing the normalized Ucell score using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109) v1.3.1. The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: 
HLA-DMA
, 
HLA-DMB
, 
HLA-DOA
, 
HLA-DOB
, 
HLA-DPA1
, 
HLA-DPB1
, 
HLA-DQA1
, 
HLA-DQA2
, 
HLA-DQB1
, 
HLA-DRA
, 
HLA-DRB1
, 
HLA-DRB5
, 
CD74.
 Based on the MHCII Ucell scores, the cells were stratified into three equal-sized groups (no expression, low expression, positive MHCII expression). For each sample and cell type, pseudo-bulk gene expression profiles were generated using the function ‘
AggregateExpression’
 from the Seurat package (RRID:SCR_016341) v4.3.0. According to the pseudo-bulk MHCII UCell signature in cancer cells, the samples were stratified into three equal-sized groups: high, medium, and low, according to tertile distribution (
S.Fig.4c
). To assess the correlation between cancer-specific MHCII expression and IFN-γ in immune populations, pseudo-bulk expression of the 
IFNG 
gene was computed for each cell type per sample. Only immune cells were included in the correlation analysis, as they exhibited higher 
IFNG
 expression compared to fibroblasts or epithelial/cancer cells (
S.Fig.5l
). Pearson’s correlation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943).

orrelation was used for the analysis. Lastly, differentially expressed genes between the MHCII high (n=13) and low groups (n=13) in cancer cells were identified using pseudobulk sample expression and analyzed with the limma package (RRID:SCR_010943). Patient information was added to the design matrix and regressed out from the DEG analysis. 
In addition, we assessed the expression of immune markers (21 genes) across cancer cells (EOC) with high MHCII Ucell score and presence of CIITA expression and immune cells.
GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis 
We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy. Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 
(10)
, and is publicly available at
(https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
). The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0055
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
mony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499). This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0056
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
II gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.
Ligand-receptor analysis
To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq analysis). MultiNicheNetR (
https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git
) analysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–

vector_chunks.text:
cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Correlation p-values were adjusted for multiple testing.
Immunopeptidomics
We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
(50)
 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0058
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text: a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0059
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
e in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient 
was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0060
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher (https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant (https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0061
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with default settings. The threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using ArcasHLA (RRID:SCR_022286, 
https://github.com/roseorenbuch/arcasHLA-quant
) with default settings and following the documentation provided on GitHub. For the analysis, peptides were mapped to significantly correlated with MHCII tumor antigen genes from TCGA, scRNA-seq and spatial transcriptomics datasets (UBC, ADGR2, FAT1, FARSB, DNB1, CXADR, UBB did not have mapped peptides in the dataset), and patients were stratified by MHCII cancer proportion based on matching t-CycIF imaging into high (patients 1, 8, 11, 12), low (patients 2, 6, 7, 9) and medium (the rest of patients) groups. Genes unique to the high group were identified by comparing shared mapped genes across high and low groups and were set as a difference between the selected groups. Enrichment analysis of uniquely shared genes in the high group was performed using Enrichr (RRID:SCR_001575) (GO Biological Process 2025 and Cellular Component 2025) and the 10 top-enriched terms were recorded. These genes were also filtered based on their presence in the ligandome of healthy ovary tissues 
(31)
. Resulting 35 genes’ expression was studied in deconvoluted bulk RNAseq of the same 12 samples from iCAN study.
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq.

vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
in deconvoluted bulk RNAseq of the same 12 samples from iCAN study.
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq. We especially focus on the somatic mutation module (PCGR, 
https://github.com/sigven/pcgr
), which contains tumor purity and ploidy estimation, variant classification (somatic SNVs/InDels and copy number aberrations), and tumor mutational burden (TMB) estimation. TMB quantifies a tumor's total somatic mutations per megabase (Mb), and was computed with command “
-estimate_tmb
”, with other settings of “
tmb_dp_min
”, “
tmb_af_min
” and “
tmb_ad_min
” as default. Among the output of iMTB reports, tumor purity was used to verify the result of PRISM deconvolution. TMB was used to show the genomic alterations related to immune proportions. 
Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data
RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale factors, and cell-type–specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0063
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
umor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and immune–stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0064
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
d expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study 
(as described in previous section)
. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725), following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089).

vector_chunks.text:
he low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089). We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.
iPDCs flow cytometry analysis
To study how tumor MHCII affects the response to ICB, t
he dataset generated in Launonen et al., 2024 
(10)
 was used. Briefly, tumor tissue from HGSC patients in the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective cohort was dissociated, and iPDCs were established by embedding the cells in an omentum-derived matrix 
(32)
. Cultures were treated with immunotherapies for 2-4 days. Multicolor flow cytometry was performed at baseline (day 0) and after treatment, and the resulting dataset was made publicly available at 
(10)
. 
In this study, we reanalyzed the data by stratifying EpCAM⁺ cancer cells (single cells and aggregates) at day 0 into HLA-DR high and HLA-DR low groups based on baseline expression relative to HLA-DR expression in all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S.Fig.6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations.

all immune (CD45
⁺
) and myeloid cells (CD11c
⁺
 and CD14
⁺
) (
S.Fig.6
). MHCII-high tumors were defined as those in which the second peak of the bimodal HLA-DR distribution exceeded the median value observed in immune and myeloid cell populations. Drug-induced CD8⁺ T cell cytotoxicity was quantified as the log2 fold change in granzyme B expression in single cells relative to sample-matched untreated controls and compared between MHCII-high and MHCII-low tumor samples. The statistical significance was assessed by the Wilcoxon rank-sum test. Data analysis was performed using FlowJo v10.9.0 Software (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520) and custom R scripts.
To reveal how the blockade of MHCII affects the response to ICB, frozen tumor-derived samples from three patients with high HLA-DPB1 expression were selected from the ONCOSYS-Ova prospective HGSC cohort based on RNA expression in deconvoluted with PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) tumor fraction. The tumor tissue had been enzymatically dissociated prior to cryopreservation. For iPDC generation, thawed cells were embedded as droplets in an omentum-derived extracellular matrix 
(32)
. Cultures were treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) (RRID:AB_395939) or isotype control was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating.

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0067
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
ashed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating. Data were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and subsequently analyzed in R. Statistical analyses were performed in RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418, RRID:SCR_000432) using R (version 4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFN-γ, Granzyme B) along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition. Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons between conditions. For each patient, the median fluorescent intensity for the IFN-γ, and Granzyme B was calculated across all cells within each cell type and experimental condition. The resulting median intensity values were then used to calculate the log2 fold change for each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2 fold change comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. pembrolizumab, and anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. control. Finally, the mean of these individual patient log2 fold change values was calculated for each comparison across all patients to determine the pooled treatment effect.
Cytokine profiling via LEGENDplex
™
 assay
