Cytokine profiling via LEGENDplex
™
 assay
Supernatants from the cultures used to assess the effect of MHC-II blockade in the responses to pembrolizumab treatment were collected at the experimental endpoint (day 5), clarified by centrifugation (1500 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and stored at –80 °C until the time of assay. Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK Panel (13-plex) (BioLegend, catalog #741187, lot #B409342). The assay was carried out according to the manufacturer’s recommended protocol. Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa
TM
 cell analyzer (BD Biosciences), and the resulting data were processed using the LEGENDplex™ Data Analysis Software. Final plots were generated using custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
S.Fig.1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S.Fig.1b
).

vector_chunks.chunk_id: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx::chunk_0069
vector_chunks.source_file: 5. Writing and dissemination/Manuscript/Revisions_from_CancerDiscovery/For_submission_v2_122025/Final version rebuttal lines drawn from here Manuscript_attending_revision.docx
vector_chunks.text:
elected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S.Fig.1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3 (RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

FIGURE LEGENDS
Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.
a
 Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: 
BRCA
loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-
BRCA
wt), homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification (
CCNE1amp
), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles.

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
 A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated.

representing the strategy used to assign cells from t-CycIF images into different populations according to the expression of indicated markers.

Abstract
The tumor microenvironment in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration remains poorly understood. We present a single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Analyzing 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival.

entify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with immunopeptidomic analysis, confirms that MHCII expression correlates with immune activation, antigen presentation, and TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we demonstrate that tumor MHCII expression 
associates
 with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade, 
while blocking MHCII inhibits this activation
. Our atlas offers new insights into immune activation, potentially improving patient stratification in HGSC.
Statement of significance
This study delivers the first large-scale single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC, revealing how tumor–immune organization shapes outcomes. We identify tumor-intrinsic MHCII as a key driver of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness, providing a mechanistic biomarker that can immediately inform improved patient stratification and therapeutic decision-making in ovarian cancer.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions
(1)
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer
,
 characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
.

,
 characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 
BRCA1/2
 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
(4)
 and better responses to combinations with immunotherapies
(5,6)
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment
(7–9)
, resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
(10)
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (clinicomolecular) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and multiomic molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features.

We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by RAndom forest), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, 
and tumor reactivity 
impacting prognosis independently of clinicomolecular profiles. 
Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition ex-vivo.
 This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.

Multiomic profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors

The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=158). To validate the predictive role of cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression on prognosis across different molecular profiles, we performed an adjusted Cox regression on the multiomics cohort, including the tumors not assigned to a particular molecular profile and not used in the CEFIIRA analysis. In the entire cohort, a higher proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells consistently predicted longer OS(
Fig.3k
), further strengthening the link between MHCII expression in cancer cells and prolonged OS in HGSC, regardless of the molecular profiles or therapy sequence. To further corroborate the prognostic role of MHCII expression by cancer cells in a larger dataset, we extended our validation to The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA)

DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-omic spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 1026 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 280 real-world clinical samples. Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI neighbourhoods generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
(33,34)
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+T cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer
(35)
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
(36)
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ

r, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and

 expression are spatially correlated, and demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of neighbouring CD4+ and CD8+T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. 
These findings, summarized in 
Fig.7g
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
(37)
. 
The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to ICBs in various tumor types
(25,35,38,39)
. 
However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated disappointing results
(40)
. We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides 
linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to post translational modifications
(31)
with increased immunopeptide potential
(41)

analysis of immunotherapy responses in iPDCs suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. 
I
mportantly, we show that MHCII positive tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally dependent on MHCII.
 This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in 
Fig 7h.
Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1
(10)
.
In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the clinicomolecular subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. 
Regardless of the validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the multiomics cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to comorbidities or extent of disease
(42)
. 
Our protein marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types.

ertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to comorbidities or extent of disease
(42)
. 
Our protein marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our conclusions encourage future studies in prospective patient cohorts.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS).

stopathological data and formalin-fixed, paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (KvdV, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as described 
(43)
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-CycIF.

Highly multiplexed immunofluorescence and image preprocessing
To facilitate throughput and minimize experimental batch effects, tumor tissues were represented in tissue microarrays (TMA). To capture the whole tumor ecosystem, we sampled two cores from the tumor center and the tumor-stroma interface, resulting in a total of 1026 tissue spots. The TMAs were stained with a validated 30-plex marker panel (
S.Table2
) and scanned with a RareCyte CyteFinder scanner following the t-CycIF protocol 
(46)
. The panel selection was done hypothesis-based, aiming to capture the main cell populations present in the HGSC TME. The images’ illumination was corrected using the BaSiC tool (RRID:SCR_016371). The per-image tiles were stitched and registered using ASHLAR (RRID:SCR_016266, 
https://github.com/labsyspharm/ashlar
). Individual TMA’s cores were cropped using the Image Processing Toolbox on MATLAB (version 2018b, The MathWorks, Inc., Natick, Massachusetts, United States). Cell segmentation was performed using Ilastik (RRID:SCR_015246) v1.3.2 and CellProfiler (RRID:SCR_007358) v3.1.8 using 2-pixel nuclear expansion, and the median intensity of each channel and morphological features such as Area, Eccentricity, Solidity, Perimeter, and Roundness were quantified using MATLAB (RRID:SCR_001622). Quality filtering was applied based on changes in the DNA channel intensity across cycles (
https://github.com/yunguan-wang/cycif_analysis_suite
), image regions with artifacts, and TMA’s cores (n=64) with less than 1000 high-quality cells.

Cell phenotyping
For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package mclust (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide.

Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.01th percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel's signal was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package FlowSOM (RRID:SCR_016899). Recurrent cellular neighborhoods and spatial analysis
For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function tl.spatial_count of the package scimap (RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k
=
40 employing the tl.spatial_cluster function of the package scimap. Every cell was assigned to only one type of RCN. To select the value of k, we used the function KElbowVisualizer of the Python package YellowBrick
 (RRID:SCR_027716)
 (
Ext.material Fig. 3
). We used a radius of 30µm to identify recurrent neighborhoods of cells that are in physical contact. The replication of our analysis using a radius of 60µm and 90µm led to similar clusters, which shows the robustness of our approach (
Ext.material Fig. 3
). The proportion of each RCN within a tumor was calculated as the ratio of the number of cells in that RCN to the total number of cells in the tumor.

Visum spatial transcriptomics analysis
To perform the analysis of the MHCII signature activity in the proximity of the tumor, we utilized a publicly available Visium spatial transcriptomics dataset, composed of 8 IDS HGSC samples with different responses to chemotherapy (GSE211956). For spatial gene expression data, we performed the QC and preprocessing of input feature-barcode matrices in h5 format, using the Seurat R package (RRID:SCR_016341). For the QC, we excluded from the analysis the spots with less than 500 genes detected, more than 25% of mitochondrial genes or more than 20% of hemoglobin genes. We normalized the feature-barcode matrices using Seurat's SCTransform function. 
Based on H&E histological images, each spot was manually annotated by an expert pathologist as ‘Cancer’, ‘TSI’ (tumor-stroma interface with partial tumor and stromal morphology), ‘Immune-Stromal’ and ‘other’. Spots annotated as ‘other’ have been excluded from the analysis. Within ‘Immune-Stromal’ spots, we have distinguished an additional type - ‘TSI2’, defined as Immune-Stromal spots with the closest proximity to either ‘Cancer’ or ‘TSI’ spots. Next, we assessed the activity of MHCII-signature across the spot types by computing its normalized UCell score, using the R UCell package (RRID:SCR_027109).

The MHCII signature comprised of the following genes: HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74. CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification
 CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled

ll data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations 
to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled
, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the PyCaret library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
)
. We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: BRCAloss, HRD, HRP, and CCNE1amp.

ells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
S.Fig.3
e
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of 
the mean decrease accuracy scores. 
By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the contribution of individual features to the model's accuracy.
 Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

Deconvoluted RNA-expression and CIBERT sort profile
For validation of the importance of immunogenic signals (MHCII and MHCI), we used publicly available RNA-expression data from The Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) (RRID:SCR_003193) 
(2)
. The deconvoluted cancer-specific RNA expression was inferred with PRISM 
(RRID:SCR_027136)
. The inferred cell proportions by CIBERSORT (RRID:SCR_016955) were obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas repository.
RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment
For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_00446
3)
 from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-PanCanAtlas reposito
ry
. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1 in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33) expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3.

GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data analysis 
We retrieved GeoMx spatial transcriptomics data from 11 HGSC tumors obtained from interval debulking surgeries (IDS) following chemotherapy. Data generation is described in Launonen et al., 2024 
(10)
, and is publicly available at
(https://doi.org/10.7303/syn53283672
). The dataset includes 54 regions of interest (ROIs) from the tumor–stromal interface (TSI), each segmented into separate tumor and stromal compartments (areas of illumination, AOIs; n = 108). ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism

mony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied BayesPrism
(
https://github.com/Danko-Lab/BayesPrism
), a deconvolution tool developed for bulk RNA-seq but validated on spatial transcriptomics datasets (RRID:SCR_027499). This method not only estimates cell-type proportions but also infers transcriptional profiles for each cell type. As a reference for the deconvolution, we used raw scRNA-seq counts from the annotated ovarian cancer dataset GSE266577, along with raw GeoMx counts. To prepare the scRNA-seq reference, we downsampled the dataset in a patient-specific manner, yielding a final dataset of 24,560 cells and 33,458 genes. Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard BayesPrism workflow, with low-complexity genes filtered out and annotations provided for both ‘cell types’ and ‘cell states’. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (ssGSEA) scores for the MHCII gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058).

II gene set (HLA-DMA, HLA-DMB, HLA-DOA, HLA-DOB, HLA-DPA1, HLA-DPB1, HLA-DQA1, HLA-DQA2, HLA-DQB1, HLA-DRA, HLA-DRB1, HLA-DRB5, CD74) in the tumor AOIs, using both full and tumor-specific deconvoluted signals via the GSVA R package (RRID:SCR_021058). To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated ssGSEA scores with the estimated cell-type fractions (from BayesPrism) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.
Ligand-receptor analysis
To predict ligand–receptor interactions, we stratified scRNA-seq data based on MHCII UCell scores in epithelial/cancer cells, categorizing them into high, medium, and low groups (as described in the scRNA-seq analysis). MultiNicheNetR
(
https://github.com/saeyslab/multinichenetr.git
) analysis was performed to assess ligand–receptor interactions between immune cell populations (CD8⁺ T cells, CD4⁺ T cells, FOXP3⁺ regulatory T cells, DCs) and tumor cells (all possible directions),
comparing high and low MHCII UCell expression samples. Cell types included in the analysis were filtered to ensure adequate representation: a minimum of five cells per type in at least two samples per group. Genes were required to meet expression thresholds, with a minimum sample proportion of 0.5 and a non-zero expression fraction cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–

cutoff of 0.05. No covariates were included in the model. DE analysis was used to identify ligand and receptor candidates between cell types, applying thresholds of log fold-change (logFC) ≥ 0.75 and p-value ≤ 0.05. The resulting prioritized ligand–
receptor pairs were narrowed down to the top seven interactions enriched in the high MHCII group for immune-tumor interactions. These candidates were validated using the GeoMx spatial transcriptomics dataset, ensuring that both ligand and receptor were present. We correlated using Pearson correlation the previously calculated tumor deconvoluted MHCII ssGSEA score with ligand expression in whole stroma segments, as well as deconvoluted ligand expression in T cells and DCs derived from stroma. Immunopeptidomics
We performed immunopeptidomic profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from iCAN study, a national R&D flagship providing solutions to the cancer challenge through a discovery and innovation cluster at the interface of precision cancer medicine and digital health (more information can be found at www.ican.fi). MHC complex purification was performed as previously described 
(50)
 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 μm cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896).

a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (Thermo Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% Igepal (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an AminoLink Plus Immunoaffinity column (Thermo Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, RayBiotech, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession. The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300 μL of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.

e in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.
Dried peptides were reconstituted in ACN/0.1% FA prior to loading onto an analytical column (Aurora Ultimate CSI, 25 cm long, 75 μm inner diameter, 1.7 μm C18; IonOpticks). Chromatographic separation (NanoElute 2; Bruker) of peptides at a flow rate of 250 nl/min was performed with a stepped gradient ranging from 0 to 5% solvent B (0.1% FA/95% ACN) over 1 min, 5 to 40% B over 20 min, 40 to 60% B over 4 min, 60 to 95% B over 1 min and held at 95% B for 4 min. The chromatographic gradient 
was finalized by flushing the column with 95 to 40% B over 1 minute, 40% B for 2 min, 40 to 95% B over 1 min, 95% B for 3 min and then 95 to 0% B over 3 min. MS acquisition was performed on a TimsTOF Ultra (Bruker), with MS1 scans from 100 to 1700 m/z and ion mobility of 1/K0  =  1.45 Vs cm−2 to 0.64 Vs cm−2 in DDA-PASEF mode. Five PASEF ramps were acquired with an accumulation and ramp time of 100 ms. Precursors above the minimum intensity threshold of 500 were isolated with 2 Th at < 700 m/z or 3 Th >800 m/z and resequenced until a target intensity of 20,000, considering a dynamic exclusion of 40 s. The collision energy (CE) was lowered linearly as a function of increasing mobility, starting from 59 eV at 1/K0  =  1.6 Vs cm−2 to 20 eV at 1/K0  =  0.6 V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).

V cm−2. Standard polygon placement for trypsin-digested proteomic samples was used to exclude singly charged ions. Instrument performance was evaluated using a 10 ng injection of a commercial Hela proteome digest standard (ThermoFisher Scientific).
Peptide spectral data were analyzed using the FragPipe computational suite (RRID:SCR_022864, 
https://github.com/Nesvilab/FragPipe
). Searches were conducted against the UniProt human reference proteome (UP000005640), inclusive of isoforms (104,973 entries, retrieved on 2025-02-11), and a set of decoy sequences for validation purposes. MSFragger (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher (https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher)
,
 applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using IonQuant (https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_02543
4)

Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) report generation
Integrated Molecular Tumor Board (iMTB) reports were generated for the samples with whole exome sequencing and bulk RNA-seq. We especially focus on the somatic mutation module (PCGR, 
https://github.com/sigven/pcgr
), which contains tumor purity and ploidy estimation, variant classification (somatic SNVs/InDels and copy number aberrations), and tumor mutational burden (TMB) estimation. 
TMB
 quantifies a tumor's total somatic mutations per megabase (Mb), and was computed with command “
-estimate_tmb
”, with other settings of “
tmb_dp_min
”, “
tmb_af_min
” and “
tmb_ad_min
” as default. Among the output of iMTB reports, tumor purity was used to verify the result of PRISM deconvolution. TMB was used to show the genomic alterations related to immune proportions. 
Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from immunopeptidomics in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data
RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for immunopeptidomics using the Illumina NovaSeq 6000 platform with the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the iCAN study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale factors, and cell-type–specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients.

umor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and immune–stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from immunopeptidomics. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched t-CycIF imaging (as described in the Immunopeptidomics section). For each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction.

d expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.
TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in immunopeptidomics and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the iCAN study 
(as described in previous section)
. TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using MiXCR (RRID:SCR_018725), following the RNA-seq workflow (
https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘mixcr analyze rnaseq’, followed by ‘mixcr exportQc align’ and ‘mixcr exportQc chainUsage’ for quality control and chain usage statistics. As expected from bulk RNA-seq (non-enriched for T cells), a low percentage of reads successfully aligned. However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the immunarch R package (RRID:SCR_023089)
.

 We employed the ‘repExplore’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in Immunopeptidomics): 1) High MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 2) Low MHCII group: 6 samples (12 clone counts for TRAD and TRB); 3) Medium (the rest). Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

. Cultures were treated with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) on day 3. For combination treatment, anti-HLA antibody (2 µg/mL) (RRID:AB_395939) or isotype control was added on day 1, with Pembrolizumab (10 µg/mL) subsequently added on day 3. At the experimental endpoint (day 5), iPDC droplets were harvested by enzymatic dissociation to obtain single-cell suspensions. Cells were washed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Dead cells were excluded using a viability dye (DCM), and doublets were excluded based on FSC-A versus FSC-H gating.

ashed and stained with a multicolor panel for lineage (CD3, CD4, CD8, CD45, EpCam), and effector function (Granzyme B, IFNγ) markers. Data were acquired on a BD LSRFortessa flow cytometer using standardized PMT voltages and compensation controls. Downstream analysis was performed in FlowJo v10.10.0 (BD Life Sciences) (RRID:SCR_008520), applying a consistent gating strategy across all samples. Following gating in FlowJo, raw fluorescence data were exported as CSV files and subsequently analyzed in R. Statistical analyses were performed in RStudio (version 2025.09.2 Build 418, RRID:SCR_000432) using R (version 4.5.1). Raw fluorescence intensities exported from FlowJo were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFN-γ, Granzyme B) along with associated metadata, such as patient id and condition. Two-sided Mann-Whitney U tests were used for statistical comparisons between conditions. For each patient, the median fluorescent intensity for the IFN-γ, and Granzyme B was calculated across all cells within each cell type and experimental condition. The resulting median intensity values were then used to calculate the log2 fold change for each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2 fold change comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. pembrolizumab, and anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. control. Finally, the mean of these individual patient log2 fold change values was calculated for each comparison across all patients to determine the pooled treatment effect.

Cytokine profiling via LEGENDplex
™
 assay
Supernatants from the cultures used to assess the effect of MHC-II blockade in the responses to pembrolizumab treatment were collected at the experimental endpoint (day 5), clarified by centrifugation (1500 rpm, 10 min, 4 °C), and stored at –80 °C until the time of assay. Secreted cytokines were quantified using the LEGENDplex™ Human CD8/NK Panel (13-plex) (BioLegend, catalog #741187, lot #B409342). The assay was carried out according to the manufacturer’s recommended protocol. Samples were analyzed using a BD LSRFortessa
TM
 cell analyzer (BD Biosciences), and the resulting data were processed using the LEGENDplex™ Data Analysis Software. Final plots were generated using custom R scripts.
Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages survminer (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
S.Fig.1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S.Fig.1b
).

elected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
S.Fig.1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3 (RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-Wallis test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Figure 1. Multiomics single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC.
a
 Study schematic. We analyzed 265 HGSC patients using multi-omics tumor profiling. Each tumor was categorized in one of four common HGSC profiles: 
BRCA
loss, homologous recombination deficient (HRD-
BRCA
wt), homologous recombination proficient (HRP) and Cyclin-E amplification (
CCNE1amp
), using methylation assay, targeted gene sequencing and shallow whole genome sequencing. The tumor microenvironment (TME) was assessed using tissue microarrays, comprising 1026 cores (spatial maps). High-dimensional imaging was employed to study the TME and extract single-cell spatial features. We developed a machine learning pipeline (CEFIIRA) to integrate the single-cell features and adjust for clinical covariates, aiming to predict features associated with survival and molecular profiles.

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
 A schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated.

h Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.

New data from functional ex vivo experiments confirming the mechanistic role of MHCII in T-cell activation
. using a novel platform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we demonstrated that MHCII-high tumors exhibit enhanced CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production.

latform of patient-derived immune-competent cultures (iPDCs) we demonstrated that MHCII-high tumors exhibit enhanced CD8+ T-cell cytotoxicity in response to PD-1 inhibition, and that MHCII blockade abolishes T-cell activation and cytokine production. These findings underscore the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII, offering valuable insights for optimizing immunotherapy strategies and improving patient outcomes in cancer treatment.
This work presents several key novelties and impactful findings. Unlike prior studies, which have only associated immune hotspot activation with prognosis, we establish a direct link between intra-tumoral immune hotspots and MHCII expression in cancer cells with single-cell spatial resolution. We show, for the first time, that tumor MHCII expression is spatially connected to localized immune activation and that immune cells adjacent to MHCII+ cancer cells are tumor-reactive and more activated. Additionally, our research reveals the spatially confined effects of tumor-stroma interface composition, independent of tumor molecular features. Crucially, we provide mechanistic evidence that tumors with high MHCII expression respond better to immune checkpoint blockade, with this effect abrogated by MHCII blockade. These findings open new therapeutic avenues, such as engineered immunopeptides or vaccines, and highlight the importance of MHCII as a biomarker for more effective patient stratification in HGSC. This work offers strong support for future clinical validation in both retrospective and prospective trials.

Reviewer #1 (Comments to the Author ):
Anniina Farkkila and colleagues report a single-cell spatial analysis of metastatic high-grade serous ovarian cancer from 280 patients, including 929 single-cell maps. The authors identified heterogeneous immune-environments at the tumor-stroma interface that are predictive of clinical outcomes. Among these predictive factors, the authors newly identified tumor cell MHCII expression as a major predictor of superior outcomes and validated their findings with independent datasets.
In addition to a technological tour of force and the use of an extensive cohort of patients, the main strength of the study lies in the identification of tumor-intrinsic IFNG-dependent MHCII expression as marker of T cell-mediated immune control of malignant progression in human ovarian cancer. This is novel and biologically interesting. In addition, the finding that lymphocyte (T and B cells) infiltration loses predictive value after chemotherapy opens new avenues to investigate the immunobiology of human ovarian cancer, the basis of which have been established for treatment-naïve tumors before clinical practices changed. Increased frequencies of immunologically cold tumors after chemotherapy suggest a deleterious effect of chemotherapy on existing anti-tumor immune responses.

In this manuscript, the authors employ multiplexed imaging along with other datasets to profile a large number of HGSC samples. Overall though I find this to be a strong manuscript that will be of interest to the HGSC and spatial biology communities.

We thank the reviewer for this comment. The adjusted Cox model for OS, adjusting for age, stage, therapy and molecular profiles is shown in 
Fig 3k,
 showing that tumor MHCII remains predictive with HR 0.76 (CI 0.62-0.93). To further clarify our approach, we confirm that CEFIIRA uses an established, cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) to 
identify the most informative predictors while adjusting for the clinicomolecular factors
. After feature selection, we compute permutation-based mean decrease in accuracy (MDA) scores, which quantify the contribution of each variable by measuring the drop in model performance when that variable is randomly permuted. This approach leverages out-of-bag samples to provide an unbiased estimate of variable importance. While CEFIIRA is not designed to produce traditional regression-based adjusted effect estimates (e.g., hazard ratios), the combination of RFECV and permutation importance allows us to assess the relative contribution of each feature 
while accounting for correlations among variables.

We thank the reviewer for raising this important point. We are very committed to ensuring that the cancer cell–specific signals we report are not confounded by spillover from immune cells. To address this, we have performed several new 
analyses in our scRNAseq and spatial proteomics data, and also produced Xenium single-cell transcriptomics data.
First, we quantified the degree of potential spillover in cancer cells and assessed its correlation with MHC-II signal, as suggested. We did not detect any correlation between CD3 or CD11b expression and MHC-II levels in cancer cells, and we now include these results in a new Supplementary Fig. 3g. Because high MHC-II expression could theoretically arise from a combined spillover of CD3+ and CD11b+ immune cells, we also tested the correlation between MHCII and the maximum signal of these two markers. Again, no association was observed (see top panel below). 
In a complementary analysis, we examined the correlation between CD11b signal in cancer cells and CD11b signal in neighboring immune cells (within 20 μm) and found a strong correlation (bottom left panel), confirming the sensitivity of our approach to detect spillover when it occurs. In contrast, this level of correlation was not observed between MHC-II in neighboring immune cells and cancer cells (bottom right panel), indicating that cancer cell MHC-II expression is not solely explained by spillover. Moreover, in sc-RNAseq, we demonstrated that there are no expressions of immune related genes in cancer cells expressing MHCII (new Supplementary Fig. 4c).

Prediction models performed better in HRP-like tumors than in HRD-like counterparts. Is this explained by technical or biological factors?
We thank the reviewer for pointing out this observation. We evaluated both technical and biological explanations for the observed better performance of prediction models in HRP-like tumors. CEFIIRA was built to minimise technical bias through i) strict sample-wise data partitioning (preventing leakage), ii) stratified splits balanced by therapy and molecular subtype, iii) nested cross-validation with parameter tuning, iv) independent validation on unseen data, and v) repeated (30×) consensus estimation. These controls make it unlikely that technical artifacts explain the performance difference.
We also examined whether intrinsic biological heterogeneity might contribute to the performance gap in OS predictions. To assess this, we quantified the variability of CEFIIRA input features within HRP-like and HRD-like tumors by computing Shannon entropy and the Jensen–Shannon (JS) divergence between the two groups (see graph of Shannon entropy and Table with below). Using permutation testing (50 permutations), we did not detect high differences in entropy or JS divergence for these features, suggesting that 
overall feature variability does not explain the performance discrepancy supporting the robustness of our approach.
These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to account for the observed differences in predictive performance. The most compelling explanation points to intrinsic biological differences between HRP-like and HRD-like tumors.

These results strongly suggest that technical factors are unlikely to account for the observed differences in predictive performance. The most compelling explanation points to intrinsic biological differences between HRP-like and HRD-like tumors. Remarkably, MHCII stood out as the top feature, exhibiting the highest entropy difference between the HRD and HRP groups, with significantly elevated values in HRD-like tumors. 
This underscores the substantial biological heterogeneity within HRD-like tumors, further reinforcing the power of our integrative approach in uncovering critical, clinically relevant features from our multi-omics dataset. 
We have also added this result into the manuscript and Extended materials (lines

We thank the reviewer for this question. CEFIIRA can technically accommodate correlated features and does so through RFECV-based feature selection and permutation importance, both of which evaluate feature contributions while accounting for multicollinearity. However, we chose to build separate models for tumor and immune cells primarily for 
interpretability
. When multiple cell types are combined into a single feature table, CEFIIRA identifies the most predictive features across the entire dataset without distinguishing whether a given feature originates from tumor or immune compartments.

, 
we now utilized a blocking antibody to inhibit MHCII to investigate the dependency of T cell activation on MHCII signalling. Prior to the experiment, we confirmed that the iPDCs contained minimal contributions of myeloid cells on the day of readout (day 5, Figure below shown for three iPDCs). 
Notably, in our new data, MHCII blockade completely abolished PD-1-induced intracellular interferon (
new Fig. 7d
) and Granzyme B (
new Fig. 7e
) expression in multiple T cell subsets. Additionally, multiplexed cytokine profiling of supernatant from the iPDCs confirmed the loss of extracellular cytokine production after MHCII blockade (
new Fig. 7f
). 
These functional assays demonstrate the critical role of MHCII in driving T-cell activation and local immune responses, supporting the mechanistic dependency of T-cell activation on MHCII. 
This has been added also to manuscript lines 
563-571
).

Abstract
The tumor microenvironment in high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma (HGSC) is a complex network of malignant–host cell interactions, yet its orchestration remains poorly understood. We present a single-cell spatial atlas of metastatic HGSC from 280 patients, integrating high-dimensional imaging and molecular profiling. Analyzing 929 single-cell maps, we identify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival.

entify spatial domains with diverse cell compositions and show that immune cell co-infiltration at the tumor-stroma interface impacts clinical outcomes. Using CEFIIRA, we find that tumor cell MHCII expression is a key predictor of prolonged survival. Validation with deconvoluted, single-cell, and two distinct spatial transcriptomic datasets, along with 
immunopeptidomic
 analysis, confirms that MHCII 
expression correlates with immune activation, antigen presentation, and TCR clonality. Using a patient-derived immuno-oncology platform, we demonstrate that tumor MHCII expression associates with increased CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity after PD-1 blockade, while blocking MHCII inhibits this activation. Our atlas offers new insights into immune activation, potentially improving patient stratification in HGSC.
Statement of significance
This study delivers the first large-scale single-cell spatial atlas of HGSC, revealing how tumor–immune organization shapes outcomes. We identify tumor-intrinsic MHCII as a key driver of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness, providing a mechanistic biomarker that can immediately inform improved patient stratification and therapeutic decision-making in ovarian cancer.
INTRODUCTION
The tumor microenvironment (TME) is a complex ecosystem comprising cancer cells, immune cells, stromal cells, and non-cellular components and their interactions
(1)
. The continuous crosstalk between tumor cells and the surrounding microenvironment is fundamental to tumor initiation, phenotypic changes, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
.

anges, cancer progression, and therapeutic resistance. Ovarian high-grade serous carcinoma (HGSC) is the most prevalent and aggressive subtype of ovarian cancer, characterized by high genomic instability
(2)
 and significant tumor heterogeneity
(3)
. The genetic diversity of HGSC tumor cells significantly influences TME. For instance, tumors with a homologous recombination (HR) DNA repair deficiency, such as those with 
BRCA1/2
 mutations, have been suggested to be more immunogenic and exhibit higher lymphocyte infiltration
(4)
 and better responses to combinations with immunotherapies
(5,6)
. Moreover, we and others have shown that exposure to neoadjuvant chemotherapy significantly shapes the tumor ecosystem by enhancing the intricate interactions within the immune compartment
(7–9)
, resulting in spatially confined T-cell exhaustion
(10)
. Therefore, it is crucial to decode the complex relationships between clinical and molecular (
clinicomolecular
) features and the tumor ecosystem to improve patient stratification and immunotherapeutic treatment strategies for patients with HGSC.
We developed a high-resolution single-cell spatial atlas capturing 15.1 million single cells from 280 advanced HGSC patient samples with comprehensive clinical data and 
multiomic
 molecular profiling leveraging DNA methylation, gene copy number, and mutational profiling. Using a tailored image analysis approach, we resolved the spatial TME at the single-cell level, identifying coordinated multicellular domains that associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by 
RAndom
 forest
), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features.

associate with patient outcomes. We employed a custom machine learning tool, CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by 
RAndom
 forest
), to integrate single-cell spatial, molecular, and survival data to uncover clinically relevant features. We highlight the pivotal role of cancer cell-intrinsic MHC class II (MHCII) expression specifically at the tumor-stroma interface, which associates with distinct patterns of immune infiltration. We show that MHCII-positive cancer cells generate immune hotspots marked by increased activation, and tumor reactivity impacting prognosis independently of 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. Importantly, MHCII drives T cell activation after PD-1 inhibition ex-vivo. This atlas decodes the interplay between tumor and immune cells, shedding light on mechanisms linked with prognosis and paving the way for targeted interventions in HGSC.
RESULTS
Multiomic
 profiling and single-cell spatial mapping of HGSC tumors
To uncover the relationship between the TME ecosystem topography, clinical prognosis, and molecular profiles in HGSC we applied 
multiomic

Fig.1a
). The tumor tissues were obtained from debulking surgeries, either from treatment-naive patients (Primary debulking surgery, PDS n=100) or after platinum-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy (Interval debulking surgery, IDS n=158). Detailed clinical data, including long-term clinical outcomes, were collected, with a median follow-up of 38.2 months (IQR: 22-67 months) (

Spatial TME domains associate with distinct molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure 
To map the spatial TME domains, we clustered the 4,644,485 single cells based on their spatial neighborhood characteristics (see Methods). We uncovered 14 phenotypically distinct spatial clusters, hereafter referred to as recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCN;
Fig.2e-g
). Four types of spatial domains were identified containing stromal (RCN01 to RCN03), cancer (RCN04 to RCN08), immune and stromal cells adjacent to cancer cells (annotated tumor-stroma interface, TSI; RCN09 and RCN10) and immune neighborhoods (RCN11 to RCN14). The stromal spatial domains included a large immune-cold stromal domain (RCN01) and an endothelial cell-rich domain (RCN03;
Fig.2e-g
). The cancer spatial domains included the phenotypically most heterogeneous 
neighbourhood
 (RCN04), and four 
neighbourhoods

tionally coordinated in HGSC.
We next characterized the relationships between RCNs and the 
clinicomolecular
 profiles. The overall comparisons did not reveal significant differences in the abundances of the RCNs after multiple hypothesis correction. However, of the two TSIs, being the most heterogeneous RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) was enriched in 
BRCA
loss

Fig.2
i,j
). Interestingly, comparison of the spatial domains across therapy sequences further showed enrichment of stromal-non endothelial 
neighbourhoods
 (RCN01, RCN02), and immune-cold TSI (RCN09) in the chemotherapy exposed IDS samples, and proliferative tumor cell 
neighbourhood
 (RCN7) in the PDS samples (
Fig.2k
). Further, the cancer heterogenous 
neighbourhood
 (RCN4) was less abundant in the IDS samples (
Fig.2k
) suggesting phenotypic convergence of cancer cell populations following chemotherapy, consistent with our previous observation
(7)
. These results highlight distinct spatial TME domain topographies associated with the molecular profiles and chemotherapy exposure. 
Tumor-stroma interface composition associates with distinct clinical outcomes
Among all the RCNs, the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) exhibited the highest diversity (Shannon diversity index: 2.3,

Fig.2l

(4)

BRCA

of the immune system (

Fig.2m
). 
In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes within the HGSC TME.

In summary, these findings suggest a role for the multicellular spatial domains and immune cell infiltrations in shaping the clinical outcomes within the HGSC TME. Among these, the TSI emerged as the most diverse spatial domain, exhibiting divergent phenotypic compositions. The intricate interplay between immune co-infiltration, spatial organization, and 
clinicomolecular
 characteristics suggests a coordinated orchestration of these processes. This emphasizes the necessity of integrative approaches to uncover the key features associated with the observed spatial phenotypes within the HGSC TME.
CEFIIRA enables the discovery of clinically relevant single-cell spatial tumor ecosystem features
To reveal the biological processes underlying spatial immune infiltration patterns and prognosis, we developed CEFIIRA (Cell Feature Importance Identification by 
RAndom
f
orest
), a robust and generalized machine learning (ML) framework (
Fig.3a
). The primary objective of CEFIIRA is to identify the most distinguishing features within high-dimensional single-cell data that differentiate between two sample groups. CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features (see Methods). Additionally, as a versatile, open-source software CEFIIRA can integrate multidimensional data from each sample, such as clinical information from the patient and molecular profiles (
Fig.3a
).
We aggregated the multidimensional spatial data for each of the 4,644,485 single cells, including the phenotype and functional marker expressions (
Fig.1
e,i
;
Fig.
S
1g
), morphological features and spatial neighborhoods (
Fig.1j

ofiles (

).

), resulting in 43 feature representations per cell (Materials and Methods). To uncover multicellular features that differentiate the TME in terms of OS, we stratified the patients into three equally sized groups: long, medium, and short OS (
Fig.

). We generated CEFIIRA models for either cancer cells or the immune-stromal compartment separately in HRD-like (
BRCA
loss
, HRD) or HRP-like (HRP, 
CCNE1
amp), and in PDS or IDS tumors, respectively, also including age and tumor stage as potential confounders. This strategy made it easier to interpret the predictive signal and dissect the cell-type–specific contributions. As expected, the patient's age and tumor stage were the top predictive features of long-OS for most of the models (
Fig.

-

),
which was not explained by overall feature variability (
Table S9
)
. 
These results

e

adnex

Cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression is a key feature associated with overall survival 
Within the HRP tumors, CEFIIRA identified cancer-cell intrinsic MHCII as one of the key features for OS prediction across the samples (
Fig.
S5
c
-
e
). Moreover, cancer cell-specific MHCII expression showed the highest fold-change enrichment from long-OS patients compared to short-OS patients in HRP tumors (
Fig.3
d,e
; Table
 S
8
). By contrast, stromal markers Vimentin, α-SMA in PDS samples, and immune checkpoint receptor PD-1 in IDS samples were enriched in tumors with shorter OS. In addition, CD3d expression was a predictive marker of long-OS in PDS samples (
Fig.3d
). The prevalence of stromal and immune markers in cancer cell models suggested the relevance of signals from neighboring cells at the TSI. However, the MHCII levels in cancer cells did not correlate with CD3 or CD11b levels, confirming that this signal is 
not due to technical spillover from neighboring T cells or myeloid cells (
Fig.
S5
g
). Indeed, visual inspection of the t-
CycIF
 images confirmed MHCII expression by cancer cells (
Fig.3f
). We also performed Xenium single-cell spatial transcriptomics, which confirmed the co-localization of CD74, a transcript part of the MHCII complex, specifically on the tumor cells (
Fig.3f

Cancer cell MHCII expression is spatially coordinated and associated with immune cell proximity
To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and the spatial organization of the TME, we analyzed cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression across the cancer cell spatial domains (RCN04 to RCN08). Our analysis revealed high levels of MHCII expression within RCN05 (

Fig.1l

We next explored the relationship between the cancer cell MHCII expression and the cell composition at the TSI. Comparison of the two TSI spatial domains revealed a significantly higher expression of MHCII in cancer cells within the immune-infiltrated TSI (RCN10) as compared to the immune-cold TSI (RCN9;

Cancer MHCII is associated with distinct antigen presentation, increased TCR diversity 
Canonically, MHCII complexes are stable on the cell surface only when antigen-loaded, and typically present exogenous antigens or endogenous misfolded proteins
(29)

Together, these findings suggest that cancer cell-intrinsic antigen presentation via MHCII is an active process with the presentation of distinct antigens and underlying spatial immunogenicity contributing to T-cell activation in HGSC. Tumor-intrinsic MHCII may therefore contribute to improved anti-tumor immunity, particularly in the context of PD-1 ICB.
