DISCUSSION
We constructed a multi-
omic
 spatial atlas of the HGSC tumor ecosystem using 
1026
 comprehensive maps of the multicellular TME derived from 
265
 real-world clinical samples. Using our machine learning framework CEFIIRA, we integrated high-dimensional single-cell data and uncovered MHCII as a key prognostic marker in HGSC. Through spatial profiling of ~15.1 million cells across two independent cohorts, we show that MHCII+ cancer cells within the immunogenic tumor and TSI 
neighbourhoods
 generate immune hotspots. The hotspots showed evidence of coordinated immune activity with enhanced cellular interactions of MHCII+ cancer cells, particularly with T cells and antigen-presenting cells. Interestingly, CD4+ T cells have been found to recognize MHCII+ cancer cell antigens in melanoma and breast cancer-promoting immune surveillance
(33,34)
, and the spatial contact between MHCII+ cancer cells and CD8+T cells was reported recently in triple-negative breast cancer
(35)
. While IFN expression in tumors has traditionally been linked to immune surveillance, recent evidence suggests it may also play a role in immune escape mechanisms
(36)
. Through a robust spatial TME analysis, we found that MHCII+ cancer cells preferentially cluster spatially within immune-reactive cancer-cell neighborhoods and the TSI with augmented IFN activity. Moreover, the cancer cell MHCII correlated with IFN produced by antigen-experienced CD4+ and CD8+ memory T cells, implying that the MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of

he MHCII tumor cells can actively present antigens and activate the local TME. Furthermore, we identified that MHCII and 
IFN-γ
 expression are spatially 
correlated and
 demonstrated that cancer-intrinsic MHCII expression induces IFN activation of 
neighbouring
 CD4+ and CD8+T, and antigen-presenting myeloid cells at the TSI. These findings, summarized in 
Fig.7g
, support the hypothesis that antigen presentation is linked to activated CD4+ and CD8+ T cells to produce IFN, resulting in augmented immune activation at the TSI associated with favorable prognosis in HGSC. 
MHCII loci in cancer cells have been reported previously to be under evolutionary pressure in several types of cancer through variation in antigen presentation 
(37)
. The presence of MHCII+ tumor cells has been linked to an improved response to ICBs in various tumor types
(25,35,38,39)
. However, clinical trials using single-agent PD-1/PD-L1 inhibitors in unselected patient populations of ovarian cancer have demonstrated 
disappointing results
(40)
. We showed that MHCII-high tumors presented distinct immunopeptides linked to cytoskeleton, cell migration and adhesion, which are common subjects to post translational modifications
(31)
 with increased immunopeptide potential
(41)
. The proximity to MHCII tumor cells associated with CD8+T cell tumor reactivity, independent of MHCI, and higher TCR clonality, supporting the role of MHCII in immunogenicity of HGSC. Our 
ex vivo 
analysis of immunotherapy responses in 
iPDCs
 suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples.

s of immunotherapy responses in 
iPDCs
 suggests that stratification of patients based on MHCII expression might enhance the efficacy of immunotherapies in HGSC by CD4-derived priming of cytotoxic activity of CD8+ T cells in MHCII-high tumor samples. Importantly, we show that MHCII positive tumors respond to PD-1 inhibition which is functionally dependent on MHCII. This mechanistic interplay is illustrated in 
Fig
.
7h.
 Additionally, our study revealed that other immune checkpoints, such as TIGIT and CTLA4, were also overexpressed in tumors with high MHCII expression, further emphasizing the need for further clinical exploration of ICBs beyond PD-1/PD-L1
(10)
. In conclusion, our single-cell spatial atlas uncovers potential mechanisms associated with immune surveillance across the 
clinicomolecular
 subtypes of HGSC, providing a rationale for future therapeutic approaches and patient stratification.
Study limitations
We acknowledge certain limitations in our study, highlighting the opportunities for future refinement and exploration. Regardless of the validation in external datasets including whole slide images, the 
multiomics
 cohort on a TMA limits the conclusions on larger tissue structures such as tertiary lymphoid structures. Moreover, in our real-world cohort, IDS was associated with a shorter OS likely due to comorbidities or extent of disease 
(42)
. Our protein marker panel has inherent limitations in representing all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data.

senting all potential cell types. While our transcriptomic findings were validated in independent datasets, there is potential to enhance the accuracy of deconvolution methods, as well as to have matched single-cell proteomic and transcriptomic data. For our machine learning models, incorporating robust approaches to address correlated traits could further improve predictive performance. Lastly, our conclusions encourage future studies in prospective patient cohorts.

MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description

H. provided materials, performed study conceptualization, and drafting of the manuscript. A.F. performed study conceptualization and design, interpretation of results, writing of the manuscript and supervision.
MATERIALS & METHODS
Cohort description
We collected tumor samples from 265 HGSC patients with FIGO stage IIb-IV tumors from the Netherlands Cancer Institute - Antoni van Leeuwenhoek Hospital (NKI-AVL), Maastricht University Medical Centre (MUMC) and Amsterdam University Medical Centre (AUMC)), between January 2008 and December 2015. The patients were treated with primary cytoreductive surgery (PDS) or with neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by interval debulking surgery (IDS). All patients received adjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, and none of them received Bevacizumab or PARP inhibitors as maintenance therapy. Clinical data was extracted from the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR). Histopathological data and formalin-fixed, 
paraffin-embedded (FFPE) tissue blocks were obtained from the nationwide network of the Dutch Pathology Registry (PALGA).
Tissue blocks originated from tumors retrieved during debulking surgeries (PDS or IDS). The histology of all tumors was confirmed by three dedicated pathologists (
KvdV
, HMH). Of the samples, 70% were extracted from primary tumors (ovaries, tuba or adnexal sites). FFPE blocks were organized into a single tissue microarray (TMA) with one-millimeter diameter cores targeting two representative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as described 
(43)

tative cores from the tumor and two from stromal-rich areas, and with an average of four cores per patient’s sample. Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining was done on all cores to assess CD8+, CD20+, CD68+, and CD103+ cell expression as described 
(43)
 and used for pathologist annotations to validate the immune infiltration detected with t-
CycIF
. 
Whole slide cohort description
We utilized HGSC tumors from 15 patients participating in the ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial (NCT06117384). All patients received neoadjuvant platinum-based chemotherapy and paclitaxel, followed by interval debulking surgeries (IDS), and none of them received PARP inhibitors. The samples were obtained from the IDS. The selection criteria for these samples included HR-proficient status according to 
ovaHRDscar
 (RRID:SCR_027138) 
(44)
 and absence of 
BRCA1
 or 
BRCA2
 germline or somatic mutation. In addition, a tumor purity greater than 10%, patients exhibiting a progression-free survival (PFS) of more than 6 months and a tumor macroscopic residual disease (R > 0cm) after surgery. The median PFS for patients with HRP tumors that underwent IDS therapy sequence is 11.7 months in the entire ONCOSYS-Ova clinical trial; based on that we selected a cutoff of 12 months to define long-PFS (>=12 months) and short-PFS (<12 months) in the survival analysis.
For genomic alteration and number of HRD scar calculations, DNA was extracted from FFPE blocks and profiled using the Illumina SNP-array GSAv3. Allelic imbalances were detected using 
GenomeStudio
 (RRID:SCR_010973) 
and ASCATv
3 (RRID:SCR_016868).

Cell phenotyping
For the TMAs’ cell phenotyping, we first classified cells as either cancer or non-cancer on a per-tumor basis. For this, the cells were clustered based on the Z-score scaled signal of epithelial markers (Cytokeratin 7 and E-cadherin) using a Gaussian mixture model from the R-package 
mclust
 (RRID:SCR_027153). Two Gaussian clusters were fit, and cells in the higher epithelial marker expression cluster were labeled as cancer cells. The clustering was manually reviewed per tumor, and poorly performing tumors (n=10) were excluded. Subsequently, to avoid the detected batch effects, the annotation of non-cancer cells was performed for each TMA slide. Outliers above the 99.9th percentile and below the 0.
01st
 percentile were adjusted to these values. Each channel's signal was Z-score scaled, and non-cancer cells were unsupervised-clustered by marker intensity with high granularity into a 2500-node self-organized map (SOM) using the R-package 
FlowSOM
 (RRID:SCR_016899). For each SOM node, we computed cell-type label scores according to the scaled expression of makers per expected cell phenotype (

S

For each cell from the collection of 253 tumors that passed quality control, we computed the fraction of neighboring cell types in a radius of 30µm, using the function 
tl.spatial
_count

scimap
 (RRID:SCR_024751). We clustered the cells based on the neighborhood information to generate recurrent cellular neighborhoods (RCNs), by using k-means and a k
=
40 employing the

UCell

CEFIIRA: Machine learning supervised classification
 CEFIIRA robustly minimizes bias in both ML model creation and the identification of critical features by: 1) Using ML model parameter tuning and feature importance evaluation in cross-validation, training and testing the model on multiple data partitions to prevent overfitting, 2) Ensuring that both the training and test sets have a proportional and balanced representation of samples from each class, 3) Validation of ML models on a prior defined validation set with unseen data, 4) Partitioning single-cell data in a sample-wise manner to minimize data leakage, 5) A robust identification of critical features using feature elimination and feature permutations to elucidate how accuracy is affected when the values of one variable are randomly shuffled, 6) Performing the previous steps over 30 iterations (nested cross validations) to achieve a consensus estimation.
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score);

ions) to achieve a consensus estimation.
The input to models consisted of single-cell data, where each cell had the following pre-processed features: 1) log2-transformed protein marker expression data, scaled by slide using standard scaler (z-score); 2) three morphological features (area, eccentricity and roundness), scaled using standard scaler; 3) 14 features for the proportion of neighbors in a 30μm radius, one feature per annotated cell type; additionally when the target for prediction was clinical outcomes like short- or long-OS the model included patients’ clinical information – FIGO stage and age at diagnosis. For each analysis, cells with marker and morphological feature values outside the 0.01 to 0.99 quantile range were excluded. In addition, samples were excluded if they had less than 100 Cancer, or Immune-Stromal cells. For the creation of the subsequent machine learning models, to prevent group leakage, stratified sampling was employed, ensuring patients were divided 80% into training and 20% into the test sets, maintaining balance across classes.
To identify the most suitable supervised machine learning (ML) algorithm for classifying single cells into molecular profiles, we used the 
PyCaret
 library on the entire dataset (RRID:SCR_021695, 
https://github.com/pycaret/pycaret
). We employed different ML algorithms for multiclass predictions of: 
BRCAloss
, HRD, HRP, and 
CCNE1amp. Based on 
PyCaret
 runs, we selected Random Forest (RF) as a suitable algorithm based on its mean performance (weighted-
averaged
 AUC and F1) on ten-fold cross-validations (
Table 
S

Based on our comparisons, we created an RF pipeline using the Python scikit-learn library (RRID:SCR_002577) for binary classification of cells into 
clinicomolecular
 groups (
https://github.com/farkkilab/CEFIIRA
). The models' training process employs nested cross-validation, where the outer loop iteratively partitions tumors into training and test sets 30 times using the stratified sampling mentioned previously. We chose 
30 data
 partitions based on a balance between computational efficiency and model robustness, as in test series of 15, 30 or 40 data partitions we achieve similar results when classifying long vs. short overall survival based on cancer cells in chemo-naive (PDS) HRDs tumor samples (
Fig.
S5f
). For each nested cross-validation, using the training set, a Bayesian optimization was used to tune the hyperparameters of the model (
min_samples_split
, 
min_samples_leaf
, 
n_estimators
). The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations;

The Bayesian optimization process iteratively evaluates the combinations of hyperparameters’ values to find the ones that achieved the highest mean weighted F1 score across nine cross-validation iterations; for each iteration, the training set was divided into three-folds three independent times. Next, the created model is subjected to a cross-validated recursive feature elimination (RFECV) based on classification accuracy. This step enables identifying and extracting the most predictive features from the training set. We further assess the importance of these selected features in predicting the target variable through the computation of the mean decrease accuracy scores. By randomly shuffling the values of each feature and observing the resulting change in model performance, MDA scores quantify the contribution of individual features to the model's accuracy. Finally, we evaluate the performance of the created model on the test set partitioned in the outer loop (unseen data) and extract single-cell level predictions of target classes. The median performance and standard deviation of models across 30 nested cross-validations are reported. Independent models were created for two cell compartments (Cancer cells, Immune-stromal cells). For the 
survival prediction models, we stratified the patients into long-survival groups (above the 66th percentile of overall survival time in the cohort) and short-survival groups (below the 33rd percentile), independently for those with HRPs and HRDs tumors.

RNA differential expression analysis and pathway enrichment
For the analysis of TCGA bulk RNA-seq, we obtained per-sample gene read counts estimated with the software STAR (RRID:SCR_004463) from the Genomics Data Commons (GDC). The associated clinical information was also obtained from the GDC-
PanCanAtlas
 repository. We stratified the tumors into three equal-sized groups based on the expression of HLA_DPB1 in the deconvoluted Epithelial Ovarian Cancer (EOC) component. We assessed the RNA differential expression between the tumors in the group with the highest (above quantile 0.66) and lowest (below quantile 0.33) expression of HLA_DPB1 in the EOC component. We excluded the genes with less than 20 counts across the tumors from each assessed group. We used the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687) for count normalization and differential expression analysis. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the next criteria: Log2-fold change >=2, FDR < 1^-3. To identify over-represented pathways or sub-represented pathways, we used as input the list of over-expressed or sub-expressed genes independently. Then, using the web portal 
PantherDB
 v19.0 (RRID:SCR_004869), we performed a Fisher-exact test, taking as reference the 
Reactome
 database (RRID:SCR_003485).

Nanostring

N=66) proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. For the differential expression analysis, we computed per gene a two-sided T-test between the assessed groups, and the fold changes were estimated by calculating the difference in mean gene expression between the groups with the highest and lowest proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells. Genes were considered with differential expression according to the following criteria: Log2-fold change >=1 or Log2-fold change <=1 and FDR<1^-3. Overexpressed genes are reported in

ROIs were initially selected based on the co-localization of CD8+ T cells and IBA1+ macrophages. However, for this study, all available AOIs from the TSI (n = 108) were included regardless of immune cell presence. The data had undergone initial quality control prior to analysis. We performed normalization using the DESeq2 R package (RRID:SCR_015687), followed by variance-stabilizing transformation. Batch effects were corrected using the Harmony R package (RRID:SCR_022206). 
Due to the mixed cellular composition of each AOI, transcriptomic deconvolution was performed to estimate cell-type-specific gene expression profiles. We applied

Due to the limitations in resolving fine-grained immune populations, related cell types were grouped into broader categories. The final reference included: 9,168 T cells, 5,240 tumor cells, 4,364 macrophages, 2,335 fibroblasts, 1,515 B cells, 1,007 NK cells, 403 dendritic cells, 287 mast cells, 90 endothelial cells, and 151 other cells (including ILCs and late erythroid cells). Tumor cells were annotated separately per patient. Deconvolution followed the standard 
BayesPrism

states’
. 
We then computed single-sample gene set enrichment analysis (

To assess the relationship between MHCII expression and immune cell presence, we correlated 
ssGSEA

BayesPrism
) for each AOI. We first applied a simple linear model (y ~ exp(x)), followed by a model incorporating ‘Patient’ as a covariate (y ~ exp(x) | Patient) to account for non-independence of AOIs within the same tumor.

We performed 
immunopeptidomic
 profiling of MHCII peptides in 12 HGSC samples from 
iCAN

(50)
 with minor modifications. Frozen ovarian cancer tissue samples were slowly thawed on ice and mechanically dissociated into a single-cell suspension by mincing with a scalpel, followed by filtration through a 70 
μm
 cell strainer using the plunger end of a syringe. Cells were then subjected to gentle rocking incubation at 4 °C for 1.5 hours in a lysis buffer composed of 150 mM NaCl, 50 mM Tris-HCl (pH 7.4), protease inhibitors (
Thermo
 Scientific Pierce, A32955), and 1% 
Igepal
 (Sigma-Aldrich, I8896). The resulting lysate was initially cleared via centrifugation at 500 × g for 10 minutes, and the supernatant was subsequently spun at 25,000 × g for 30 minutes to remove debris. The clarified lysate was applied to an 
AminoLink
 Plus Immunoaffinity column (
Thermo
 Fisher), pre-packed with 2 ml agarose resin covalently coupled to 1 mg of anti-HLA-DR, DP, DQ antibody (clone IVA12, 
RayBiotech
, Inc.) using the manufacturer’s coupling protocol at neutral pH (7.2). To enhance antigen capture, the lysate was passed through the column twice in succession.

The column was then sequentially washed with five column volumes of the following buffers, in order: 1) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 2) 400 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; 3) 150 mM NaCl, 20 mM Tris-HCl; and 4) 20 mM Tris-HCl, pH 8.0. MHC class II-peptide complexes bound to the resin were dissociated, and peptides were eluted using 0.1 M acetic acid. Peptides, along with MHC subunits, were desalted using Sep-Pak C18 cartridges (Waters, WAT054960), which were preconditioned with 80% acetonitrile in 0.1% trifluoroacetic acid (TFA), followed by 0.1% TFA. Peptides were eluted in two rounds using 300

 of 30% and 40% acetonitrile in 0.1% TFA directly into polypropylene screw-cap vials (Waters, 186002626). The eluates were dried via vacuum centrifugation (Eppendorf) at 45 °C using the V-AQ setting until halfway dry, after which both eluates were pooled into a single sample.

MSFragger
 (https://github.com/Nesvilab/MSFragger) was employed for MHC peptide identification under default settings, with a few modifications: enzyme specificity was set to ‘non-specific’ and ‘null’, allowing up to two missed cleavages. Peptides were constrained to lengths between 7 and 30 amino acids, a mass range of 700–5000 Da, and charge states from +1 to +5. Only methionine oxidation (+15.9949 Da) was considered as a variable post-translational modification. Validation of spectral matches and PSM filtering was performed with Philosopher (https://github.com/Nesvilab/philosopher), applying a false discovery rate (FDR) cutoff of 0.05 at both the peptide-spectrum match (PSM) and peptide levels. Label-free quantification was carried out using 
IonQuant
 (https://github.com/Nesvilab/IonQuant), enabling between-run normalization while disabling the match-between-runs (MBR) option.
Peptides’ searches were confirmed by MHCII binding affinity prediction using the online version of NetMHCIIpan-4.1 (RRID:SCR_025434) with default settings. The 
threshold for strong binder (% Rank) was set to 1, while the threshold for weak binder (% Rank) was set to 5. Only peptides confirmed to be MHCII binders were considered for further analysis. Patient HLA-typing was performed from bulk RNA sequencing data (starting from .bam files) using

Analysis of genes with mapped peptides from 
immunopeptidomics
 in deconvoluted bulk RNA-seq data
RNA sequencing was performed on the 12 HGSC samples used for 
immunopeptidomics
 using the Illumina 
NovaSeq
 6000 platform with the Illumina Stranded Total RNA kit (Ribo-Zero Plus), targeting more than 100 million paired-end reads at 2x101 bp read length. Sequencing data were processed using the Illumina DRAGEN pipeline as part of the 
iCAN
 study. Bulk RNA-seq profiles were deconvoluted using PRISM (RRID:SCR_027136) to estimate cellular composition, sample-level scale factors, and cell-type–specific whole-transcriptome profiles for tumor, stromal, and immune compartments. Raw read counts were used as input for PRISM. As the single-cell reference, we used the HGSC single-cell RNA-seq dataset from 
(10)
, which includes tumor, stromal, and immune cell annotations from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile.

ons from 29 patients. PRISM produced gene-level read counts corresponding to the tumor, immune and stromal fractions for each bulk RNA-seq sample. After decomposition, immune and stromal components were combined to generate an immune–stromal profile. Scaled expression values for each gene and each compartment (tumor and immune–stromal) were computed using the PRISM-derived gain factors, which correct for technical variation such as sequencing efficiency and library preparation, and composition weights, which reflect the relative contribution of each compartment within a given sample. Scaled expression therefore represents the expression level expected for each cell type independent of its abundance in the bulk sample. Scaled expression values were extracted for the 35 genes identified from 
immunopeptidomics
. Patients were stratified into high- and low-MHCII cancer groups based on matched t-
CycIF
 imaging (as described in the 
Immunopeptidomics
 section). For each gene, the median scaled expression and median z-score of scaled expression were calculated within each patient group. Tumor specificity of gene expression in the MHCII-high group was then assessed by comparing the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment with the median scaled expression across both the tumor and immune–stromal compartments combined. This yields a value between 0 and 1, where values closer to 1 indicate expression predominantly in tumor fraction. Finally, fold change was calculated as the ratio of the median scaled expression in the tumor compartment of MHCII-high samples to the mean scaled expression in the tumor fractions of MHCII-mid and MHCII-low samples.

TCR repertoire analysis
We performed TCR repertoire analysis on bulk RNA-seq data from 11 HGSC samples used in 
immunopeptidomics
 and an additional 5 HGSC samples (total n = 16) originating from the 
iCAN
 study (as described in previous section). TCR analysis was based on reads mapped to chromosomes 2, 7, 14, and 22, which harbor TCR gene loci. TCR sequences were extracted using 
MiXCR

https://mixcr.com/mixcr/guides/rnaseq/
) with default settings. We used ‘
mixcr

However, all samples yielded identifiable TRA and TRB clones, with TRG clones detected in 15 of 16 samples and TRD clones detected in 7 of 16 samples (3 from the high MHCII group, 1 from the low group). Notably, B cell receptor sequences (IGH, IGK, IGL) were also highly represented, consistent with the bulk nature of the dataset. Clonal diversity of TRAD and TRB repertoires was assessed using the 
immunarch
 R package (RRID:SCR_023089). We employed the ‘
repExplore
’ function with the "volume" method to compare TCR clone counts between groups stratified by MHCII cancer proportion (similar to the stratification done in

Differences in clone numbers between groups high and low groups were evaluated using the Wilcoxon rank-sum test, with p-values adjusted for multiple comparisons using the Holm method.

 were processed for each cell type to extract marker expression (IFN-γ, Granzyme B) along with associated metadata, such

For each patient, the median fluorescent intensity for the IFN-γ, and Granzyme B was calculated across all cells within each cell type and experimental condition. The resulting median intensity values were then used to calculate the log2 fold change for each patient, cell type, and marker. Three distinct log2 fold change comparisons were performed: pembrolizumab vs. control, anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. pembrolizumab, and anti-HLA + pembrolizumab vs. control. Finally, the mean of these individual patient log2 fold change values was calculated for each comparison across all patients to determine the pooled treatment effect.

Survival analysis
To create survival plots and perform Log-rank tests and Cox regression models, we used the R packages 
survminer
 (RRID:SCR_021094) and survival (RRID:SCR_021137). For TMAs, cell population abundance was estimated by calculating the median abundance per cell type across sampled cores. The proportion of MHCII+ cancer cells was determined relative to the total cancer cells per tumor. Tumors with at least 5% cancer cells and more than one core post-preprocessing (n=233, 
Fig.
S
1b
) were selected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
Fig.

1b

lected for survival analysis when assessing the association of cancer cell phenotypes and clinical outcomes. For survival analysis using immune populations or RCNs, tumors were analyzed with more than one core post-preprocessing (n=244, 
Fig.
S
1b
). Patient stratification based on cell population used the cohort's median, defining high (above median) and low (below median) groups. For Cox models, tumors were split into three groups by abundance (below quantile 
0.33, between 0.33-0.66, and above 0.66). Stratification by gene expression followed the same approach. Cox models were adjusted for age, FIGO stage, and molecular profiles when applicable. For the TCGA dataset survival analysis, only high-stage HGSCs (III and IV) were included.
Statistical analyses
We performed statistical analysis using R version 4.2.3 (RRID:SCR_001905). The difference in abundance between the two groups was calculated using a two-sided Wilcoxon rank sum test, unless stated that it was one-sided. The difference in abundance across different groups was calculated using a two-tailed Kruskal-
Wallis
 test. The correlations between variables were assessed using Pearson correlations. For multiple testing, we adjusted P-values using the Benjamini and Hochberg method. P-values or adjusted P-values (FDR) less than 0.05 were considered statistically significant.

Ethics approval
For the present study, approval of the institutional review boards of the Netherlands Cancer Registry (NCR), PALGA, and NKI-AVL was obtained for the 265 patient cohorts. Informed patient consent was waived according to Dutch legislation due to the use of archival samples and pseudonymization of the material.  The whole slide tumor specimens were collected as part of the ONCOSYS-OVA study (NCT06117384) in accordance with the ethical standards from the 1975 Declaration of Helsinki. Each patient gave their written informed consent, and the use of samples and clinical data was approved by the local Ethics Committee (HUS334/2021). The 
iCAN
 Flagship project was reviewed by the HUS Ethical Committee and is executed based on a HUS research permit (4.5.2023 §38 (HUS/223/2023)).

Figure 3. CEFIIRA models identify cancer cell intrinsic MHCII expression as a key prognostic feature in HGSC 
a
A
 schematic illustrating our machine learning pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss

BRCA
loss

pipeline ‘CEFIIRA’. Firstly, we defined targets for binary predictions, for example, predicting if cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors. Data preprocessing is performed for the tumors related to the target (for example, 
BRCA
loss
 and HRP). Next, 30 nested cross-validation iterations are performed. In each iteration, 80% of the tumors (their corresponding cells) are randomly assigned to the training set, with the remaining 20% allocated to the test set. Subsequently, for each iteration, a random forest model is built using the training set, with several parameters optimized through Bayesian tuning and K-fold cross-validation. The trained model is validated on the test set to predict the target classes (for example, whether cancer cells belong to either 
BRCA
loss
 or HRP tumors) and its most predictive features are calculated. After completing the 30 iterations of nested cross-validations, the model’s median accuracy and consensus critical features are identified.

### word/document.xml
Point-by-point rebuttal letter
We thank the editor and reviewer for the final comments. We have now addressed all these and finished the manuscript edits accordingly. 
Ed
itorial comments
We have updated the article
 file, Figures and Supplementary material based on editors’ comments and the requirements of the journal.
Reviewer comment
Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author): 
The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only have one remark.
The authors present new data supporting the notion that MHCII is expressed by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. While this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments, I am wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the values for MHCII on cancer cells don’t line up between the different correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3).
 In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII vs max(CD3d or CD11b)
. Where are the different input cells used here? The authors should show this data for all tumor cells.
We thank the reviewer for this comment and agree that key aspects of the analysis were not sufficiently described previously, which may have caused confusion. This confusion was due to the downsampling of the data prior to analysis. We have now performed a new in-silico experiment supporting the validity of our downsampling approach, and added a clarification of the analysis details to the figure legend, as detailed in our response Importantly, we have now added a new 
Supplementary Figure 3g (new S5g)
 with further details of the downsampling, and added the statistics calculated using all cancer cells.

vector_chunks.text:
of the analysis details to the figure legend, as detailed in our response Importantly, we have now added a new 
Supplementary Figure 3g (new S5g)
 with further details of the downsampling, and added the statistics calculated using all cancer cells.
Our previous analysis was performed using a random downsampling of 500 cancer cells. We adopted this approach because when all cancer cells were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures below that include all the cancer cells. Importantly, according to the central limit theorem for correlated data, random sampling preserves the statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in Figures 2F and 2H. 
To assess the robustness of this downsampling strategy, we performed a new in-silico experiment. Downsampling was conducted 10,000 times, with 500 cancer cells sampled per replicate. For each replicate, the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) was calculated between the expression of the indicated markers and MHCII in the sampled cancer cells. The mean R across the 10,000 replicates is reported together with the corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI) in the next table. In addition, the mean p-value and its confidence intervals for testing the deviation of R from zero. As observed, the mean correlations and confidence intervals across replicates fall within the range of correlations previously reported in Supplementary Figure 3g and are consistent with results obtained using the full dataset.

We therefore consider that displaying a downsampling is a more faithful representation of the data for visualization purposes. We have now added a new 
Supplementary Figure 3g (new S5g)
 with further details of the downsampling, and added the statistics calculated using all cancer cells.

### word/document.xml
REVISION 
Fernando Perez
Reviewer #3 (Comments to the Author ): 
The authors have addressed most of my comments to satisfaction, I only have one remark.
The authors present new data supporting notion that MHCII is expressed by tumor cells and not detected due to spillover from imaging. While this is appreciated and supported by their functional experiments, I am wondering why in Supp 3g and in the point-by-point response, the values for MHCII on cancer cells don’t line up between the different correlation analyses (i.e. the correlations with CD11b, MHCII and CD3).
 In the MHCII in cancer vs. neighbor plot there are lots of cancer MHCII values between 11 and 15 while these are missing from the cancer MHCII vs max(CD3d or CD11b)
. Where different input cells used here? The authors should show this data for all tumor cells.
We appreciate the reviewer's comment, and we agree that we did not add important descriptions of our analysis before and this can lead to confusion. We added a clarification in the figure legend according to our next comments. 
Our previous analysis was performed using a random downsampling of 500 cancer cells. We adopted this approach because when all cancer cells were included (N ≈ 2 million), the high dispersion in the data precluded reliable visualization of correlated trends, as shown in the two figures below that include all the cancer cells. Importantly, according to the central limit theorem for correlated data, random sampling preserves the statistical properties of the original distribution. This principle has also been discussed and demonstrated in the context of spatial tumor profiling by Jia-Ren Lin et al. (2023; PMID: 36669472), specifically in Figures 2F and 2H.

Downsampling was conducted 10,000 times, with 500 cancer cells sampled per replicate. For each replicate, the Pearson correlation coefficient (R) was calculated between the expression of the indicated markers and MHCII in the sampled cancer cells. The mean R across the 10,000 replicates is reported together with the corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CI) in the next table. In addition, the mean p-value and its confidence intervals for testing the deviation of R from zero. As observed, the mean correlations and confidence intervals across replicates fall within the range of correlations previously reported in Supplementary Figure S5g and are consistent with results obtained using the full dataset. We therefore consider that displaying a downsampling is a more faithful representation of the data for visualization purposes. g
 Scatter plot showing the intensity of immune markers (CD3d and CD11b) versus MHCII intensity in cancer cells. The figure displays the Pearson correlation coefficient (R), the correlation test p-value, and the linear regression line (blue) based on a downsample of 500 cells. Downsampling was preferred for visualization purposes, when all cancer cells are included (N ≈ 2 million), the correlation with MHCII is R = 0.26 for CD11b and R = 0.25 for CD3d, respectively.

## Slide 2
Objectives of the study & their impact
The project aims to discover how the spatial tumor microenvironment (TME) and MHC dysregulation facilitate immune escape in ovarian cancer:
Objective 1.
Identify MHC patterns in HGSC subtypes and determine their clinical significance.
Using t-CycIF, we profiled single-cell spatial landscapes of HGSC in 265 samples and characterized cellular communities and their interactions. Through machine-learning–guided feature extraction, we identified distinct MHC dysregulation patterns associated with specific HGSC molecular subtypes and clinical outcomes.
Impact
This work demonstrates that tumor-intrinsic MHCII expression is a key determinant of local immune activation and immunotherapy responsiveness. The findings, validated across four independent cohorts, were submitted to
Cancer Discovery
(second author contribution) and provide strong evidence that spatially resolved antigen presentation programs influence prognosis and therapeutic response.
Objective 2.
Elucidate molecular mechanisms of TME-dependent MHC dysregulation and tumor progression.
To investigate how chemotherapy and TME remodeling influence MHC regulation, we integrated spatial proteomics (t-CycIF) and spatial transcriptomics (NanoString GeoMx) in 41 tumor tissues (including rare paired pre- and post-chemotherapy samples). Across 1,180 transcriptomic segments, we are identifying transcriptional programs associated with spatial immunophenotypes and resistance mechanisms.
Impact:
This integrative approach enables the identification of molecular pathways underlying resistant spatial niches. By linking spatial immune architecture with gene-expression programs, we aim to reveal targetable vulnerabilities in chemotherapy-resistant HGSC.

egrative approach enables the identification of molecular pathways underlying resistant spatial niches. By linking spatial immune architecture with gene-expression programs, we aim to reveal targetable vulnerabilities in chemotherapy-resistant HGSC.
Collectively, these objectives provide mechanistic insight into immune evasion in ovarian cancer and establish spatial MHC dysregulation as a biomarker-driven framework for personalized therapy and drug development.

## Slide 5
Objective 2: Spatial MHC Dysregulation & Resistance Mechanisms
Data integration is performed across 41 tumors and 1,180 segments using tCycIF and GeoMx, with explicit ROI identification.
SPACEStat analyzes cell networks, including direct niche diagrams, molecular programs via heatmap, and validation.
Direct niche diagrams contrast an active immune niche (MHCII+ tumor cells and activated T cells) with a resistant niche (MHCII- tumor cells and excluded T cells).
Molecular programs, presented in a mini-heatmap, show differential gene/pathway expression between these niche types.
Direct visual validation includes a bar chart demonstrating a significant difference in patient outcome scores between active and resistant signatures, as well as a validation shield with a checkmark.
The key result concludes that the research identified transcriptional programs driving resistant niches.

22_NKI_project
Analysis of a large collection of 265 tumors, using tCycif and 30 panels, and using 10 tissue microarrays, using four cores per tumor. Further information is in the publication: 
https://doi.org/10.1158/2159-8290.CD-25-1492
Responsible personnel:
Fernando Perez (2020-)
Aleksandra Shavanoba (2023-)
